topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Basics of Machine Learning — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — GNEST305, Module 1):**
> The student is expected to understand the *foundational taxonomy* of Machine Learning, distinguish between the major learning paradigms, identify the components of a typical ML pipeline, and recognize the standard terminology (features, labels, model, training, inference).

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computer systems to **learn patterns from data** and **improve their performance on a specific task** over time **without being explicitly programmed** for every possible scenario.

In the formal KTU/AI literature, this is often attributed to **Arthur Samuel (1959)**, who defined ML as:

> *"The field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

A more modern, mathematically rigorous definition by **Tom Mitchell (1997)** is:

> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience **E** with respect to some class of tasks **T** and performance measure **P**, if its performance at tasks in **T**, as measured by **P**, improves with experience **E**."*

The triplet $(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{P}, \mathbf{E})$ is the **learning problem definition** and is fundamental to framing any ML problem in the KTU examination context.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Learning Like a Toddler

Imagine you are teaching a small child to recognize a **cat** versus a **dog**:

- You don't write down *rules* like "if ear_shape = pointy AND whiskers = long AND sound = meow, then class = cat."
- Instead, you **show the child many examples** ("This is a cat. This is a dog. This is a cat...").
- The child gradually builds an **internal mental model** that maps visual features to the correct animal.

Machine Learning works **exactly the same way**:

- The *examples* are the **dataset**.
- The *features* (whiskers, ear shape, sound) are the **input variables** $X$.
- The *category* (cat/dog) is the **label** $y$.
- The *mental model* is the **learned function** $f_\theta(X) \approx y$.
- The act of refining the model by seeing more examples is **training**.

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks of an ML System

The standard KTU-mandated vocabulary for an ML problem includes the following:

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{X}$ | Feature matrix (input) | The "clues" the model sees |
| $\mathbf{y}$ | Label / target vector (output) | The "answer" the model should predict |
| $f_\theta(\cdot)$ | Hypothesis / model with parameters $\theta$ | The "decision rule" being learned |
| $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ | Dataset of $N$ samples | The "textbook" the model studies |
| $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ | Loss function | The "penalty" for being wrong |
| Training | Minimizing $\mathcal{L}$ on $\mathcal{D}$ | The "studying" phase |
| Inference / Prediction | Using $f_\theta$ on new, unseen data | The "exam" phase |
| Generalization | Performing well on *unseen* data | The "real-world test" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The single most important goal in ML is **generalization** — not memorization. A model that memorizes the training data is *overfit* and useless in practice.

## 1.4 Why Machine Learning Matters in Modern Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> ML is no longer optional — it sits at the core of:
> - **Recommendation systems** (Netflix, Amazon, Spotify)
> - **Computer vision** (medical imaging, autonomous vehicles)
> - **Natural Language Processing** (chatbots, translation, search)
> - **Predictive analytics** (weather, finance, energy grids)
> - **Anomaly detection** (cybersecurity, industrial fault diagnosis)

For KTU 2024 graduates, ML literacy is a baseline expectation across nearly all engineering disciplines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Basics of Machine Learning — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Learning Problem (Mitchell's Formalism)

Given:

- **Task** $\mathbf{T}$: e.g., classify an email as spam / not-spam
- **Experience** $\mathbf{E}$: a dataset of past labeled emails
- **Performance** $\mathbf{P}$: e.g., accuracy, F1-score

The ML algorithm searches a **hypothesis space** $\mathcal{H}$ for a function $h^* \in \mathcal{H}$ that best maps inputs to outputs:

$$h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \big[\, \mathcal{L}\big(h(x),\, y\big) \big]$$

The expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ is taken over the **true (unknown) data distribution** $\mathcal{D}$ — this is the **generalization risk**.

In practice, we do not know $\mathcal{D}$, so we approximate it using the **empirical risk** on the training set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$:

$$\hat{h} = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(h(x_i),\, y_i\big)$$

This is the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle — the theoretical backbone of supervised ML.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Machine Learning (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 demands a clear understanding of the four primary learning paradigms.** Examiners frequently ask 3-mark and 14-mark questions on this taxonomy.

### 2.2.1 Supervised Learning

- The dataset contains **input-output pairs** $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$.
- The model learns a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
- Two sub-tasks:
  - **Classification:** $y$ is discrete (e.g., spam / not-spam).
  - **Regression:** $y$ is continuous (e.g., house price).
- Examples: Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, SVM, k-NN, Neural Networks.

### 2.2.2 Unsupervised Learning

- The dataset contains **only inputs** $\mathbf{x}_i$ — **no labels**.
- The model discovers **structure, clusters, or patterns** on its own.
- Common tasks:
  - **Clustering:** group similar points (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical).
  - **Dimensionality Reduction:** compress features (e.g., PCA, t-SNE, UMAP).
  - **Association Rule Mining:** discover co-occurrence (e.g., Apriori, FP-Growth).

### 2.2.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL)

- An **agent** interacts with an **environment**.
- At each step, the agent takes an **action** $a_t$, receives a **reward** $r_t$, and transitions to a new **state** $s_{t+1}$.
- The goal: learn a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes cumulative future reward:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor** that prioritizes near-term rewards.

- Examples: AlphaGo, robotic locomotion, game-playing agents.

### 2.2.4 Semi-Supervised Learning

- A **small labeled** dataset + a **large unlabeled** dataset.
- Combines the strengths of both worlds; common when labeling is expensive (e.g., medical imaging).

## 2.3 The Standard ML Pipeline (KTU High-Yield)

A typical end-to-end ML project follows this sequence:

1. **Problem Definition** — frame the business/research question as an ML task.
2. **Data Collection** — gather raw data from databases, sensors, APIs, web scraping.
3. **Data Preprocessing** — handle missing values, outliers, duplicates.
4. **Feature Engineering** — create, transform, select features.
5. **Data Splitting** — divide into **Training**, **Validation**, and **Test** sets (typical ratio: 60/20/20 or 70/15/15).
6. **Model Selection** — choose an appropriate algorithm.
7. **Training** — fit the model by minimizing the loss function.
8. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — adjust learning rate, regularization, depth, etc.
9. **Evaluation** — measure performance on the held-out test set.
10. **Deployment & Monitoring** — serve predictions and track drift.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **These are the formulas most frequently tested in KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations for GNEST305 Module 1.** Memorize the table — it is your safety net for the 14-mark question.

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(h(x_i), y_i)$ | Unitless; averaged loss over $N$ samples |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Unit = (target unit)$^2$ |
| Root Mean Squared Error | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same unit as target |
| Mean Absolute Error | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Use $\vert$ carefully in tables (prefer $\vert \cdot \vert$ outside) |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| Precision | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | "Of those predicted positive, how many correct?" |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | "Of actual positives, how many caught?" |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean; range $[0, 1]$ |
| RL Cumulative Reward | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}$ | Discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ |
| Train/Test Split Ratio | $\text{Typical} = 70\% / 30\%$ or $80\% / 20\%$ | Validation carved out from training |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — LaTeX in Tables:** When writing $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ inside a markdown table row, the vertical bar `\|` may be misinterpreted as a column separator. Use the LaTeX command `\vert` or wrap the equation inside a `$\dots$` math block placed *between* table rows (not inside), as demonstrated in the cheat sheet above.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | ML Application | Learning Type |
|---|---|---|
| Civil (Structural Health) | Crack detection from vibration sensors | Supervised (Classification) |
| Mechanical (Predictive Maintenance) | Remaining-useful-life of bearings | Supervised (Regression) |
| Electrical (Smart Grid) | Load forecasting | Supervised (Regression) |
| CS / IT (Cybersecurity) | Intrusion detection in network traffic | Semi-Supervised / Supervised |
| Biomedical (Diagnostics) | Tumor classification from MRI | Supervised (Deep Learning) |
| Retail (Recommendation) | "Customers who bought X also bought Y" | Unsupervised (Association) |
| Robotics (Locomotion) | Walking policy for quadruped | Reinforcement Learning |

## 2.6 The 'Why' Behind the 'How'

- **Why supervised learning needs labels:** The model requires ground-truth "answers" to compute the loss $\mathcal{L}$ and update parameters $\theta$ via gradient descent.
- **Why unsupervised learning exists:** In many real domains (genomics, anomaly detection, customer segmentation), labels are unavailable or too costly.
- **Why RL uses rewards and not labels:** Actions have *delayed consequences*; only a scalar reward signal is available, not a per-step target.
- **Why we split data:** To honestly measure generalization; training on the test set is academic fraud and a guaranteed KTU valuation penalty.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Basics of Machine Learning — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Empirical Risk to Gradient Descent

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is the **analytical heart** of nearly every ML model. Examiners often test it as a 7-mark sub-question in Part B of the ESE.

We want to find parameters $\theta$ that minimize the **empirical risk**:

$$\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i\big)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the inner objective.**
Substitute the model $f_\theta(x)$ (for example, a linear model $f_\theta(x) = \theta^\top x$):

$$\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(y_i - \theta^\top x_i\big)^2$$

This is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** loss.

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient with respect to $\theta$.**
Apply the chain rule. The derivative of $(y_i - \theta^\top x_i)^2$ with respect to $\theta$ is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \big(y_i - \theta^\top x_i\big)^2 = 2 \big(y_i - \theta^\top x_i\big) \cdot \big(-x_i\big)$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all $N$ samples and divide by $N$:**

$$\nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta) = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(y_i - \theta^\top x_i\big) \, x_i$$

**Step 4 — Update rule (Gradient Descent).**
Move $\theta$ in the direction opposite to the gradient, scaled by the **learning rate** $\eta > 0$:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \, \nabla_\theta \hat{R}\big(\theta^{(t)}\big)$$

Substituting the gradient computed in Step 3:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} + \frac{2\eta}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(y_i - {\theta^{(t)}}^\top x_i\big) \, x_i$$

**Step 5 — Convergence criterion.**
Stop iterating when one of the following holds:

- $\vert \vert \nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta^{(t)}) \vert \vert_2 < \epsilon$ for a small tolerance $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$).
- A maximum number of iterations $T_{\max}$ is reached.
- The validation loss fails to improve for $k$ consecutive epochs (**early stopping**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Normal Equation (closed-form solution).** For linear regression, we can solve $\nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta) = 0$ directly, yielding:
> $$\theta^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y$$
> This requires $X^\top X$ to be invertible, which fails when features are collinear — a classic KTU viva question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Confusion Matrix Metrics

For a binary classification problem with classes $\{+, -\}$, every prediction falls into one of four outcomes:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

**Step 1 — Accuracy.**
Accuracy is the fraction of correct predictions out of all predictions:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Step 2 — Precision.**
Among all samples the model *predicted* as positive, what fraction is *truly* positive?

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Step 3 — Recall (True Positive Rate).**
Among all samples that are *actually* positive, what fraction did the model *catch*?

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (harmonic mean of Precision and Recall).**
The F1-score penalizes extreme imbalances between precision and recall:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**
Suppose a spam classifier produces: $TP = 90$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 5$, $TN = 895$.

Compute each metric:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{90 + 895}{90 + 895 + 10 + 5} = \frac{985}{1000} = 0.985$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{90}{90 + 5} = \frac{90}{95} \approx 0.947$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.90 \times 0.947}{0.90 + 0.947} = \frac{1.7046}{1.847} \approx 0.923$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU answers:** A 98.5% accuracy looks great — but precision and recall reveal the model is missing 5 real spam emails. Always report **multiple metrics**, not just accuracy.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Learning Paradigm

The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to *justify* your choice of learning paradigm. Use this decision matrix:

| If your data has... | And your goal is... | Then use... | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Labels for every sample | Predict a discrete class | Supervised (Classification) | Email spam detection |
| Labels for every sample | Predict a continuous value | Supervised (Regression) | House price prediction |
| No labels | Discover groups | Unsupervised (Clustering) | Customer segmentation |
| No labels | Reduce feature count | Unsupervised (Dim. Reduction) | Visualizing high-D data |
| No labels | Find co-occurrence rules | Unsupervised (Association) | Market basket analysis |
| Few labels + many unlabeled | Leverage both | Semi-Supervised | Medical image classification |
| Sequential interaction with environment | Maximize long-term reward | Reinforcement Learning | Game AI, robotics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Basics of Machine Learning — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Taxonomy of Machine Learning (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Machine Learning]) --> B[Supervised Learning]
    A --> C[Unsupervised Learning]
    A --> D[Reinforcement Learning]
    A --> E[Semi-Supervised Learning]

    B --> B1[Classification]
    B --> B2[Regression]
    B1 --> B1A[Logistic Regression]
    B1 --> B1B[Decision Trees]
    B1 --> B1C[SVM]
    B1 --> B1D[k-Nearest Neighbors]
    B2 --> B2A[Linear Regression]
    B2 --> B2B[Random Forest Regression]
    B2 --> B2C[Neural Networks]

    C --> C1[Clustering]
    C --> C2[Dimensionality Reduction]
    C --> C3[Association Rules]
    C1 --> C1A[K-Means]
    C1 --> C1B[DBSCAN]
    C1 --> C1C[Hierarchical]
    C2 --> C2A[PCA]
    C2 --> C2B[t-SNE]
    C2 --> C2C[UMAP]
    C3 --> C3A[Apriori]
    C3 --> C3B[FP-Growth]

    D --> D1[Model-Based RL]
    D --> D2[Model-Free RL]
    D1 --> D1A[Policy Iteration]
    D1 --> D1B[Value Iteration]
    D2 --> D2A[Q-Learning]
    D2 --> D2B[SARSA]

    E --> E1[Self-Training]
    E --> E2[Co-Training]
    E --> E3[Graph-Based Methods]
```

## 4.2 Standard ML Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA([Raw Data]) --> stepB[Data Collection]
    stepB --> stepC[Data Preprocessing]
    stepC --> stepD[Feature Engineering]
    stepD --> stepE[Train/Val/Test Split]
    stepE --> stepF[Model Training]
    stepF --> stepG{Hyperparameter Tuning}
    stepG --> stepH[Model Evaluation on Test Set]
    stepH --> stepI{Performance Acceptable?}
    stepI -- No --> stepG
    stepI -- Yes --> stepJ[Deployment and Monitoring]
    stepJ --> stepK([Predictions on New Data])
    stepK --> stepL[Feedback Loop and Retraining]
    stepL --> stepB
```

## 4.3 Supervised Learning Workflow (Input → Model → Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1([x1]) --> mat[Feature Matrix X]
    in2([x2]) --> mat
    in3([xn]) --> mat
    mat --> mod[f_theta: Learned Model]
    mod --> out1([y_hat = f_theta of x1])
    mod --> out2([y_hat = f_theta of x2])
    mod --> out3([y_hat = f_theta of xn])
    lbl([Label y]) --> loss[Loss Function L]
    out1 --> loss
    loss --> opt[Optimizer: Update theta]
    opt --> mod
```

## 4.4 Reinforcement Learning Agent–Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env([Environment at State s_t]) --> obs[Observation]
    obs --> agent([Agent])
    agent --> act[Action a_t]
    act --> env
    env --> rew[Reward r_t+1]
    env --> nxt[Next State s_t+1]
    nxt --> obs
    rew --> agent
```

## 4.5 Bias–Variance Tradeoff (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Error] --> B[Bias^2]
    A --> C[Variance]
    A --> D[Irreducible Noise]

    B --> B1[High = Underfitting]
    B --> B2[Model too simple]

    C --> C1[High = Overfitting]
    C --> C2[Model too complex]

    D --> D1[Cannot be reduced by any model]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Basics of Machine Learning — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme (ESE Pattern for GNEST305):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (answer in 3–4 sentences, with definitions).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark descriptive questions with internal choice (each part 7 marks × 2 sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
**Define Machine Learning. State any two applications of ML in real-world engineering domains.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Machine Learning is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to learn patterns from data and improve their performance on a given task without being explicitly programmed (Mitchell, 1997). Two real-world applications are:
1. **Predictive maintenance** in mechanical engineering — using sensor data and supervised regression to predict the remaining useful life of bearings and motors.
2. **Medical image classification** in biomedical engineering — using deep convolutional networks to detect tumors in MRI scans.

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for each application = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Paradigm | Data Requirement | Goal | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supervised | Labeled $(\mathbf{x}, y)$ pairs | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Email spam classification |
| Unsupervised | Unlabeled $\mathbf{x}$ only | Discover structure / clusters | Customer segmentation using K-Means |
| Reinforcement | Reward signal $r_t$ from environment | Maximize cumulative reward $G_t$ | AlphaGo learning to play Go |

*(1 mark per row = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four major types of Machine Learning paradigms in detail. For each, state the data requirement, the objective, and provide one engineering example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a binary classifier evaluated on a test set with the following counts: $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 870$. Compute the Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on whether accuracy alone is a reliable metric here.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]

**1. Supervised Learning** *(2 marks)*
- Data requirement: a dataset of labeled examples $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- Objective: learn a mapping $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \approx y$ by minimizing the empirical risk.
- Sub-tasks: classification (discrete $y$) and regression (continuous $y$).
- Engineering example: predicting the compressive strength of concrete from its mix proportions (regression).

**2. Unsupervised Learning** *(2 marks)*
- Data requirement: only inputs $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$; no labels.
- Objective: discover hidden structure — clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or association rules.
- Engineering example: grouping customers of a power utility into load-profile clusters using K-Means.

**3. Reinforcement Learning** *(2 marks)*
- Data requirement: interaction tuples $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ collected by an agent acting in an environment.
- Objective: learn a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes the expected discounted return $\mathbb{E}[G_t]$.
- Engineering example: training a bipedal robot to walk on uneven terrain.

**4. Semi-Supervised Learning** *(1 mark)*
- Data requirement: a small labeled set + a large unlabeled set.
- Objective: leverage unlabeled structure to improve a model that would otherwise be data-starved.
- Engineering example: classifying satellite imagery where manual labeling is expensive.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 870$. Total $N = 1000$.

**Step 1 — Accuracy** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 870}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95$$

**Step 2 — Precision** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 3 — Recall** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.727$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score** *(1.5 marks)*

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times \text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.727}{0.80 + 0.727} = \frac{1.1632}{1.527} \approx 0.762$$

**Step 5 — Comment** *(1 mark)*
Although accuracy is 95% (which looks impressive), the **class imbalance** is striking: positives are only $110/1000 = 11\%$ of the data. A trivial model that *always predicts negative* would also achieve ~89% accuracy, yet be useless. The low recall (72.7%) means the model is missing roughly 1 in 4 actual positive cases — a serious concern in high-stakes domains like medical diagnosis or fault detection. Hence, **accuracy alone is not a reliable metric** for imbalanced datasets; F1-score, precision, and recall must be reported jointly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the standard end-to-end Machine Learning pipeline. Explain the importance of the train/validation/test split.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the gradient descent update rule for a linear regression model with Mean Squared Error loss. State two conditions under which the algorithm is said to have converged.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**Pipeline (Block Diagram)** *(3 marks)*

The standard pipeline proceeds through the following stages (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Problem Definition** — frame the task (classification / regression / clustering) and choose a performance metric $P$.
2. **Data Collection** — gather raw samples from sensors, databases, or web sources.
3. **Data Preprocessing** — handle missing values (imputation / deletion), remove duplicates, and detect outliers.
4. **Feature Engineering** — encode categorical variables (one-hot, ordinal), scale numerical features (standardization / min-max), and perform feature selection.
5. **Data Splitting** — partition the data into **Training Set** (e.g., 70%), **Validation Set** (e.g., 15%), and **Test Set** (e.g., 15%).
6. **Model Training** — fit parameters $\theta$ by minimizing the loss on the training set.
7. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — use the validation set to choose learning rate $\eta$, regularization strength, tree depth, etc.
8. **Evaluation** — measure final performance on the **untouched** test set.
9. **Deployment & Monitoring** — serve the model and watch for data drift.

**Importance of Train/Validation/Test Split** *(4 marks)*

- The **training set** is used to learn the model parameters $\theta$.
- The **validation set** is used to tune hyperparameters and perform model selection — it provides an *unbiased* estimate of performance during development.
- The **test set** is used **only once**, at the very end, to estimate the model's **generalization error** on truly unseen data.
- **Why three sets, not two?** If we use the test set for hyperparameter tuning, the model indirectly *sees* the test data and we lose an honest measure of generalization. The validation set acts as a "sacrificial" proxy for the test set during development.
- A **typical split ratio** is $60\% / 20\% / 20\%$ or $70\% / 15\% / 15\%$. For very large datasets (millions of samples), the test split can be as small as 5%.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define the model and loss** *(1.5 marks)*

For a single training example $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$, the linear regression model is:

$$\hat{y}_i = f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i) = \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i$$

The Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss over $N$ samples is:

$$\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(y_i - \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i\big)^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient** *(2 marks)*

Differentiating $\hat{R}(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i)^2 \right]$$

Apply the chain rule term-by-term:

$$\nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \cdot (y_i - \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(y_i - \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i)$$

Since $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(y_i - \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i) = -\mathbf{x}_i$:

$$\nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta) = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i) \, \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 3 — Gradient descent update rule** *(2 marks)*

The parameters are updated by moving in the direction opposite to the gradient, scaled by the learning rate $\eta$:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \, \nabla_\theta \hat{R}\big(\theta^{(t)}\big)$$

Substituting the gradient:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} + \frac{2\eta}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(y_i - {\theta^{(t)}}^\top \mathbf{x}_i\big) \, \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 4 — Convergence conditions** *(1.5 marks)*

The algorithm is said to have converged when **either** of the following holds:

1. **Gradient norm is small:** $\vert \vert \nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta^{(t)}) \vert \vert_2 < \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (e.g., $10^{-6}$). This means the loss surface is essentially flat — further updates change $\theta$ negligibly.
2. **Loss plateau:** $\vert \hat{R}(\theta^{(t+1)}) - \hat{R}(\theta^{(t)}) \vert < \epsilon$, i.e., the loss has stopped improving meaningfully.
3. **Maximum iterations reached:** $t \geq T_{\max}$ (a safety bound to prevent infinite loops).
4. **Early stopping on validation loss:** training halts when the validation loss fails to improve for $k$ consecutive epochs (e.g., $k = 10$).

*(Any two of the above for full marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Before Writing Your Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing "features" and "labels":** In supervised learning, $\mathbf{x}$ = features (input), $y$ = label (output). Reversing them is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Reporting only accuracy:** For imbalanced datasets, accuracy is misleading. Always compute **Precision, Recall, and F1-score** when the class distribution is skewed.
> 3. **Forgetting the validation set:** Many students split into *only* train and test, then tune hyperparameters on the test set. This is **data leakage** and will cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up clustering algorithms:** K-Means requires specifying $k$ in advance; DBSCAN does not. Examiners love this trap.
> 5. **Using `\|` inside a markdown table:** When writing equations in tabular form, vertical bars are interpreted as column separators. Use `\vert` or place the equation outside the table.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in pipeline questions:** A 7-mark pipeline question **without** a block diagram typically caps at 5 marks. Always include one.
> 7. **Not stating the learning paradigm explicitly:** When asked to "explain an algorithm," always open with "This is a *supervised* learning algorithm because..." — examiners award marks for the framing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **ML Definition (Mitchell):** A program learns from experience $\mathbf{E}$ to improve performance $\mathbf{P}$ on task $\mathbf{T}$.
- **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM):** The cornerstone principle — minimize average loss on the training set.
- **Four Paradigms:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (unlabeled), Reinforcement (reward), Semi-Supervised (mixed).
- **Supervised sub-tasks:** Classification (discrete $y$) vs Regression (continuous $y$).
- **Unsupervised sub-tasks:** Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Association Rule Mining.
- **Key metrics for classification:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score (always report together for imbalanced data).
- **Key metrics for regression:** MSE, RMSE, MAE.
- **RL objective:** Maximize $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$.
- **Gradient Descent update:** $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta^{(t)})$.
- **Pipeline order:** Data → Preprocess → Features → Split → Train → Validate → Test → Deploy.
- **Split ratio:** 70/15/15 (train/val/test) is the KTU-recommended default.
- **Generalization > Memorization:** A model's true value is its performance on **unseen** data.
- **Overfitting:** Model too complex → high variance → fails on new data.
- **Underfitting:** Model too simple → high bias → fails on training data too.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Total Error = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Noise.
- **No Free Lunch Theorem:** No single algorithm is universally best — choice depends on data and task.
- **KTU Viva Favorites:** "Why is F1 preferred over accuracy in imbalanced problems?" / "What's the role of $\gamma$ in RL?" / "Why do we need a separate validation set?"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Machine Learning Systems - Challenges in ML - Supervised Learning Model Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Machine Learning Systems - Challenges in ML - Supervised Learning Model Example

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Machine Learning System?

A **Machine Learning (ML) System** is a computational framework that automatically learns patterns, relationships, and decision rules from data without being explicitly programmed for every scenario. According to the classical definition formalized in foundational AI literature, *a computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Machine Learning is a subset of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to learn and improve from experience (data) automatically, focusing on the development of computer programs that can access data and use it to learn for themselves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Learning Like a Toddler"

Imagine teaching a small child to recognize fruits:

- You show the child many apples and tell them "This is an apple."
- You show oranges, bananas, and grapes, each labeled.
- After seeing thousands of examples, the child can identify a new fruit it has never seen before.

This is exactly how a **Machine Learning System** works — it *learns from labeled examples (training data)*, builds an internal *model of the world*, and *generalizes* that knowledge to make predictions on new, unseen data. The child does not memorize each apple — it learns the *general features* (round, red/green, has a stem) that define an apple.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of ML Wisdom

Every ML system balances two opposing forces:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias-Variance Trade-off (Foundational Concept):**
> - **Bias** = Wrong assumptions made by the model → causes **Underfitting** (model too simple).
> - **Variance** = Excessive sensitivity to small fluctuations in training data → causes **Overfitting** (model too complex).
> - The goal of a good ML system is to find the **sweet spot** between bias and variance.

## 1.4 The Seven Major Types of Machine Learning Systems

ML systems are categorized based on **how they learn**, **what data they use**, and **how they generalize**.

| Category | Sub-Types | Key Distinguishing Feature |
|----------|-----------|----------------------------|
| By Supervision | Supervised, Unsupervised, Semisupervised, Self-supervised, Reinforcement | Whether labels are present in training |
| By Data Ingestion | Batch (Offline) vs Online (Incremental) Learning | Whether the system learns continuously or in chunks |
| By Generalization | Instance-based vs Model-based Learning | Whether the system memorizes or builds a general model |

### 1.4.1 Supervised Learning
The training data is **labeled** — every input has a corresponding correct output (the "supervisor" provides the answer key). The model learns the mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$.

> **Real-world examples:** Spam detection, image classification, house price prediction, medical diagnosis, credit scoring.

**Main Tasks:**
- **Classification** — Predict a discrete category (e.g., spam vs. not spam, cat vs. dog).
- **Regression** — Predict a continuous numerical value (e.g., temperature, price, height).

### 1.4.2 Unsupervised Learning
Training data is **unlabeled** — the system must find structure on its own.

> **Real-world examples:** Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, dimensionality reduction (PCA), recommendation systems, topic modeling.

**Main Tasks:**
- **Clustering** — Group similar data points (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical).
- **Dimensionality Reduction** — Compress feature space (e.g., PCA, t-SNE).
- **Association Rule Learning** — Find item relationships (e.g., Apriori).

### 1.4.3 Semisupervised Learning
A **hybrid** approach — training data has *mostly unlabeled* instances with a *small portion labeled*. Common when labeling is expensive (e.g., medical scans).

> **Classic example:** Google Photos clustering faces using unsupervised learning, then asking the user to label a few names — the labels propagate to the rest.

### 1.4.4 Self-Supervised Learning
A special form where the system **generates its own labels** from the input data, typically by hiding part of the input and asking the model to predict it.

> **Real-world examples:** BERT (predicts masked words), GPT (predicts next token), autoencoders, contrastive learning in computer vision.

### 1.4.5 Reinforcement Learning (RL)
The system (called an **agent**) learns by **interacting with an environment**, receiving **rewards** or **penalties** for actions, and optimizing a long-term cumulative reward strategy.

> **Real-world examples:** AlphaGo, autonomous driving, robotics, game playing, dynamic pricing.

### 1.4.6 Batch vs Online Learning
- **Batch Learning (Offline):** The system is trained on the *entire dataset at once* and then deployed. It cannot learn incrementally — to learn new data, it must be retrained from scratch.
- **Online Learning (Incremental):** The system learns *incrementally* as new data arrives, either one instance at a time or in mini-batches. Ideal for systems that receive continuous data streams (e.g., stock prices, IoT sensors).

### 1.4.7 Instance-based vs Model-based Learning
- **Instance-based Learning:** The system **memorizes** the training examples and generalizes to new instances using a *similarity measure* (e.g., K-Nearest Neighbors, case-based reasoning).
- **Model-based Learning:** The system builds a **parametric model** from the training data and uses that model to make predictions (e.g., Linear Regression, Decision Trees, Neural Networks).

## 1.5 What is a "Supervised Learning Model Example"?

In the context of the KTU syllabus (Module 1), a **Supervised Learning Model Example** typically refers to a worked demonstration of a labeled classification or regression task — for instance, classifying handwritten digits (like the **MNIST** dataset) or predicting housing prices using a labeled dataset. The example illustrates the **end-to-end ML pipeline**: data collection → preprocessing → model selection → training → evaluation → deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** A *Supervised Learning Model Example* in the syllabus almost always involves the workflow: (1) Collect labeled dataset, (2) Train a model (e.g., Decision Tree, k-NN, or Linear Regression), (3) Evaluate using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score for classification, or MSE/MAE/R² for regression.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Machine Learning System

Every ML system, regardless of its type, follows a structured operational pipeline. The following is the canonical **ML Pipeline** as defined in the KTU 2024 curriculum:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Data Collection** — Acquire raw data from sensors, databases, APIs, or web scraping. Quantity and quality of data fundamentally determine model performance.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — Clean the data by handling missing values, removing duplicates, normalizing/standardizing numerical features, and encoding categorical variables.
3. **Feature Engineering** — Select, extract, or transform the most informative attributes (features) that will serve as inputs to the model.
4. **Model Selection** — Choose an appropriate algorithm (e.g., Linear Regression for regression, Decision Tree for classification) based on the problem type, data size, and interpretability needs.
5. **Training (Learning Phase)** — Feed the training data into the model. The algorithm adjusts its internal parameters to minimize a **loss function** (also called cost or objective function).
6. **Evaluation** — Test the trained model on *unseen* data (the test set) using performance metrics.
7. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — Optimize the model's *external configuration* (e.g., learning rate, tree depth, number of clusters) that is not learned from data.
8. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Deploy the model to production and continuously monitor for performance degradation (model drift).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Sequence Matters:** Skipping or reordering these steps is a leading cause of poor ML models. According to industry surveys, ~80% of an ML practitioner's time is spent on steps 1–3 (data-centric work), not on algorithm design.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each ML Type

| ML Type | Why Use It? | How It Works (Mechanism) |
|---------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Supervised | You have labeled data and want accurate predictions | Learn mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ by minimizing prediction error |
| Unsupervised | You have no labels and want to discover hidden structure | Find patterns through clustering, association, or projection |
| Semisupervised | Labels are expensive; you have lots of unlabeled + few labeled data | Combine small labeled set with large unlabeled set using methods like label propagation |
| Self-Supervised | You want to leverage massive unlabeled data | Create pretext tasks (e.g., predict masked words, predict rotations) |
| Reinforcement | You need sequential decision-making under uncertainty | Agent learns policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ maximizing expected cumulative reward $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ |
| Online | Data arrives as a continuous stream | Update model parameters $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla L$ for each new sample |
| Instance-based | Data is small, non-parametric, and you need simple models | Use distance metric (e.g., Euclidean) to compare new instance with stored instances |
| Model-based | Data is large, parametric, and you need fast predictions | Fit a parametric function and use it for inference |

## 2.3 Deep Dive: Challenges in Machine Learning

This is a **high-frequency KTU exam topic**. The following challenges are exhaustively catalogued:

### Challenge 1: Insufficient Quantity of Training Data
- Most ML algorithms require **thousands to millions** of examples to learn effectively.
- Even simple models like text classifiers often need ~10,000+ examples per class.
- **Why it matters:** A 2017 landmark study (often cited in KTU materials) showed that *for the same algorithm, performance scales logarithmically with data size*.

### Challenge 2: Non-Representative Training Data
- If the training sample does not reflect the population the model will serve, predictions become **biased**.
- **Sampling bias** occurs when certain groups are over- or under-represented.
- **Example:** Training a facial recognition model only on light-skinned faces causes poor accuracy on darker-skinned faces.

### Challenge 3: Poor Quality Data
- Training data riddled with **errors, outliers, and noise** leads to poor models.
- **Solution:** Data cleaning, outlier removal (e.g., using IQR or Z-score), and robust preprocessing.

### Challenge 4: Irrelevant Features
- *Garbage in, garbage out.* If features don't carry predictive signal, the model cannot learn.
- **Solution:** Feature selection (filter, wrapper, embedded methods) and feature engineering.

### Challenge 5: Overfitting the Training Data
- The model learns the training data **too well**, including its noise, and fails to generalize.
- Symptoms: **High training accuracy, low test accuracy**.
- **Solutions:** Get more data, simplify the model (reduce parameters), use **regularization** (L1/L2), apply **dropout** (in neural networks), use **early stopping**, or apply **cross-validation**.

### Challenge 6: Underfitting the Training Data
- The model is **too simple** to capture the underlying pattern.
- Symptoms: **Poor performance on both training and test sets**.
- **Solutions:** Use a more powerful model, add better features, reduce regularization.

### Challenge 7: Data Mismatch
- Training data and production data come from different distributions.
- **Example:** A speech recognizer trained on studio recordings fails on noisy street audio.

### Challenge 8: Hyperparameter Tuning & Validation
- The wrong hyperparameters can destroy model performance.
- **Solution:** Use a **validation set** (or **k-fold cross-validation**) distinct from the test set.

### Challenge 9: Concept Drift
- The statistical properties of the target variable change over time.
- **Example:** User behavior during COVID-19 shifted drastically; models trained on pre-pandemic data failed.

## 2.4 The Supervised Learning Model Example — MNIST Walkthrough

The canonical supervised learning example in KTU materials is the **MNIST handwritten digit classification problem**:

**Problem:** Given a 28×28 grayscale image of a handwritten digit (0–9), predict the digit.

**Pipeline:**
1. **Dataset:** 70,000 labeled images (60,000 train + 10,000 test).
2. **Features:** 784 pixel intensity values (0–255) per image.
3. **Model Options:** Logistic Regression, SVM, k-NN, Decision Tree, Neural Network.
4. **Output:** Predicted class $y \in \{0, 1, 2, ..., 9\}$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **non-negotiable for KTU 2024 exams** on Module 1.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Linear Regression Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | Predicts a continuous output as a linear function of input |
| 2 | Multiple Linear Regression | $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x = \sum_{j=0}^{n} \theta_j x_j$ | Vectorized form with multiple features |
| 3 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) Cost | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$ | Measures average squared prediction error |
| 4 | Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 5 | Coefficient of Determination (R²) | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Proportion of variance explained |
| 6 | Sigmoid (Logistic) Function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps real values to $(0, 1)$ for binary classification |
| 7 | Logistic Regression Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ | Probability of class $= 1$ |
| 8 | Binary Cross-Entropy Loss | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$ | Used for logistic regression |
| 9 | Accuracy | $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of correct predictions |
| 10 | Precision | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted positives, how many are correct |
| 11 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many were found |
| 12 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| 13 | Entropy (Information) | $H(P) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measure of impurity used in Decision Trees |
| 14 | Euclidean Distance (k-NN) | $d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - x_j')^2}$ | Distance metric for instance-based learning |
| 15 | L2 Regularization Term | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2$ | Penalizes large weights to prevent overfitting |
| 16 | L1 Regularization Term | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert$ | Induces sparsity in weights |
| 17 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$ | Iterative parameter update rule |
| 18 | RL Cumulative Reward | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted future return |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *validation accuracy* with *test accuracy*. Validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning; test set is used **only once** for final, unbiased performance estimation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Supervised learning on X-ray images detects pneumonia and tumors.
- **Finance:** Regression models predict stock prices; classification models detect fraud.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Reinforcement learning trains driving policies.
- **Agriculture:** Unsupervised clustering segments farmland based on soil and yield data.
- **Cybersecurity:** Anomaly detection identifies novel attacks.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Self-supervised learning powers ChatGPT, BERT, and translation systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost Function of Linear Regression (Ordinary Least Squares)

We derive the Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function for simple linear regression from first principles.

### Setup
We have a training set of $m$ examples: $\{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots, (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$.

Our hypothesis is: $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$

**Step 1 — Define a single prediction error (residual).**
The error for the $i$-th example is the difference between the actual value and the predicted value:

$$
e^{(i)} = h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Square the error to remove sign and penalize large errors more heavily.**

$$
(e^{(i)})^2 = (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the squared errors over all training examples.**

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2
$$

**Step 4 — Take the mean to obtain the average squared error (MSE).**

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2
$$

**Step 5 — For mathematical convenience in gradient descent, divide by 2 (this does not change the optimum but simplifies the derivative).**

$$
J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2
$$

This is the **MSE Cost Function** used universally in linear regression.

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** We started with a per-example error, squared it to penalize large errors, summed to aggregate over the dataset, divided by $m$ to get an average, and divided by 2 for calculus convenience.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression

We want to minimize $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ by adjusting $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ in the direction of steepest descent.

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $\theta_j$.**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Update each parameter by stepping in the negative gradient direction, scaled by learning rate $\alpha$.**

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply simultaneously** for all $j \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$ until convergence.

> **Intuition:** The parameter is updated *proportional to the error* $(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$. If the model over-predicts (positive error), parameters are decreased. The learning rate $\alpha$ controls step size — too large causes divergence, too small causes slow convergence.

## 3.3 Derivation: Confusion Matrix Metrics

Given a binary classifier with predictions and ground truth, we define:

- **TP** (True Positives): Correctly predicted positives.
- **TN** (True Negatives): Correctly predicted negatives.
- **FP** (False Positives): Incorrectly predicted as positive (Type I error).
- **FN** (False Negatives): Incorrectly predicted as negative (Type II error).

### Accuracy

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Of all predictions, what fraction was correct?

### Precision

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Of all predicted positives, how many truly were positive?

### Recall (True Positive Rate)

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?

### F1-Score

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Harmonic mean of precision and recall. The harmonic mean (not arithmetic) is used because it heavily penalizes extreme imbalances — a model with precision = 1.0 but recall = 0.0 gets $F_1 = 0$, which correctly reflects its uselessness.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which ML Type

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended ML Type | Reasoning |
|------------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| You have labeled data and want to predict an outcome | Supervised | Direct mapping from $X \rightarrow Y$ is learnable |
| You have no labels and want to discover groups | Unsupervised (Clustering) | Algorithms find structure without supervision |
| You have a few labels but lots of unlabeled data | Semisupervised | Leverage cheap unlabeled data alongside expensive labels |
| You have massive unlabeled data (text, images) | Self-supervised | Pretext tasks generate labels for free |
| You have an agent interacting with an environment | Reinforcement | Learn from trial-and-error via reward signals |
| Data arrives as a stream (e.g., IoT, stocks) | Online Learning | Update incrementally without full retraining |
| Data is small and you need an interpretable model | Instance-based (k-NN) | Memorize training data; non-parametric |
| Data is large and you need fast inference | Model-based (e.g., Neural Net) | Trained model makes O(1) predictions |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Binary Classification Metrics

**Scenario:** A spam classifier is tested on 1,000 emails.
- **TP** = 350 (correctly flagged as spam)
- **TN** = 600 (correctly passed as not spam)
- **FP** = 25 (legitimate email flagged as spam)
- **FN** = 25 (spam email passed as legitimate)

**Step 1 — Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{350 + 600}{350 + 600 + 25 + 25} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95 \;(95\%)
$$

**Step 2 — Precision.**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{350}{350 + 25} = \frac{350}{375} \approx 0.9333 \;(93.33\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Recall.**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{350}{350 + 25} = \frac{350}{375} \approx 0.9333 \;(93.33\%)
$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score.**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9333 \times 0.9333}{0.9333 + 0.9333} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8710}{1.8666} \approx 0.9333
$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** When precision and recall are equal, $F_1$ equals them too. In imbalanced cases, $F_1$ is much lower than the arithmetic mean.

## 3.6 The "Supervised Learning Model Example" — End-to-End Pipeline

For a typical KTU-style supervised learning example (e.g., predicting house prices):

| Step | Action | Example Output |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Collect dataset | Kaggle "House Prices" dataset, 1460 rows, 80 features |
| 2 | Explore and clean data | Handle missing values, remove outliers |
| 3 | Feature engineering | One-hot encode location, scale area |
| 4 | Split data | 80% train (1168) / 20% test (292) |
| 5 | Choose model | Linear Regression (baseline), Random Forest (advanced) |
| 6 | Train | Fit $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ on training set |
| 7 | Evaluate on test set | Compute MAE, RMSE, R² |
| 8 | Tune | Adjust hyperparameters using cross-validation |
| 9 | Deploy | Save model (`.pkl` or `.h5`) and serve via API |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Labels** | Training data has labeled outputs | Training data has no labels |
| **Goal** | Learn a mapping $X \rightarrow Y$ | Discover hidden structure in $X$ |
| **Tasks** | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction |
| **Algorithms** | Linear Regression, SVM, Decision Tree, k-NN | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA, Apriori |
| **Examples** | Spam detection, house price prediction | Customer segmentation, anomaly detection |

*(Full 3-mark answer requires the table + 1–2 line example each. Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four major challenges in Machine Learning and briefly explain any two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Four major challenges in ML:
1. **Insufficient training data** — Many algorithms need thousands to millions of examples.
2. **Non-representative training data** — Sampling bias leads to skewed predictions.
3. **Poor data quality** — Noise, errors, and outliers degrade performance.
4. **Overfitting** — Model memorizes training data, fails to generalize.

*Explanation of any two (1.5 marks each):*

**Overfitting:** When a model learns the training data too well, including its noise and outliers, it performs excellently on the training set but poorly on unseen test data. Solutions include regularization (L1/L2), dropout, early stopping, and obtaining more training data.

**Insufficient training data:** Most ML algorithms require large volumes of data to learn generalizable patterns. A model trained on very few samples will have high variance and cannot capture the true distribution of the data. Techniques like data augmentation, transfer learning, and synthetic data generation can help.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the different **types of Machine Learning systems** based on supervision, data ingestion, and generalization. Give one example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major **challenges in Machine Learning** with appropriate solutions for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

Machine Learning systems can be classified along three independent axes:

**1. Based on Supervision (4 marks):**
- **Supervised Learning:** Training data contains labeled examples $(x_i, y_i)$. The algorithm learns the input-output mapping. *Example:* Email spam classification where emails are labeled as "spam" or "not spam."
- **Unsupervised Learning:** Training data has no labels. The algorithm finds structure. *Example:* Customer segmentation using K-Means clustering.
- **Semisupervised Learning:** Combines a small labeled set with a large unlabeled set. *Example:* Face recognition in photo apps.
- **Self-supervised Learning:** The system generates its own labels from input data. *Example:* BERT predicting masked words in a sentence.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** An agent learns by interacting with an environment via rewards/penalties. *Example:* AlphaGo learning to play the game of Go.

**2. Based on Data Ingestion (2 marks):**
- **Batch (Offline) Learning:** Trains on the full dataset at once; cannot adapt to new data without retraining.
- **Online (Incremental) Learning:** Learns from streaming data one sample/mini-batch at a time; suitable for real-time applications.

**3. Based on Generalization (1 mark):**
- **Instance-based Learning:** Memorizes training data and uses similarity (e.g., k-NN).
- **Model-based Learning:** Builds a parametric model (e.g., Linear Regression, Neural Networks).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| # | Challenge | Explanation | Solution |
|---|-----------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Insufficient training data | Algorithms need thousands of examples | Data augmentation, transfer learning, synthetic data |
| 2 | Non-representative data | Sampling bias skews the model | Stratified sampling, resampling, gather diverse data |
| 3 | Poor data quality | Noise and errors corrupt learning | Data cleaning, outlier detection, imputation |
| 4 | Irrelevant features | Model cannot learn from non-informative inputs | Feature selection, feature engineering, PCA |
| 5 | Overfitting | Model memorizes training data | Regularization (L1/L2), dropout, early stopping, more data |
| 6 | Underfitting | Model is too simple | Use a more complex model, add features, reduce regularization |
| 7 | Data mismatch | Train and production distributions differ | Domain adaptation, careful data collection |
| 8 | Hyperparameter tuning | Wrong settings hurt performance | Grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization |
| 9 | Concept drift | Data distribution changes over time | Continuous monitoring, periodic retraining |

*(Marking: 0.5 mark per challenge explanation + 0.25 mark for solution ≈ 6.75 marks; round to 7.)*

---

### Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe a **supervised learning model example** (e.g., MNIST digit classification) covering the full pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **overfitting and underfitting** in machine learning. How do you detect and mitigate them? Include the bias-variance trade-off in your answer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**MNIST Handwritten Digit Classification — Supervised Learning Example**

**Problem Statement:** Given a 28×28 grayscale image of a handwritten digit, classify it as 0–9.

**Pipeline Steps:**

1. **Data Collection (1 mark):** The MNIST dataset contains 70,000 labeled images — 60,000 for training and 10,000 for testing. Each image is labeled with the correct digit.

2. **Data Preprocessing (1 mark):** Convert each 28×28 image into a flat vector of 784 features. Normalize pixel values from [0, 255] to [0, 1] for stable training. Optionally apply data augmentation (rotation, shift) to increase dataset size.

3. **Feature Representation (1 mark):** Each image is now a feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{784}$, with the label $y \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 9\}$.

4. **Model Selection (1 mark):** Choose a classifier — Logistic Regression (simple baseline), Support Vector Machine (high accuracy), k-Nearest Neighbors (instance-based), or a small Neural Network (deep learning approach).

5. **Training (1 mark):** Feed training set $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{60000}$ into the chosen model. The model learns parameters $\theta$ by minimizing a loss function (e.g., cross-entropy).

6. **Evaluation (1 mark):** Test the model on the 10,000 held-out test images. Report metrics: accuracy, confusion matrix, per-class precision/recall.

7. **Tuning & Deployment (1 mark):** Tune hyperparameters (e.g., regularization strength, learning rate, number of neighbors) using cross-validation. Deploy final model for inference on new handwritten digits.

**Expected Results:** Simple models achieve ~92% accuracy; advanced models (CNNs) achieve >99.5%.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Overfitting (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A model learns the training data *too well*, including its noise and random fluctuations, resulting in poor generalization to unseen data.
- **Symptoms:** High training accuracy, low test accuracy; large gap between training and validation error.
- **Detection:** Plot learning curves (training vs. validation error). If validation error rises while training error keeps falling, the model is overfitting.
- **Mitigation:** (i) Get more training data, (ii) Simplify the model (reduce parameters, depth), (iii) Apply **L1/L2 regularization** which penalizes large weights, (iv) Use **dropout** in neural networks, (v) Apply **early stopping** based on validation loss, (vi) Use **cross-validation** to tune hyperparameters.

**Underfitting (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A model is *too simple* to capture the underlying structure of the data, performing poorly on both training and test sets.
- **Symptoms:** Low accuracy on both training and validation sets.
- **Detection:** Both training and validation errors are high and converge to a similar (poor) value.
- **Mitigation:** (i) Use a more complex model (more parameters, deeper network), (ii) Add more informative features, (iii) Reduce regularization strength, (iv) Train for more iterations.

**Bias-Variance Trade-off (1 mark):**
The **total expected error** of a model decomposes into three components:

$$
\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}
$$

- **High bias** → underfitting (model is too rigid).
- **High variance** → overfitting (model is too flexible).
- The goal is to find the **sweet spot** that minimizes total error, achieved through proper model complexity, regularization, and cross-validation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "validation set" with "test set":** The validation set is used for *hyperparameter tuning*; the test set is used *only once* for final reporting. Mixing them invalidates evaluation.
> 2. **Forgetting to scale features:** Algorithms like k-NN, SVM, and gradient descent are sensitive to feature scales. Always apply StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler before training.
> 3. **Reporting training accuracy as final accuracy:** This is *cheating* — the model has already seen this data. Always report test/validation accuracy.
> 4. **Confusing precision and recall:** Precision is about *predicted* positives; recall is about *actual* positives. A spam filter with high recall but low precision flags too many legitimate emails.
> 5. **Ignoring class imbalance:** Using accuracy on an imbalanced dataset (e.g., 99% negatives) is misleading. Use F1-score, ROC-AUC, or PR-AUC.
> 6. **Skipping cross-validation:** Single train-test splits can be lucky/unlucky. Use k-fold cross-validation for reliable estimates.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Module 1 Exam:**

- ✅ **Five types of ML by supervision:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Semisupervised, Self-supervised, Reinforcement.
- ✅ **Two types by data ingestion:** Batch (offline) vs Online (incremental).
- ✅ **Two types by generalization:** Instance-based (k-NN) vs Model-based (Linear Regression, Neural Nets).
- ✅ **Nine major challenges in ML:** Insufficient data, non-representative data, poor data quality, irrelevant features, overfitting, underfitting, data mismatch, hyperparameter tuning, concept drift.
- ✅ **Overfitting solutions:** More data, simpler model, L1/L2 regularization, dropout, early stopping, cross-validation.
- ✅ **Underfitting solutions:** More complex model, better features, less regularization, more training.
- ✅ **Confusion Matrix Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score — know the formulas and interpretation.
- ✅ **MSE Formula:** $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent Update:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$.
- ✅ **Sigmoid Function:** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance Decomposition:** $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Noise}$.
- ✅ **Supervised Learning Example Pipeline:** Data → Preprocess → Features → Split → Train → Evaluate → Tune → Deploy.
- ✅ **Reinforcement Learning Key Equation:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- ✅ **Euclidean Distance (k-NN):** $d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - x_j')^2}$.
- ✅ **Always normalize/standardize features** before training distance-based or gradient-based models.
- ✅ **Use cross-validation** for reliable model evaluation and hyperparameter selection.
