topics.title: Regression Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regression Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression** is a **supervised machine learning** technique used to model and analyze the relationship between a **dependent variable (target/output, $Y$)** and one or more **independent variables (predictors/features, $X$)**. The primary objective is to estimate a continuous, real-valued output by fitting a mathematical function to observed data.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework for **GNEST305**, regression is positioned as the foundational **predictive modeling** paradigm under the broader umbrella of **Statistical Learning Theory**. It is formally expressed as:

$$\hat{Y} = f(X) + \epsilon$$

where:
- $\hat{Y}$ is the **predicted (estimated) value** of the dependent variable.
- $f(X)$ is the **regression function** mapping inputs to outputs.
- $\epsilon$ is the **irreducible error term** (random noise) assumed to be independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.) with mean $\mu_{\epsilon} = 0$.

The learned function $f(X)$ is parameterized by a vector of weights $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \{\theta_0, \theta_1, \dots, \theta_n\}$ that are optimized to minimize a **loss function** (typically Mean Squared Error).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Real Estate Advisor" Intuition

Imagine you are a **real estate advisor** in Kerala trying to estimate the **fair price of a house**. You don't guess randomly — instead, you look at historical data:

| Feature ($X$) | Observation |
|---|---|
| Area (sq. ft.) | Bigger houses cost more |
| Bedrooms | More bedrooms → higher price |
| Locality | Urban > Rural (generally) |
| Distance from city | Farther → cheaper |

You mentally build a **rule of thumb**:
> *"Price ≈ 2500 × Area + 50000 × Bedrooms − 10000 × Distance + BasePrice"*

This **rule of thumb** is exactly what a regression model learns! The **coefficients** (2500, 50000, −10000) are the **model parameters** ($\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$) that the algorithm discovers by minimizing the difference between predicted and actual historical prices.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Regression is a *parametric* approach (assumes a functional form) and is the gateway to understanding **Neural Networks** (where $f(X)$ becomes a non-linear composition of layers).

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Regression Models

The following hierarchy is the standard classification expected in KTU examinations:

| Type | Functional Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Linear Regression** | $\hat{Y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 X$ | One predictor, linear trend |
| **Multiple Linear Regression** | $\hat{Y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 X_1 + \dots + \theta_n X_n$ | Multiple predictors, linear |
| **Polynomial Regression** | $\hat{Y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 X + \theta_2 X^2 + \dots$ | Non-linear curvature |
| **Ridge Regression (L2)** | Linear + $\lambda \sum \theta_i^2$ | Multicollinearity control |
| **Lasso Regression (L1)** | Linear + $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_i \vert$ | Feature selection |
| **Logistic Regression** | $\hat{Y} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\theta_0 + \theta_1 X)}}$ | Classification (despite name) |
| **Support Vector Regression** | $\epsilon$-insensitive loss | Robust to outliers |
| **Decision Tree Regression** | Piecewise constant splits | Non-parametric |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Learning Framework

The journey of regression under KTU's outcome-based education (OBE) begins with the **statistical model**:

$$Y = f(X) + \epsilon$$

The systematic decomposition is:
- **Systematic part** $f(X)$ — the predictable structure we wish to learn.
- **Stochastic part** $\epsilon$ — random noise, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ for the standard OLS assumption.

### Why we cannot solve $f(X)$ exactly

In reality, $f(X)$ is an **unknown deterministic function** mapping inputs to outputs. We only observe a finite dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$. The **goal of regression is to estimate** an approximation $\hat{f}(X)$ such that the expected prediction error on unseen data is minimized.

## 2.2 Simple Linear Regression — Detailed Mechanics

For a single feature $X$, the hypothesized model is:

$$\hat{y}^{(i)} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)}$$

- $\theta_0$ = **intercept** (the predicted $Y$ when $X = 0$).
- $\theta_1$ = **slope** (the average change in $Y$ for a unit change in $X$).

### 2.2.1 The Cost Function (Mean Squared Error)

The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** is the canonical loss for regression:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience — it cancels out the exponent **2** during differentiation, making gradient formulas cleaner. **It does not change the optimal parameters.**

### 2.2.2 Two Methods to Find Optimal Parameters

| Method | Type | Formula | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Equation** | Closed-form (analytical) | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ | Exact, but $O(n^3)$ — slow for large $n$ |
| **Gradient Descent** | Iterative (numerical) | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Scalable, requires learning rate tuning |

## 2.3 Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)

For $n$ features, the model generalizes to:

$$\hat{y}^{(i)} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \theta_2 x_2^{(i)} + \dots + \theta_n x_n^{(i)}$$

In **vectorized matrix form**:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ is the **design matrix** (with a leading column of **1s** for $\theta_0$).
- $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times 1}$ is the parameter vector.
- $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ is the prediction vector.

The **MSE** in matrix form:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

## 2.4 Gradient Descent — The Iterative Workhorse

The **update rule** for each parameter $\theta_j$ is:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \theta_j - \frac{\alpha}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (hyperparameter).

| Learning Rate | Effect |
|---|---|
| $\alpha$ **too small** | Slow convergence (takes many iterations) |
| $\alpha$ **too large** | Divergence / oscillation around minimum |
| $\alpha$ **just right** | Smooth, monotonic decrease in $J(\theta)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Students must remember that gradient descent **requires feature scaling** (standardization or min-max normalization) for fast convergence. Otherwise, elongated contour lines cause zig-zag paths.

## 2.5 The Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution)

Setting the gradient $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{0}$ yields:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{1}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}$$

This is the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse solution** when $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is non-invertible (singular).

## 2.6 Polynomial Regression

A non-linear relationship is captured by introducing polynomial terms:

$$\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x + \theta_2 x^2 + \theta_3 x^3$$

This is still a **linear model** in parameters — the linearity refers to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, not $X$. We can map it to MLR by setting $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = x^2$, $x_3 = x^3$.

## 2.7 Regularized Regression (Handling Overfitting)

### 2.7.1 Ridge Regression (L2 Penalty)

$$J_{\text{Ridge}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2 \right]$$

- $\lambda$ is the **regularization strength** (hyperparameter).
- Shrinks coefficients toward zero but **never exactly zero**.

### 2.7.2 Lasso Regression (L1 Penalty)

$$J_{\text{Lasso}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert \right]$$

- Produces **sparse models** (drives some $\theta_j$ to exactly **0**).
- Useful for **automatic feature selection**.

### 2.7.3 Elastic Net (L1 + L2 Combined)

$$J_{\text{EN}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 + \lambda \left( \alpha \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert + (1 - \alpha) \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2 \right) \right]$$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | Simple linear hypothesis | Model definition |
| 2 | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}$ | Vectorized hypothesis | MLR model |
| 3 | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$ | MSE cost | Optimization objective |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Normal equation | Closed-form solution |
| 5 | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | GD update | Iterative learning |
| 6 | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{\text{res}}}{SS_{\text{tot}}}$ | Coefficient of determination | Model evaluation |
| 7 | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{m-1}{m-n-1}$ | Adjusted R² | MLR with many features |
| 8 | $J_{\text{Ridge}} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum \theta_j^2$ | L2 penalty | Ridge |
| 9 | $J_{\text{Lasso}} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ | L1 penalty | Lasso |
| 10 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2}$ | Root MSE | Interpretable metric |
| 11 | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum \vert \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \vert$ | Mean Absolute Error | Robust to outliers |
| 12 | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{m} \sum \left\vert \frac{\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{y^{(i)}} \right\vert$ | Mean Abs. % Error | Scale-independent metric |

## 2.9 Model Evaluation Metrics — Deeper View

The **R² (coefficient of determination)** measures the proportion of variance explained:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{\text{residual}}}{SS_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2}$$

- $R^2 = 1$ → perfect fit.
- $R^2 = 0$ → model is as good as predicting the mean.
- $R^2 < 0$ → model is worse than the mean (catastrophic underfitting).

> [!NOTE]
> In MLR, $R^2$ **always increases** (or stays the same) as we add features — even useless ones! Hence we use the **Adjusted R²** which penalizes unnecessary features.

## 2.10 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff

| Concept | High Bias | High Variance |
|---|---|---|
| **Symptom** | Underfitting | Overfitting |
| **Training error** | High | Low |
| **Test error** | High | High |
| **Model complexity** | Too simple (e.g., linear for non-linear data) | Too complex (e.g., degree-10 polynomial for 5 points) |
| **Remedy** | Add features, use polynomial, reduce regularization | More data, feature selection, increase $\lambda$ |

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Predicting concrete strength from mix proportions |
| **Electrical** | Forecasting power consumption from temperature/humidity |
| **Mechanical** | Estimating tool wear from operating parameters |
| **Economics** | Demand forecasting for commodities |
| **Healthcare** | Predicting patient recovery time from vitals |
| **IoT/AIoT** | Smart sensor calibration & predictive maintenance |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Normal Equation for Simple Linear Regression

**Given** $m$ data points $\{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \dots, (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$, we want to find $\theta_0, \theta_1$ that minimize:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 1:** Take the partial derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\theta_0$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Take the partial derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\theta_1$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}$$

**Step 3:** Set both partial derivatives to **zero** (first-order optimality condition).

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Expand the first equation.

$$m \theta_0 + \theta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)} - \sum_{i=1}^{m} y^{(i)} = 0$$

$$\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$$

where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{m}\sum x^{(i)}$ and $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{m}\sum y^{(i)}$.

**Step 5:** Expand the second equation and substitute $\theta_0$ from Step 4.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} \theta_0 x^{(i)} + \theta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)})^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)} y^{(i)} = 0$$

Substituting $\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$:

$$(\bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}) \sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)} + \theta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)})^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)} y^{(i)} = 0$$

Using $\sum x^{(i)} = m \bar{x}$:

$$m \bar{x} \bar{y} - \theta_1 m (\bar{x})^2 + \theta_1 \sum (x^{(i)})^2 - \sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\theta_1$.

$$\theta_1 \left[ \sum (x^{(i)})^2 - m (\bar{x})^2 \right] = \sum x^{(i)} y^{(i)} - m \bar{x} \bar{y}$$

Recognize the bracketed term as $m \cdot \text{Var}(X)$ and the right-hand side as $m \cdot \text{Cov}(X, Y)$:

$$\boxed{\theta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\text{Var}(X)}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classic OLS estimator**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive this in Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Matrix-Form Normal Equation for MLR

**Given** the MSE in matrix form:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

**Step 1:** Expand the quadratic.

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2 \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} \right]$$

**Step 2:** Take the gradient w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

Using the matrix calculus identities:
- $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} (\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{A}^T$
- $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} (\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}) = 2 \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}$ (when $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric)

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \right]$$

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} \left[ \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Set gradient to zero.

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ by left-multiplying with $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$.

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times (n+1)}$ must be **invertible** (i.e., $\det(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}) \neq 0$). This fails when:
> - Features are **linearly dependent** (perfect multicollinearity).
> - $m < n$ (more features than samples).
>
> **Solution:** Use **pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{X}^{+} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T$ via SVD, or apply **regularization** to make $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}$ invertible.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression

**Goal:** Find the negative gradient direction for each $\theta_j$.

Starting from the MSE:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 1:** Compute the partial derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\theta_j$ (for $j \geq 1$).

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j}$$

**Step 2:** Since $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \dots + \theta_n x_n^{(i)}$, we have $\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the gradient descent update:

$$\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}}$$

For $\theta_0$ (intercept), $x_0^{(i)} = 1$ always, so the same formula applies.

### Vectorized Form of Gradient Descent

$$\boldsymbol{\theta} := \boldsymbol{\theta} - \frac{\alpha}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Ridge Regression Closed-Form

The Ridge cost function is:

$$J_{\text{Ridge}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y}) + \lambda \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \boldsymbol{\theta} \right]$$

**Step 1:** Take the gradient and set to zero.

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J_{\text{Ridge}} = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + 2\lambda \boldsymbol{\theta} \right] = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange.

$$(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}) \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 3:** Solve.

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\text{Ridge}}^{*} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\lambda \mathbf{I}$ term **guarantees invertibility** even when $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is singular. This is why Ridge regression is the standard fix for multicollinearity.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Given data points $(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 4)$, fit a simple linear regression $\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ using the Normal Equation.

**Step 1:** Compute the means.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4}{4} = 2.5, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2+3+5+4}{4} = 3.5$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator (covariance sum) and denominator (variance sum).

| $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ | $x^{(i)} - \bar{x}$ | $y^{(i)} - \bar{y}$ | Product | $(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | −1.5 | −1.5 | 2.25 | 2.25 |
| 2 | 3 | −0.5 | −0.5 | 0.25 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 5 |  0.5 |  1.5 | 0.75 | 0.25 |
| 4 | 4 |  1.5 |  0.5 | 0.75 | 2.25 |
| **Sum** | | | | **4.00** | **5.00** |

**Step 3:** Compute $\theta_1$.

$$\theta_1 = \frac{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{4.00}{5.00} = 0.8$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\theta_0$.

$$\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x} = 3.5 - 0.8 \times 2.5 = 3.5 - 2.0 = 1.5$$

**Step 5:** Final fitted model.

$$\hat{y} = 1.5 + 0.8 x$$

**Step 6:** Compute $R^2$ to verify fit quality.

Predictions: $\hat{y}(1) = 2.3$, $\hat{y}(2) = 3.1$, $\hat{y}(3) = 3.9$, $\hat{y}(4) = 4.7$.

$$SS_{\text{res}} = (2-2.3)^2 + (3-3.1)^2 + (5-3.9)^2 + (4-4.7)^2 = 0.09 + 0.01 + 1.21 + 0.49 = 1.80$$

$$SS_{\text{tot}} = (2-3.5)^2 + (3-3.5)^2 + (5-3.5)^2 + (4-3.5)^2 = 2.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 = 5.00$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{1.80}{5.00} = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64$$

The model explains **64%** of the variance — a moderate fit.

## 4.1 End-to-End Regression Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize theta randomly] --> B[Compute Predictions yhat]
    B --> C[Compute Cost J theta]
    C --> D[Compute Gradients dJ dtheta]
    D --> E[Update theta: theta minus alpha times gradient]
    E --> F{Converged OR Max Iterations Reached}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[Return Optimal theta]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Model Complexity Increases] --> B[Error Decomposition]
    B --> C[Bias squared decreases]
    B --> D[Variance increases]

    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[U Shaped Curve: Sweet Spot at Optimal Complexity]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linear Regression MSE Base] --> B[Add Regularization Penalty]
    B --> C{Choose Penalty Type}
    C -->|L2: sum of theta squared| D[Ridge Regression: Shrinks coefficients]
    C -->|L1: sum of absolute theta| E[Lasso Regression: Sparse models some theta = 0]
    C -->|L1 plus L2 Combined| F[Elastic Net: Balance shrinkage and sparsity]
    D --> G[Good for Multicollinearity]
    E --> H[Good for Feature Selection]
    F --> I[Good for High Dimensional Data with Correlated Features]
```

