## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define regression. Differentiate between simple linear regression and multiple linear regression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Regression** is a supervised learning technique used to predict a continuous dependent variable $Y$ from one or more independent variables $X$ by learning a mapping function $f: X \to Y$.

| Feature | Simple Linear Regression | Multiple Linear Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Number of predictors | **One** | **Two or more** |
| Functional form | $\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | $\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots$ |
| Visualization | 2D straight line | n-dimensional hyperplane |
| Complexity | Low | Higher; risk of multicollinearity |

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clear differentiation with formulas.)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the cost function in linear regression? Why is Mean Squared Error (MSE) preferred over Mean Absolute Error (MAE)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cost function** quantifies the error between predicted values $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ and actual values $y^{(i)}$. For linear regression, the standard cost is the **Mean Squared Error**:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Why MSE is preferred:**
1. **Differentiable** everywhere — gradient descent optimization works smoothly. (MAE is not differentiable at zero error.)
2. **Quadratic penalty** — larger errors are penalized more heavily, driving the model to fit outliers more aggressively.
3. **Closed-form solution exists** — Normal Equation $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ is derivable.

**(1 mark for cost function formula, 2 marks for the three reasons.)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the normal equation for simple linear regression. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following data, fit a linear regression model using the normal equation and predict $y$ when $x = 6$.

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 6 |

Compute $R^2$ for the fit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The simple linear model: $\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$.

The MSE cost function:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) = 0 \quad (1)$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)} = 0 \quad (2)$$

From (1): $m\theta_0 + \theta_1 \sum x^{(i)} = \sum y^{(i)} \Rightarrow \theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$.

Substituting into (2) and solving:

$$\theta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Compute means: $\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3$, $\bar{y} = \frac{2+4+5+4+6}{5} = 4.2$.

| $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ | $x^{(i)} - 3$ | $y^{(i)} - 4.2$ | Product | $(x^{(i)} - 3)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | −2 | −2.2 | 4.4 | 4 |
| 2 | 4 | −1 | −0.2 | 0.2 | 1 |
| 3 | 5 |  0 |  0.8 | 0.0 | 0 |
| 4 | 4 |  1 | −0.2 | −0.2 | 1 |
| 5 | 6 |  2 |  1.8 | 3.6 | 4 |
| **Sum** | | | | **8.0** | **10** |

$$\theta_1 = \frac{8.0}{10} = 0.8$$

$$\theta_0 = 4.2 - 0.8 \times 3 = 4.2 - 2.4 = 1.8$$

**Fitted model:** $\hat{y} = 1.8 + 0.8 x$.

**Prediction at $x = 6$:** $\hat{y} = 1.8 + 0.8 \times 6 = 1.8 + 4.8 = \mathbf{6.6}$.

**Compute $R^2$:**

Predictions: $\hat{y}(1) = 2.6$, $\hat{y}(2) = 3.4$, $\hat{y}(3) = 4.2$, $\hat{y}(4) = 5.0$, $\hat{y}(5) = 5.8$.

$$SS_{\text{res}} = (2-2.6)^2 + (4-3.4)^2 + (5-4.2)^2 + (4-5.0)^2 + (6-5.8)^2$$
$$= 0.36 + 0.36 + 0.64 + 1.00 + 0.04 = 2.40$$

$$SS_{\text{tot}} = (2-4.2)^2 + (4-4.2)^2 + (5-4.2)^2 + (4-4.2)^2 + (6-4.2)^2$$
$$= 4.84 + 0.04 + 0.64 + 0.04 + 3.24 = 8.80$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{2.40}{8.80} = 1 - 0.2727 = \mathbf{0.7273}$$

The model explains **72.73%** of variance — a good fit.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the gradient descent algorithm for linear regression. List and explain three variants of gradient descent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is regularization? Compare Ridge (L2) and Lasso (L1) regression with suitable mathematical formulations. When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Gradient Descent for Linear Regression (7 marks):**

Gradient descent is an **iterative optimization algorithm** that minimizes $J(\theta_0, \theta_1, \dots, \theta_n)$ by updating parameters in the direction of steepest descent (negative gradient).

**Algorithm steps:**
1. Initialize $\theta_j$ (often to 0 or small random values).
2. Compute predictions: $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \dots + \theta_n x_n^{(i)}$.
3. Compute the gradient: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$.
4. Update simultaneously:
   $$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$$
5. Repeat until convergence ($\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$) or max iterations.

**Three variants of gradient descent:**

| Variant | Data per Update | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Uses **all $m$ samples** per iteration | Stable convergence, deterministic | Slow for large $m$; needs full dataset in memory |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | Uses **one sample** per iteration | Fast, can escape local minima, online learning | Noisy convergence path |
| **Mini-batch GD** | Uses **batch of $b$ samples** ($1 < b < m$) per iteration | Balance between speed and stability; GPU-friendly | Requires tuning batch size $b$ |

**Part (b) — Regularization: Ridge vs Lasso (7 marks):**

**Regularization** is a technique to **prevent overfitting** by adding a penalty term to the cost function that discourages large parameter values.

**Ridge Regression (L2):**

$$J_{\text{Ridge}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2 \right]$$

Closed-form solution: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\text{Ridge}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$.

- Shrinks coefficients **toward zero** but never exactly zero.
- Handles **multicollinearity** effectively.
- All features retained.

**Lasso Regression (L1):**

$$J_{\text{Lasso}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert \theta_j \vert \right]$$

- Drives some coefficients to **exactly zero** → automatic **feature selection**.
- Produces **sparse, interpretable models**.
- Not closed-form; solved via **coordinate descent** or **LARS**.

**When to prefer which:**

| Scenario | Preferred Method |
|---|---|
| Multicollinearity, all features important | **Ridge** |
| High-dimensional data, need feature selection | **Lasso** |
| Many correlated features, want both effects | **Elastic Net** |
| $m \ll n$ (fewer samples than features) | **Lasso** (sparsity helps) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Gradient Descent Convergence:** Many students forget to mention the **simultaneous update** of parameters — sequential updates can yield incorrect results. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **MSE vs MAE:** Confusing the two cost functions is a common mistake — MSE squares, MAE uses absolute value. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **R² Interpretation:** Students often forget that R² can be **negative** (model worse than mean). **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Ridge vs Lasso:** Writing the penalty as $\lambda \sum \theta_i^2$ for Lasso is a **fatal error** — Lasso uses **absolute values**, not squares. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 5. **Multicollinearity:** OLS fails when features are correlated — Ridge fixes this; students often skip this distinction. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Regression** predicts **continuous** outputs; **Classification** predicts **discrete labels**.
- **Simple Linear Regression** has one feature; **Multiple Linear Regression** has $n \geq 2$ features.
- **Cost function** is **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: $J = \frac{1}{2m}\sum(\hat{y} - y)^2$.
- **Normal Equation** is the **closed-form OLS solution**: $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$.
- **Gradient Descent** is **iterative**, requires tuning of learning rate $\alpha$ and feature scaling.
- **Three GD variants:** Batch (stable/slow), Stochastic (fast/noisy), Mini-batch (balanced).
- **R²** measures variance explained: $R^2 = 1 - SS_{\text{res}}/SS_{\text{tot}}$.
- **Adjusted R²** penalizes extra features: $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{m-1}{m-n-1}$.
- **Ridge (L2)** shrinks coefficients; good for **multicollinearity**.
- **Lasso (L1)** produces **sparse models**; good for **feature selection**.
- **Polynomial Regression** models **non-linear** trends using $x, x^2, x^3, \dots$.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Simple models = high bias, complex models = high variance.
- **OLS Assumptions:** Linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, normality, no multicollinearity.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** MAE, MSE, RMSE, R², Adjusted R².
- **Mean of X and Y** appears in intercept formula: $\theta_0 = \bar{y} - \theta_1 \bar{x}$.
- **Slope** = Covariance(X,Y) / Variance(X).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification Model Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL TELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification Model Example

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Model** is a supervised machine learning technique that learns a mapping function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ from a labeled training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{n}$, where $x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is a feature vector with $m$ attributes and $y^{(i)} \in \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ is a discrete class label, in order to predict the categorical output for new, unseen instances.

In simpler terms, a **Classification Model** is a function that sorts incoming data into predefined buckets (classes) based on patterns it has already seen during training. The keyword here is **"discrete"** — we are not predicting a quantity (like height or price); we are predicting a *category* (Yes/No, Spam/Not-Spam, Disease/Healthy).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction**:
> - **Classification** → Output is a *categorical/discrete* label.
> - **Regression** → Output is a *continuous* real-valued number.
> This distinction is a guaranteed **Part-A question** in every KTU exam cycle.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Email Spam Filter

Imagine you are a postal worker who has spent years delivering mail. Over time, you have developed a strong "sixth sense" — you can glance at the sender, subject line, and keywords inside a letter and instantly decide whether to drop it in the **"Inbox"** tray or the **"Spam"** tray.

You did not memorize every rule. Instead, you learned a *pattern*:
- "FREE!!!" in the subject → probably spam.
- Email from your boss with a meeting agenda → probably not spam.

A **Classification Model** does exactly this. It studies a historical dataset (emails you already labeled as spam or not spam), extracts statistical patterns, and builds a mathematical rule to classify future, never-before-seen emails. The "postal worker" is the trained model; the "letters" are the test instances.

> [!TIP]
> **Other Real-World Examples of Classification:**
> - **Medical Diagnosis**: Predicting whether a tumor is *benign* or *malignant*.
> - **Credit Scoring**: Approving or rejecting a loan application.
> - **Image Recognition**: Tagging a photo as *cat, dog, or bird*.
> - **Sentiment Analysis**: Classifying a tweet as *positive, negative, or neutral*.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Any Classification Problem

Any classification task, no matter how complex, is defined by these **three** components. Memorize this — KTU examiners love testing this:

| Pillar | Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feature Vector** | $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$ | The measurable input attributes | `[Outlook=Sunny, Temp=Hot, Humidity=High]` |
| **Class Label** | $y \in \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ | The discrete category to predict | `{Yes, No}` for "Play Tennis" |
| **Hypothesis Function** | $h_\theta(x)$ | The learned mapping $X \rightarrow Y$ | The trained Naive Bayes model |

## 1.4 The Working Example We Will Use Throughout

To keep things concrete, this module will walk through the **"Play Tennis"** decision problem — a benchmark dataset used in classical AI/ML textbooks. The question is: *"Given the weather conditions, should we play tennis today?"*

> [!NOTE]
> The dataset has **14 historical days** and **4 features** (Outlook, Temperature, Humidity, Wind). The class label is binary: **Yes** (play) or **No** (don't play). We will use the **Naive Bayes Classifier** to build the model because it has a transparent, hand-computable mathematical formulation — perfect for a KTU board exam.

This example will be the spine of Sections 2, 3, and 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Naive Bayes Classifier — Theoretical Foundation

**Naive Bayes** is a *probabilistic* classifier grounded directly in **Bayes' Theorem** from probability theory. It is called "naive" because it makes a strong simplifying assumption: **all features are conditionally independent of each other given the class label**.

While this assumption is rarely true in the real world (e.g., humidity and outlook are correlated), it dramatically simplifies computation and still produces surprisingly accurate results — which is why Naive Bayes is a staple in spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and medical diagnosis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Naive Bayes falls under the "Probabilistic Learning" paradigm in the GNEST305 syllabus. The examiner expects you to (1) state Bayes' Theorem, (2) explain the "naive" independence assumption, and (3) solve a numerical problem end-to-end.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Decision Rule (Step-by-Step Logic)

The classifier must decide which class $C_k$ is most probable given the observed feature vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$. Mathematically, we want:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k \in \{Yes, No\}} P(C_k \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)
$$

We cannot compute this posterior directly, so we apply **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$
P(C_k \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(x)}
$$

Since $P(x)$ is the same constant for every class (it does not depend on $C_k$), we can ignore it for comparison and write:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m \mid C_k)
$$

Now we apply the **chain rule of probability**:

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, C_k) \cdots
$$

This grows computationally infeasible. The **Naive Independence Assumption** states:

> Given the class $C_k$, every feature $x_i$ is independent of every other feature $x_j$.

This collapses the chain rule into a simple product of marginals:

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m \mid C_k) \approx \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(x_j \mid C_k)
$$

**Final Decision Rule for Naive Bayes:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} \; P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(x_j \mid C_k)}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> This table is your **exam-day survival kit**. Memorize the symbols, meanings, and units.

| # | Symbol / Formula | Name | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(C_k)$ | **Prior Probability** | How frequent class $C_k$ is in the training data | Starting belief before seeing evidence |
| 2 | $P(x_j \mid C_k)$ | **Likelihood** | Probability of feature $x_j$ occurring *given* class $C_k$ | Evidence from each attribute |
| 3 | $P(C_k \mid x)$ | **Posterior Probability** | Updated belief after seeing all features | Final decision quantity |
| 4 | $P(x)$ | **Evidence / Marginal** | Normalizing constant (same for all classes) | Can be ignored during $\arg\max$ |
| 5 | $P(C_k) \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(x_j \vert C_k)$ | **Naive Bayes Score** | Unnormalized posterior for class $C_k$ | Used to rank candidate classes |
| 6 | $\arg\max_{C_k}$ | **Argmax Operator** | Returns the class with the highest score | The actual predicted label $\hat{y}$ |
| 7 | $P(x_j = v \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_j = v, C_k)}{\text{count}(C_k)}$ | **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)** | Frequency-based estimate of likelihood | Tabulating the training data |
| 8 | $\hat{P}(x_j = v \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_j = v, C_k) + 1}{\text{count}(C_k) + \vert V_j \vert}$ | **Laplace Smoothing** | Avoids zero-probability for unseen values | When a feature value never co-occurs with a class |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often write $P(x \mid C_k)$ as the **likelihood** but mistakenly call it the "posterior". Remember: **Likelihood = evidence given the class; Posterior = belief about the class given the evidence.** They are inverses (in direction), related through Bayes' Theorem.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Naive Bayes classifiers are deployed in:
- **Email gateways** (Gmail, Outlook spam filters).
- **Medical decision support systems** (calculating disease risk from symptoms).
- **Text classification pipelines** (news categorization, sentiment scoring).
- **Real-time recommendation engines** (low-latency probabilistic scoring).

Its main engineering virtues are: **lightning-fast training, low memory footprint, and graceful handling of high-dimensional sparse data** — which is why it is the "first model to try" in any new text-classification project.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Worked Example

## 3.1 The "Play Tennis" Training Dataset (14 Days)

Below is the complete labeled dataset. **Memorize the structure** — KTU examiners may give a *slightly different* version and expect you to re-do the calculations.

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | **Play (Class)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | **No** |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | **No** |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | **Yes** |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | **Yes** |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | **No** |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | **Yes** |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | **No** |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | **Yes** |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | **Yes** |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | **No** |

**Test Query (Day 15)**: `Outlook = Sunny`, `Temp = Cool`, `Humidity = High`, `Wind = Strong`. Should we play tennis?

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute the Prior Probabilities $P(\text{Yes})$ and $P(\text{No})$

We count the number of days labeled "Yes" and "No" out of the total 14.

$$
P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{\text{Number of "Yes" days}}{\text{Total days}} = \frac{9}{14} \approx 0.6429
$$

$$
P(\text{No}) = \frac{\text{Number of "No" days}}{\text{Total days}} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.3571
$$

**Sanity check:** $P(\text{Yes}) + P(\text{No}) = \frac{9}{14} + \frac{5}{14} = \frac{14}{14} = 1.0$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The priors reflect the **base rate** of play in our training history. On most historical days, tennis was played, so the model starts with a slight bias toward "Yes" — but the evidence from the features can override this.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Build the Likelihood Tables (Frequency Tables)

We need $P(x_j \mid C_k)$ for every feature value and every class. The general formula is:

$$
P(x_j = v \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_j = v \text{ AND } C_k)}{\text{count}(C_k)}
$$

### Table A — Outlook (3 values: Sunny, Overcast, Rain)

| Outlook | Yes (out of 9) | $P(\text{Outlook} \mid \text{Yes})$ | No (out of 5) | $P(\text{Outlook} \mid \text{No})$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Sunny | 2 | $2/9$ | 3 | $3/5$ |
| Overcast | 4 | $4/9$ | 0 | $0/5$ |
| Rain | 3 | $3/9$ | 2 | $2/5$ |
| **Total** | **9** | **1.0** | **5** | **1.0** |

### Table B — Temperature (3 values: Hot, Mild, Cool)

| Temperature | Yes (out of 9) | $P(\text{Temp} \mid \text{Yes})$ | No (out of 5) | $P(\text{Temp} \mid \text{No})$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Hot | 2 | $2/9$ | 2 | $2/5$ |
| Mild | 4 | $4/9$ | 2 | $2/5$ |
| Cool | 3 | $3/9$ | 1 | $1/5$ |

### Table C — Humidity (2 values: High, Normal)

| Humidity | Yes (out of 9) | $P(\text{Humidity} \mid \text{Yes})$ | No (out of 5) | $P(\text{Humidity} \mid \text{No})$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| High | 3 | $3/9$ | 4 | $4/5$ |
| Normal | 6 | $6/9$ | 1 | $1/5$ |

### Table D — Wind (2 values: Weak, Strong)

| Wind | Yes (out of 9) | $P(\text{Wind} \mid \text{Yes})$ | No (out of 5) | $P(\text{Wind} \mid \text{No})$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Weak | 6 | $6/9$ | 2 | $2/5$ |
| Strong | 3 | $3/9$ | 3 | $3/5$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Zero-Probability Trap**: Notice that $P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = 0/5 = 0$. If our test instance had `Outlook = Overcast`, the entire product for class "No" would collapse to zero. In real-world systems, this is fixed using **Laplace Smoothing** (add-1 to numerator, add-$\vert V \vert$ to denominator). In a KTU exam, you may be asked to apply Laplace smoothing if the test query hits a zero-count cell.

## 3.4 Step 3 — Apply the Naive Bayes Decision Rule

Our test instance is $x = (\text{Sunny}, \text{Cool}, \text{High}, \text{Strong})$. We need to compare:

$$
P(\text{Yes} \mid x) \;\propto\; P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes})
$$

$$
P(\text{No} \mid x) \;\propto\; P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No})
$$

### Computation for Class = Yes

$$
P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes})
$$

$$
= \frac{9}{14} \cdot \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9}
$$

$$
= \frac{9 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3}{14 \times 9 \times 9 \times 9 \times 9}
$$

$$
= \frac{486}{91854} \approx 0.00529
$$

### Computation for Class = No

$$
P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No})
$$

$$
= \frac{5}{14} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{5}
$$

$$
= \frac{5 \times 3 \times 1 \times 4 \times 3}{14 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5}
$$

$$
= \frac{180}{8750} \approx 0.02057
$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Normalize the Posteriors (Optional but Examiner-Friendly)

The two values 0.00529 and 0.02057 are unnormalized scores. To convert them into true probabilities that sum to 1, we divide by their sum:

$$
P(\text{Yes} \mid x) = \frac{0.00529}{0.00529 + 0.02057} = \frac{0.00529}{0.02586} \approx 0.2046
$$

$$
P(\text{No} \mid x) = \frac{0.02057}{0.00529 + 0.02057} = \frac{0.02057}{0.02586} \approx 0.7954
$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — Make the Final Prediction

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} \{ P(\text{Yes} \mid x), P(\text{No} \mid x) \} = \arg\max \{0.2046, \; 0.7954\} = \text{No}}
$$

**Conclusion**: The model predicts we should **NOT play tennis** on a Sunny, Cool, High-Humidity, Strong-Wind day. The model's confidence in "No" is **79.54%**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why did "No" win despite the prior favoring "Yes"?** Three features (Sunny, Cool, High) push strongly toward "No" — their likelihoods under "No" are all higher than under "Yes". The single feature (Strong wind) is equally likely under both classes, so it acts as a tie-breaker. This is a classic example of **evidence overcoming the prior** — a core Bayesian principle.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Summary of the Decision

| Quantity | Class = Yes | Class = No | Winner |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Prior $P(C_k)$ | $9/14 = 0.6429$ | $5/14 = 0.3571$ | Yes (slight edge) |
| $P(\text{Sunny} \mid C_k)$ | $2/9 = 0.2222$ | $3/5 = 0.6000$ | No (strong) |
| $P(\text{Cool} \mid C_k)$ | $3/9 = 0.3333$ | $1/5 = 0.2000$ | Yes |
| $P(\text{High} \mid C_k)$ | $3/9 = 0.3333$ | $4/5 = 0.8000$ | No (very strong) |
| $P(\text{Strong} \mid C_k)$ | $3/9 = 0.3333$ | $3/5 = 0.6000$ | No |
| **Naive Bayes Score** | **0.00529** | **0.02057** | **No (3.9× higher)** |
| **Normalized Posterior** | **20.46%** | **79.54%** | **No** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Classification Pipeline

This block-level diagram maps the **complete lifecycle** of a classification model, from raw data ingestion to deployment. Each stage is essential — skipping any one of them produces a broken model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Data: 14 historical days with features and labels] --> B[Stage 1: Data Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Handle missing values]
    B --> B2[Encode categorical features]
    B --> B3[Split into Train and Test sets]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Feature Engineering]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Select relevant attributes]
    C --> C2[Construct likelihood tables]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: Model Training]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Compute Prior Probabilities P Yes and P No]
    D --> D2[Compute Likelihoods P x given Ck for all features]
    D1 --> E[Stage 4: Model Storage]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Store Naive Bayes Score Function]
    E --> F[Stage 5: Inference on New Instance]
    F --> F1[Receive test query: Sunny, Cool, High, Strong]
    F1 --> G[Stage 6: Apply Bayes Decision Rule]
    G --> G1[Compute Score Yes]
    G --> G2[Compute Score No]
    G1 --> H[Stage 7: Argmax Selection]
    G2 --> H
    H --> H1{Pick class with higher score}
    H1 -->|Yes 0.00529| I[Output: Play Tennis]
    H1 -->|No 0.02057| J[Output: Do Not Play]
    I --> K[Stage 8: Evaluation and Deployment]
    J --> K
    K --> K1[Compare with ground truth labels]
    K --> K2[Compute Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score]
    K1 --> L[Production Deployment]
    K2 --> L
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow Inside Naive Bayes Inference

This diagram zooms into **Stage 6** of the pipeline above, showing exactly how the model crunches the four features for a single test instance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        Q[Test Query X equals Sunny, Cool, High, Strong]
    end

    subgraph Evidence Extraction
        E1[P Sunny under Yes equals 2 by 9]
        E2[P Cool under Yes equals 3 by 9]
        E3[P High under Yes equals 3 by 9]
        E4[P Strong under Yes equals 3 by 9]
    end

    subgraph Class Yes Branch
        Y1[Prior P Yes equals 9 by 14]
        Y2[Multiply all likelihoods together]
        Y3[Score Yes equals 0.00529]
        Y1 --> Y2
        E1 --> Y2
        E2 --> Y2
        E3 --> Y2
        E4 --> Y2
        Y2 --> Y3
    end

    subgraph Class No Branch
        N1[Prior P No equals 5 by 14]
        N2[P Sunny under No equals 3 by 5]
        N3[P Cool under No equals 1 by 5]
        N4[P High under No equals 4 by 5]
        N5[P Strong under No equals 3 by 5]
        N6[Multiply all likelihoods together]
        N7[Score No equals 0.02057]
        N1 --> N6
        N2 --> N6
        N3 --> N6
        N4 --> N6
        N5 --> N6
        N6 --> N7
    end

    Q --> E1
    Q --> E2
    Q --> E3
    Q --> E4
    Q --> N2
    Q --> N3
    Q --> N4
    Q --> N5

    Y3 --> R[Argmax Comparison]
    N7 --> R
    R -->|No wins 0.02057| OUT[Final Prediction: Do Not Play Tennis]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Model — Bayesian Belief Update

This diagram shows the conceptual "flow of belief" as evidence is incorporated one feature at a time. The prior starts as a 64%/36% split in favor of "Yes". Each feature either reinforces or flips that belief.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initial Prior: P Yes equals 0.64, P No equals 0.36] --> F1[Add Evidence: Outlook equals Sunny]
    F1 --> F1a[Belief shifts: P No rises to 0.75]
    F1a --> F2[Add Evidence: Temperature equals Cool]
    F2 --> F2a[Belief shifts back slightly: P No equals 0.72]
    F2a --> F3[Add Evidence: Humidity equals High]
    F3 --> F3a[Strong shift: P No rises to 0.85]
    F3a --> F4[Add Evidence: Wind equals Strong]
    F4 --> F4a[Final: P No equals 0.7954, P Yes equals 0.2046]
    F4a --> End[Final Decision: NO, do not play tennis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above belief-update diagram is **conceptual** — actual numbers are illustrative to show direction of change, not exact posterior values after each step. The *exact* final values come from the joint product calculation in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a classification model with a suitable real-world example. Differentiate it from a regression model.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **classification model** is a supervised machine learning technique that predicts a *discrete categorical label* for a given input. It learns a function $h: X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ is a finite set of classes. (1 mark)

**Example**: An email spam filter that classifies incoming messages as "Spam" or "Not Spam" based on features like subject line, sender domain, and body keywords. (1 mark)

**Difference from regression**: In **classification**, the output is a *qualitative category* (e.g., Yes/No, Spam/Ham), while in **regression**, the output is a *quantitative continuous value* (e.g., price = ₹4,50,000, temperature = 32.5°C). (1 mark)

---

### Q2. State Bayes' Theorem and explain the role of the "naive" independence assumption in the Naive Bayes classifier.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bayes' Theorem** (1 mark):

$$
P(C_k \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(x)}
$$

Where $P(C_k \mid x)$ is the posterior, $P(x \mid C_k)$ is the likelihood, $P(C_k)$ is the prior, and $P(x)$ is the evidence.

**Naive Independence Assumption** (2 marks): The Naive Bayes classifier assumes that *given the class label, all features are conditionally independent of one another*. This simplifies the joint likelihood into a product of individual likelihoods:

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m \mid C_k) = \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(x_j \mid C_k)
$$

This assumption is "naive" because real-world features are often correlated (e.g., humidity and outlook), yet the assumption makes the model computationally tractable and surprisingly effective for tasks like spam filtering and medical diagnosis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Consider the "Play Tennis" dataset given below:

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

Build a **Naive Bayes classifier** from scratch. Compute the prior probabilities and all four likelihood tables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the model built in part (a), classify the new instance: `Outlook = Sunny`, `Temp = Cool`, `Humidity = High`, `Wind = Strong`. Show all multiplication steps explicitly. Also explain why the model arrived at its final prediction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Model Construction (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Count classes (1 mark)**
- "Yes" appears on days 3, 4, 5, 7, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13 → **9 days**.
- "No" appears on days 1, 2, 6, 8, 14 → **5 days**.

**Step 2: Prior probabilities (1 mark)**

$$
P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{9}{14}, \quad P(\text{No}) = \frac{5}{14}
$$

**Step 3: Likelihood tables (5 marks — one for each feature, distribute 1.25 marks each, or use a balanced rubric)**

| Feature | Value | $P(\cdot \mid \text{Yes})$ | $P(\cdot \mid \text{No})$ |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| Outlook | Sunny | 2/9 | 3/5 |
| Outlook | Overcast | 4/9 | 0/5 |
| Outlook | Rain | 3/9 | 2/5 |
| Temperature | Hot | 2/9 | 2/5 |
| Temperature | Mild | 4/9 | 2/5 |
| Temperature | Cool | 3/9 | 1/5 |
| Humidity | High | 3/9 | 4/5 |
| Humidity | Normal | 6/9 | 1/5 |
| Wind | Weak | 6/9 | 2/5 |
| Wind | Strong | 3/9 | 3/5 |

---

**Part (b) — Inference on New Instance (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify feature values (0.5 marks)**
$x = (\text{Sunny}, \text{Cool}, \text{High}, \text{Strong})$

**Step 2: Compute the unnormalized score for "Yes" (2 marks)**

$$
P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes})
$$

$$
= \frac{9}{14} \cdot \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} = \frac{486}{91854} \approx 0.00529
$$

**Step 3: Compute the unnormalized score for "No" (2 marks)**

$$
P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No})
$$

$$
= \frac{5}{14} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{5} = \frac{180}{8750} \approx 0.02057
$$

**Step 4: Normalize (1 mark)**

$$
P(\text{Yes} \mid x) = \frac{0.00529}{0.02586} \approx 0.2046, \quad P(\text{No} \mid x) = \frac{0.02057}{0.02586} \approx 0.7954
$$

**Step 5: Apply argmax and explain (1.5 marks)**

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max\{0.2046, \; 0.7954\} = \text{No}
$$

**Explanation**: Although the prior slightly favored "Yes" (64.29% vs 35.71%), three out of four features — Sunny, High Humidity, and Strong Wind — strongly favor "No". The likelihoods under "No" are roughly 2-3× higher for these features. The Temperature = Cool feature weakly favors "Yes", but it is overwhelmed by the other three. Thus, the joint evidence overrides the prior, and the model predicts "**Do not play tennis**" with 79.54% confidence.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the three core components of a classification problem: the feature vector, the class label, and the hypothesis function. How does the Naive Bayes classifier represent the hypothesis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-class medical diagnosis dataset with 100 patients, where 60 are "Healthy" and 40 are "Diseased". The model is asked to classify a patient with symptoms $\{x_1 = \text{Fever}, x_2 = \text{Cough}, x_3 = \text{Fatigue}\}$. Given the following likelihoods:

$$
P(\text{Fever} \mid \text{Healthy}) = 0.1, \quad P(\text{Fever} \mid \text{Diseased}) = 0.7
$$
$$
P(\text{Cough} \mid \text{Healthy}) = 0.2, \quad P(\text{Cough} \mid \text{Diseased}) = 0.8
$$
$$
P(\text{Fatigue} \mid \text{Healthy}) = 0.15, \quad P(\text{Fatigue} \mid \text{Diseased}) = 0.6
$$

Apply the Naive Bayes decision rule to predict the diagnosis. Show all steps and compute the normalized posterior probabilities. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)**

1. **Feature Vector** (2 marks): A feature vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$ is an $m$-dimensional representation of an instance, where each $x_j$ is a measurable attribute. For the Play Tennis example, $x = (\text{Outlook}, \text{Temperature}, \text{Humidity}, \text{Wind})$ with $m = 4$. The feature vector is the *input* to the classifier.

2. **Class Label** (2 marks): A class label $y \in \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ is a discrete categorical output from a finite set of $k$ classes. In binary classification, $k = 2$ (e.g., {Yes, No} or {Spam, Ham}). The class label is the *target* the model is trying to predict.

3. **Hypothesis Function** (3 marks): The hypothesis $h_\theta: X \rightarrow Y$ is the learned mapping from feature space to label space. In Naive Bayes, the hypothesis is fully characterized by the prior distribution $P(C_k)$ and the likelihood distribution $P(x_j \mid C_k)$ for every feature $x_j$ and every class $C_k$. Together, these parameters are sufficient to compute the posterior for any test instance. The "training" phase of Naive Bayes is simply the estimation of these probability tables from the training data.

---

**Part (b) — Medical Diagnosis Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Write down the priors (1 mark)**

$$
P(\text{Healthy}) = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60, \quad P(\text{Diseased}) = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40
$$

**Step 2: Score for "Healthy" (2 marks)**

$$
P(\text{Healthy}) \cdot P(\text{Fever} \mid \text{Healthy}) \cdot P(\text{Cough} \mid \text{Healthy}) \cdot P(\text{Fatigue} \mid \text{Healthy})
$$

$$
= 0.60 \times 0.10 \times 0.20 \times 0.15 = 0.00180
$$

**Step 3: Score for "Diseased" (2 marks)**

$$
P(\text{Diseased}) \cdot P(\text{Fever} \mid \text{Diseased}) \cdot P(\text{Cough} \mid \text{Diseased}) \cdot P(\text{Fatigue} \mid \text{Diseased})
$$

$$
= 0.40 \times 0.70 \times 0.80 \times 0.60 = 0.13440
$$

**Step 4: Normalize (1 mark)**

$$
P(\text{Healthy} \mid x) = \frac{0.00180}{0.00180 + 0.13440} = \frac{0.00180}{0.13620} \approx 0.0132
$$

$$
P(\text{Diseased} \mid x) = \frac{0.13440}{0.00180 + 0.13440} = \frac{0.13440}{0.13620} \approx 0.9868
$$

**Step 5: Final prediction (1 mark)**

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max\{0.0132, \; 0.9868\} = \text{Diseased}
$$

**Interpretation**: The model predicts the patient is **Diseased** with **98.68% confidence**. Despite the prior favoring "Healthy" (60% vs 40%), the symptoms Fever, Cough, and Fatigue are 4-7× more likely under the "Diseased" class. The likelihood evidence overwhelmingly overrides the prior.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this type of question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute priors explicitly** (–1 mark): Many students jump straight to likelihoods. Always show $P(\text{Yes})$ and $P(\text{No})$ first.
> 2. **Wrong denominator in MLE** (–1 to –2 marks): The likelihood denominator is $\text{count}(C_k)$, NOT the total dataset size. $P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = 2/9$, not $2/14$.
> 3. **Skipping normalization** (–1 mark): KTU examiners often award a mark for normalized posteriors. Always convert unnormalized scores to actual probabilities.
> 4. **Not stating the final argmax** (–0.5 to –1 mark): Always end with a clear sentence: *"Therefore, the predicted class is No."*
> 5. **Ignoring Laplace Smoothing** (–1 mark if the query hits a zero-count cell): If a feature-class combination has zero count, the entire product becomes zero. Always mention smoothing in such cases.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification Models & Naive Bayes Example**

- **Classification** predicts a *discrete label*; **Regression** predicts a *continuous value*.
- A **classification model** is a function $h: X \rightarrow Y$ where $Y$ is a finite set of class labels.
- The **Naive Bayes classifier** is grounded in **Bayes' Theorem** and assumes **conditional independence** of features given the class.
- The **Naive Bayes decision rule** is:
  $$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} \; P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(x_j \vert C_k)$$
- **Prior** $P(C_k)$ = class frequency in training data.
- **Likelihood** $P(x_j \vert C_k)$ = frequency of feature value within a class, computed as $\frac{\text{count}(x_j = v, C_k)}{\text{count}(C_k)}$.
- The **evidence** $P(x)$ is the same for all classes and can be ignored during $\arg\max$.
- **Normalization** converts unnormalized scores to true probabilities: $P(C_k \vert x) = \frac{\text{score}(C_k)}{\sum_{C_i} \text{score}(C_i)}$.
- The final prediction is the class with the **highest posterior probability**.
- **Laplace Smoothing** (add-1) prevents zero-probability collapse for unseen feature-class combinations.
- **Play Tennis** is a classic 14-day, 4-feature, 2-class dataset used to demonstrate Naive Bayes by hand.
- **Real-world applications** of Naive Bayes include spam filtering, medical diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and news categorization.
- Always show **explicit multiplication steps** in the exam — examiners mark intermediate work, not just the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logistic Regression** is a fundamental **supervised machine learning algorithm** used primarily for **binary classification tasks**, where the goal is to predict the probability that a given input instance belongs to a specific class (labeled 0 or 1). It is a variant of the generalized linear model that uses the **logistic (sigmoid) function** to model a binary outcome based on one or more independent predictor variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Logistic regression is a classification algorithm—not a regression algorithm—despite its misleading name. It estimates the parameters of a logistic model (a form of binary regression) to predict the probability of a categorical dependent variable.

The mathematical core of logistic regression is built on the **logit transformation**, which converts a probability $p \in [0, 1]$ into an unbounded real value $z \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ through the log-odds formulation:

$$
\text{logit}(p) = \ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)
$$

The inverse of this transformation, the **sigmoid function**, is what gives logistic regression its characteristic S-shaped curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary):**
> - **Logit:** The natural logarithm of the odds.
> - **Odds:** The ratio of the probability of success to failure, $\frac{p}{1-p}$.
> - **Sigmoid (Logistic) Function:** The mapping that transforms any real number into a value between 0 and 1.
> - **MLE (Maximum Likelihood Estimation):** The parameter estimation method used to fit the model.
> - **Cross-Entropy Loss:** The cost function optimized during training.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **college admissions officer** trying to decide whether to admit a student based on their entrance exam score. The decision isn't a sharp "yes" or "no"—it's a *probability* (e.g., "This student has a 78% chance of succeeding"). **Logistic Regression** works exactly like this:

- **Input:** Student's exam score (continuous numerical value).
- **Output:** A probability between **0** and **1** (e.g., 0.78 = 78% chance of admission).
- **Decision Rule:** If probability $\geq 0.5$, classify as **"Admit"** (class 1); otherwise, **"Reject"** (class 0).

Another classic real-world analogy is the **medical diagnosis scenario**:
- A doctor has a patient's tumor size, age, and blood test results.
- Logistic regression takes these features and outputs the probability that the tumor is **malignant** (class 1) or **benign** (class 0).
- A threshold (typically **0.5**) is then applied to make the final classification.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of logistic regression as a **"soft decision-maker"**—it never makes a hard rule; it always gives you a *confidence score*, and you decide the cutoff.

### 1.3 Why Not Linear Regression for Classification?

| Aspect | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Range** | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| **Purpose** | Predict continuous values | Predict probabilities (classification) |
| **Activation** | None (identity) | **Sigmoid function** |
| **Cost Function** | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)** |
| **Problem with Classification** | Predictions can be < 0 or > 1 | Always bounded in $[0, 1]$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Applying linear regression to classification problems leads to unbounded predictions (e.g., a probability of -0.3 or 1.7), which is mathematically meaningless. Logistic regression **solves this** by using the sigmoid squashing function.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logistic (Sigmoid) Function

The **sigmoid function** is the cornerstone of logistic regression. It maps any real-valued number into the range $(0, 1)$, making it suitable for representing probabilities.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

**Why does this function matter?**
- It is **monotonically increasing** (always goes up as $z$ increases).
- It is **S-shaped** (sigmoidal), with a smooth transition.
- At $z = 0$, $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ (the natural decision boundary).
- As $z \to +\infty$, $\sigma(z) \to 1$.
- As $z \to -\infty$, $\sigma(z) \to 0$.

**Properties of the Sigmoid Function (High-Yield for KTU):**

$$
\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot \left(1 - \sigma(z)\right)
$$

This elegant derivative property is **why** the sigmoid function is computationally efficient for gradient descent—a KTU favorite question!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** The derivative of the sigmoid function is expressible in terms of itself. This property simplifies the gradient computation in logistic regression significantly.

### 2.2 Hypothesis Representation

In logistic regression, the hypothesis $h_\theta(x)$ represents the estimated probability that the output $y = 1$ for a given input $x$, parameterized by weights $\theta$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Linear Combination:** First, compute a linear combination of inputs and weights (just like linear regression):
$$
z = \theta^T x = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_n x_n
$$

2. **Apply Sigmoid Activation:** Pass $z$ through the sigmoid to get a probability:
$$
h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}
$$

3. **Interpretation:**
$$
h_\theta(x) = P(y = 1 \mid x; \theta)
$$

   The probability that $y = 0$ is simply:
$$
P(y = 0 \mid x; \theta) = 1 - h_\theta(x)
$$

### 2.3 Decision Boundary

The **decision boundary** is the line (or surface) that separates the regions where $h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5$ from where $h_\theta(x) < 0.5$.

**Derivation of the Boundary:**
- $\sigma(z) \geq 0.5$ when $z \geq 0$
- $\sigma(z) < 0.5$ when $z < 0$

Therefore, the decision boundary is defined by:
$$
\theta^T x = 0
$$

**Types of Decision Boundaries:**
- **Linear Boundary:** When the model uses raw features (e.g., $\theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 = 0$)
- **Non-Linear Boundary:** When polynomial features are introduced (e.g., $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = r$)

### 2.4 Cost Function: Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)

Why can't we use Mean Squared Error (MSE) for logistic regression? Because the sigmoid function is **non-linear**, MSE produces a **non-convex** cost function with many local minima—gradient descent is not guaranteed to find the global minimum.

**Solution:** Use the **Log Loss (Cross-Entropy)** function, which is convex and well-suited for probability-based predictions.

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log\left(h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right) + \left(1 - y^{(i)}\right) \log\left(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right) \right]
$$

**Intuition Behind Each Term:**
- When $y = 1$: Only the first term $\log(h)$ matters. The cost is **0** if the model predicts $h = 1$, and **infinity** if $h = 0$ (heavy penalty!).
- When $y = 0$: Only the second term $\log(1 - h)$ matters. The cost is **0** if $h = 0$, and **infinity** if $h = 1$.

This **asymmetric penalty** is what forces the model to be confident in the correct class.

### 2.5 Gradient Descent Update Rule

The gradient of the cost function with respect to parameter $\theta_j$ is surprisingly elegant:

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Update Rule (Vectorized Form):**
$$
\theta := \theta - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} X^T \left( h_\theta(X) - y \right)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Surprise Fact:** The gradient descent update rule for logistic regression looks **identical** to that of linear regression! The critical difference is the definition of $h_\theta(x)$—sigmoid for logistic, identity for linear.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid Function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps real values to probabilities |
| Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ | Used in backpropagation |
| Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ | Probability estimate |
| Logit (Log-Odds) | $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | Linearizing the sigmoid |
| Decision Boundary | $\theta^T x = 0$ | Separates classes |
| Cost Function (Log Loss) | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum \left[ y \log h + (1-y)\log(1-h) \right]$ | Model optimization |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Parameter learning |
| Regularized Cost | $J_{reg}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum \theta_j^2$ | Prevent overfitting |
| Odds Ratio | $\text{Odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}$ | Interpret feature impact |
| Multinomial Extension | Softmax: $P(y=k) = \frac{e^{\theta_k^T x}}{\sum_j e^{\theta_j^T x}}$ | Multiclass classification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Notation Reminder:** In KTU board exam answers, never write absolute values as `|x|` inside markdown tables—this breaks formatting. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Healthcare:** Disease prediction (diabetes, cancer, heart disease).
- **Finance:** Credit card fraud detection, loan default prediction.
- **Marketing:** Customer churn prediction, click-through rate forecasting.
- **NLP:** Spam detection, sentiment analysis (binary).
- **Manufacturing:** Defect detection in quality control.
- **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion detection systems.
- **Aerospace:** Engine failure prediction from sensor data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Why the Sigmoid Function?

**Starting Premise:** We want to model $P(y=1 \mid x)$ as a function of $x$, with output bounded in $(0, 1)$.

**Step 1: Start with the odds ratio.**

The odds of an event are defined as the ratio of its probability of occurring to its probability of not occurring:

$$
\text{Odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}
$$

where $p = P(y=1 \mid x)$. The odds are bounded in $(0, +\infty)$.

**Step 2: Apply the natural logarithm (logit transformation).**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = z
$$

This maps odds from $(0, +\infty)$ to $(-\infty, +\infty)$—a full real line. This is ideal for a linear combination of features.

**Step 3: Assume linearity in the log-odds.**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \theta^T x
$$

This is the **core assumption** of logistic regression: the log-odds are linear in the features.

**Step 4: Solve for $p$.**

Exponentiate both sides:
$$
\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

Multiply both sides by $(1-p)$:
$$
p = (1-p) \cdot e^{\theta^T x}
$$

Expand:
$$
p = e^{\theta^T x} - p \cdot e^{\theta^T x}
$$

Collect $p$ terms on the left:
$$
p + p \cdot e^{\theta^T x} = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

Factor out $p$:
$$
p \left(1 + e^{\theta^T x}\right) = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

Solve for $p$:
$$
p = \frac{e^{\theta^T x}}{1 + e^{\theta^T x}}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by $e^{\theta^T x}$:**

$$
p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is exactly the sigmoid function!

$$
\boxed{P(y=1 \mid x; \theta) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost Function from Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

**Starting Premise:** Logistic regression is trained by maximizing the **likelihood** of the observed data.

**Step 1: Write the likelihood function.**

For $m$ independent training examples, the probability of observing all labels $y^{(i)}$ given inputs $x^{(i)}$ and parameters $\theta$ is:

$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta)
$$

**Step 2: Use the Bernoulli distribution property.**

For each example, $P(y \mid x; \theta)$ can be compactly written as:

$$
P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta) = \left(h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right)^{y^{(i)}} \cdot \left(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right)^{1 - y^{(i)}}
$$

Why? Because:
- If $y^{(i)} = 1$: expression reduces to $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$.
- If $y^{(i)} = 0$: expression reduces to $1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})$.

**Step 3: Substitute into the likelihood:**

$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \left[ h_\theta(x^{(i)}) \right]^{y^{(i)}} \cdot \left[ 1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}) \right]^{1 - y^{(i)}}
$$

**Step 4: Take the natural log to get the log-likelihood.**

The logarithm is monotonically increasing, so maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to maximizing the likelihood. The product becomes a sum:

$$
\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + \left(1 - y^{(i)}\right) \log \left(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right) \right]
$$

**Step 5: Convert maximization to minimization.**

We want a *cost* (loss) to *minimize*. Negate the log-likelihood and average over $m$ examples:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + \left(1 - y^{(i)}\right) \log \left(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right) \right]
$$

**Conclusion:** This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss** (Log Loss).

$$
\boxed{J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log\left(h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right) + \left(1 - y^{(i)}\right) \log\left(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\right) \right]}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gradient of the Cost Function

**Goal:** Compute $\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ for the gradient descent update.

**Step 1: Start with a single training example's loss:**

$$
\text{Loss}^{(i)} = -\left[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 2: Compute the derivative of $h$ with respect to $z$:**

Recall $h = \sigma(z)$ and $z = \theta^T x$. We already know:

$$
\frac{\partial h}{\partial z} = h(1 - h)
$$

**Step 3: Apply the chain rule to the loss:**

$$
\frac{\partial \text{Loss}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial \text{Loss}^{(i)}}{\partial h^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j}
$$

**Step 4: Compute each partial derivative.**

First term:
$$
\frac{\partial \text{Loss}^{(i)}}{\partial h^{(i)}} = -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}}{h^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - h^{(i)}} \right] = \frac{h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{h^{(i)}\left(1 - h^{(i)}\right)}
$$

Second term (sigmoid derivative):
$$
\frac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = h^{(i)}\left(1 - h^{(i)}\right)
$$

Third term (linear):
$$
\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 5: Multiply the three terms:**

$$
\frac{\partial \text{Loss}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}}{h^{(i)}\left(1 - h^{(i)}\right)} \cdot h^{(i)}\left(1 - h^{(i)}\right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}
$$

The $h(1-h)$ terms cancel beautifully:

$$
\frac{\partial \text{Loss}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = \left(h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 6: Average over all $m$ examples for the full cost gradient:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}}
$$

**Step 7: Vectorized implementation:**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} X^T \left( h_\theta(X) - y \right)
$$

where $X$ is the $(m \times n)$ feature matrix and $h_\theta(X)$ is the $(m \times 1)$ vector of predicted probabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The cancellation in Step 5 is **why logistic regression's gradient is computationally cheap**—the expensive-to-compute $h(1-h)$ terms vanish! Memorize this elegant simplification.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example with Numerical Data

**Problem Setup:** Given 3 training examples, manually compute one gradient descent step.

| $i$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 1.5 | 1 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 0.5 | 0 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 1 |

Initialize: $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0.1$, $\theta_2 = 0.1$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$.

**Step A: Compute $z^{(i)} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \theta_2 x_2^{(i)}$ for each example.**

For $i=1$:
$$
z^{(1)} = 0 + 0.1(0.5) + 0.1(1.5) = 0.05 + 0.15 = 0.20
$$

For $i=2$:
$$
z^{(2)} = 0 + 0.1(1.0) + 0.1(0.5) = 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.15
$$

For $i=3$:
$$
z^{(3)} = 0 + 0.1(1.5) + 0.1(2.0) = 0.15 + 0.20 = 0.35
$$

**Step B: Apply sigmoid to get $h^{(i)}$.**

$$
h^{(1)} = \sigma(0.20) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.20}} \approx 0.5498
$$

$$
h^{(2)} = \sigma(0.15) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.15}} \approx 0.5374
$$

$$
h^{(3)} = \sigma(0.35) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.35}} \approx 0.5866
$$

**Step C: Compute the gradient $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$.**

For $\theta_0$ (using $x_0 = 1$):
$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (0.5498 - 1)(1) + (0.5374 - 0)(1) + (0.5866 - 1)(1) \right]
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{3} \left[ -0.4502 + 0.5374 - 0.4134 \right] = \frac{1}{3}(-0.3262) \approx -0.1087
$$

For $\theta_1$:
$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (0.5498 - 1)(0.5) + (0.5374 - 0)(1.0) + (0.5866 - 1)(1.5) \right]
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{3} \left[ -0.2251 + 0.5374 - 0.6201 \right] = \frac{1}{3}(-0.3078) \approx -0.1026
$$

For $\theta_2$:
$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (0.5498 - 1)(1.5) + (0.5374 - 0)(0.5) + (0.5866 - 1)(2.0) \right]
$$
$$
= \frac{1}{3} \left[ -0.6753 + 0.2687 - 0.8268 \right] = \frac{1}{3}(-1.2334) \approx -0.4111
$$

**Step D: Update parameters using $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$.**

$$
\theta_0 := 0 - 0.5 \times (-0.1087) = 0 + 0.0544 = 0.0544
$$

$$
\theta_1 := 0.1 - 0.5 \times (-0.1026) = 0.1 + 0.0513 = 0.1513
$$

$$
\theta_2 := 0.1 - 0.5 \times (-0.4111) = 0.1 + 0.2056 = 0.3056
$$

**Final Updated Parameters:** $\theta_0 = 0.0544$, $\theta_1 = 0.1513$, $\theta_2 = 0.3056$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU board exam rarely asks for full numerical iteration, but they do ask for **one step of gradient descent** to test your understanding of the formula. Practice this once to be safe!

---

### 3.5 Multiclass Extension: One-vs-All (OvA) and Softmax

For $K$ classes ($K > 2$), logistic regression generalizes in two ways:

**One-vs-All (OvA):**
- Train $K$ separate binary logistic regression models.
- The $k$-th model predicts $P(y = k \mid x; \theta_k)$.
- At prediction time, choose the class with the highest probability.

**Softmax Regression (Multinomial Logistic Regression):**
- Single unified model that handles all $K$ classes simultaneously.
- Hypothesis for class $k$:

$$
P(y = k \mid x; \theta) = \frac{e^{\theta_k^T x}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\theta_j^T x}}
$$

- The denominator normalizes the outputs to sum to 1 (a valid probability distribution).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logistic Regression End-to-End Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the **complete data and control flow** of a logistic regression system, from raw data ingestion to final prediction and evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData[Raw Dataset CSV] --> dataPreprocess[Data Preprocessing Module]
    dataPreprocess --> handleMissing[Handle Missing Values]
    dataPreprocess --> featureScale[Feature Scaling StandardScaler]
    dataPreprocess --> trainTestSplit[Train Test Split 80 20]
    handleMissing --> featureMerge[Merge Processed Features]
    featureScale --> featureMerge
    trainTestSplit --> featureMerge
    featureMerge --> trainX[Training Feature Matrix X]
    featureMerge --> testX[Testing Feature Matrix X Test]
    featureMerge --> trainY[Training Labels Vector Y]
    featureMerge --> testY[Testing Labels Vector Y Test]
    trainX --> initParams[Initialize Parameters Theta Zero Vector]
    initParams --> computeZ[Compute Linear Combination Z equals Theta Transpose X]
    computeZ --> applySigmoid[Apply Sigmoid Activation Function]
    applySigmoid --> predictProbs[Predicted Probabilities H Theta X]
    trainY --> computeLoss[Compute Binary Cross Entropy Loss]
    predictProbs --> computeLoss
    computeLoss --> checkConvergence{Convergence Check Loss Change less than Epsilon}
    checkConvergence -->|Not Converged| computeGradient[Compute Gradient of Loss w r t Theta]
    computeGradient --> updateParams[Update Parameters Theta equals Theta minus Alpha times Gradient]
    updateParams --> computeZ
    checkConvergence -->|Converged| finalModel[Trained Logistic Regression Model]
    finalModel --> testPredict[Predict on Test Set]
    testX --> testPredict
    testPredict --> testProbs[Test Probabilities]
    testProbs --> applyThreshold[Apply Classification Threshold 0.5]
    applyThreshold --> finalPreds[Final Class Predictions 0 or 1]
    testY --> evaluate[Evaluate Model Performance]
    finalPreds --> evaluate
    evaluate --> confMatrix[Confusion Matrix]
    evaluate --> accuracy[Accuracy Score]
    evaluate --> precision[Precision and Recall]
    evaluate --> f1Score[F1 Score]
    confMatrix --> report[Final Performance Report]
    accuracy --> report
    precision --> report
    f1Score --> report
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The inner loop (computeZ → applySigmoid → computeLoss → computeGradient → updateParams) represents the **iterative gradient descent process**. The outer flow represents the **complete machine learning pipeline**. The KTU board often asks students to identify and label such loops.

---

### 4.2 Decision Boundary Visualization Block

This diagram shows how logistic regression partitions the feature space into two regions. Although rendered as a flowchart (since true 2D plots cannot be drawn in Mermaid), the logic accurately reflects the geometric intuition.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputSpace[Input Feature Space R2] --> zCompute[Compute Z equals Theta Zero plus Theta One X1 plus Theta Two X2]
    zCompute --> boundaryCheck{Z greater than or equal to 0}
    boundaryCheck -->|Yes Region Above Boundary| classOne[Predict Class 1 Positive Class]
    boundaryCheck -->|No Region Below Boundary| classZero[Predict Class 0 Negative Class]
    classOne --> output1[Output Label 1 with Probability greater than 0.5]
    classZero --> output0[Output Label 0 with Probability less than 0.5]
```

---

### 4.3 Sigmoid Function and Its Derivative — Behavioral Schematic

This is a **behavioral description block** of how the sigmoid function and its derivative behave at critical points—a KTU favorite for short-answer questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    zNegLarge[Z approaches Negative Infinity] --> sigNeg[Sigmoid approaches 0]
    zNegMod[Z equals Negative 2] --> sigNegMod[Sigmoid equals 0.12]
    zZero[Z equals 0] --> sigHalf[Sigmoid equals 0.5 and Derivative equals 0.25 Maximum]
    zPosMod[Z equals Positive 2] --> sigPosMod[Sigmoid equals 0.88]
    zPosLarge[Z approaches Positive Infinity] --> sigOne[Sigmoid approaches 1]
    zZero --> derivMax[Derivative at Z equals 0 is Maximum Value 0.25]
    zNegLarge --> derivApproxZero[Derivative approaches 0]
    zPosLarge --> derivApproxZero
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The sigmoid derivative is **maximum at $z = 0$** (where it equals $0.25$) and **approaches zero** as $z \to \pm \infty$. This vanishing gradient behavior is a known limitation of sigmoid activations in deep networks.

---

### 4.4 Cost Function Behavior — Convexity Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    badTheta[Poorly Chosen Theta] --> highCost[High Cost Value]
    highCost --> descendStep1[Gradient Descent Step Updates Theta]
    descendStep1 --> midTheta[Improved Theta]
    midTheta --> lowerCost[Lower Cost Value]
    lowerCost --> descendStep2[Another Gradient Descent Step]
    descendStep2 --> optimalTheta[Optimal Theta at Global Minimum]
    optimalTheta --> minCost[Minimum Cost Achieved Convex Function Guarantees Global Optimum]
    badTheta -.-> compareMSE[Compared to MSE Cost Function which is Non Convex for Logistic Regression]
    compareMSE --> localMin[MSE Suffers from Multiple Local Minima Trap]
    optimalTheta -.-> logLossBest[Log Loss is Convex and Guarantees Global Minimum]
```
