## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define a **vector** and explain how a dataset with 500 customer records, each having 4 features (age, income, spending score, loyalty years), can be represented as a matrix. What is the dimension of this matrix?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **vector** is an ordered tuple of $n$ real numbers $\mathbf{x} = (x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n})^{\top}$ belonging to the $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. It represents a single data point with $n$ features.

For 500 customer records, each having 4 features, the dataset forms a matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{500 \times 4}$ where:
- Each **row** $i$ (for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, 500$) is a vector $\mathbf{x}_{i} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$ representing one customer.
- Each **column** $j$ (for $j = 1, 2, 3, 4$) is a vector of length 500 representing one feature across all customers.

Hence the **dimension of the matrix is $500 \times 4$** (m = 500 samples, n = 4 features).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **L¹ norm** and **L² norm** of a vector. State one practical scenario in data science where each norm is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | L¹ Norm | L² Norm |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Formula | $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_{1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_{i} \vert$ | $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_{2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}^{2}}$ |
| Geometric meaning | Manhattan / taxicab distance | Straight-line Euclidean distance |
| Sensitivity to outliers | More robust (uses absolute values) | More sensitive (squares amplify outliers) |
| Sparsity promotion | Strongly promotes sparse solutions | Does not promote sparsity |

- **L¹ norm preferred:** In **Lasso regression**, the L¹ penalty $\lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_{1}$ drives many feature weights to exactly zero, performing **automatic feature selection** in high-dimensional datasets.
- **L² norm preferred:** In **Ridge regression** and **k-NN classification**, the L² norm gives the standard Euclidean distance, which is geometrically natural and computationally efficient.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — For 14-mark questions, students **consistently lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Skipping the **mathematical setup** (e.g., not writing down the optimization objective before solving).
> 2. Forgetting to **normalize** eigenvectors — unnormalized eigenvectors are penalized.
> 3. Not **justifying** why eigenvectors of the covariance matrix yield principal components (the connection to Lagrangian / variance maximization must be stated).
> 4. Mixing up **row-major vs column-major** conventions when defining the data matrix.
> 5. **Omitting the final interpretation** — always close with a one-line meaning of the computed result.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — EIGEN-BASED PCA**

**(a)** *With suitable derivations, explain how eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the covariance matrix yield the **principal components** of a dataset. How is the optimal number of principal components chosen?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the data setting.**

Let the centered data matrix be $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ where each row is a centered $n$-dimensional sample. The sample covariance matrix is:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{m} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top} \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

$\mathbf{C}$ is real and symmetric, so it admits an orthogonal diagonalization.

**Step 2 — Set up the optimization problem.**

We seek a unit vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ ($\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{v} = 1$) that maximizes the variance of the projection of the data onto $\mathbf{v}$. The variance of the projected scalars is:

$$\text{Var} = \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v}$$

Hence the problem is:

$$\max_{\mathbf{v}} \, \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{v} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply Lagrange multipliers.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{v}, \lambda) = \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} - \lambda(\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - 1)$$

Setting the gradient to zero:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{v}} \mathcal{L} = 2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} - 2\lambda\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

The optimal direction $\mathbf{v}$ is an **eigenvector** of $\mathbf{C}$, and the variance preserved is the **eigenvalue** $\lambda$. The eigenvector corresponding to the **largest** eigenvalue is the **first principal component**; the second-largest gives the **second principal component**, and so on.

**Step 5 — Construct the projection matrix.**

Collect the top-$k$ eigenvectors into a matrix $\mathbf{V}_{k} = [\mathbf{v}_{1}, \mathbf{v}_{2}, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_{k}]$. The reduced representation of the data is:

$$\mathbf{Z}_{m \times k} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_{m \times n} \mathbf{V}_{k}$$

**Step 6 — Choose the number of components $k$.**

Compute the **explained variance ratio**:

$$\text{EVR}_{i} = \frac{\lambda_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_{j}}$$

The cumulative ratio is:

$$\text{Cumulative EVR}(k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_{j}}$$

**Standard criteria:**
- Choose the smallest $k$ such that $\text{Cumulative EVR}(k) \geq 0.95$ (95% rule).
- Plot the **scree plot** (eigenvalues vs index) and pick $k$ at the "elbow" where the curve flattens.

**Step 7 — Closing interpretation (mandatory for full marks).**

The principal components are **orthogonal directions of maximum variance**, and they form a new coordinate system aligned with the natural "axes of spread" in the data. Projecting onto the top $k$ components discards the directions of least variance, which typically correspond to noise.

---

**(b)** *Given the following centered 2D dataset with covariance matrix* $\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 3 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$, *find the eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and principal components. Verify orthogonality.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Form the characteristic equation.**

$$\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} 5 - \lambda & 3 \\ 3 & 4 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$(5 - \lambda)(4 - \lambda) - 9 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Expand the polynomial.**

$$20 - 5\lambda - 4\lambda + \lambda^{2} - 9 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2} - 9\lambda + 11 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve using the quadratic formula.**

$$\lambda = \frac{9 \pm \sqrt{81 - 44}}{2} = \frac{9 \pm \sqrt{37}}{2}$$

$$\lambda_{1} = \frac{9 + \sqrt{37}}{2} \approx \frac{9 + 6.083}{2} \approx 7.541$$

$$\lambda_{2} = \frac{9 - \sqrt{37}}{2} \approx \frac{9 - 6.083}{2} \approx 1.459$$

**Step 4 — Find the eigenvector for $\lambda_{1} \approx 7.541$.**

Solve $(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_{1}\mathbf{I})\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 5 - 7.541 & 3 \\ 3 & 4 - 7.541 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.541 & 3 \\ 3 & -3.541 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

From the first row: $-2.541\,v_{1} + 3\,v_{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{2} = 0.847\,v_{1}$.

Take $v_{1} = 1$, so $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.847 \end{bmatrix}$. The norm is $\sqrt{1 + 0.7174} = \sqrt{1.7174} \approx 1.3105$.

Normalized eigenvector:
$$\mathbf{v}_{1} = \frac{1}{1.3105} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.847 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.763 \\ 0.646 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Find the eigenvector for $\lambda_{2} \approx 1.459$.**

Solve $(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_{2}\mathbf{I})\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 5 - 1.459 & 3 \\ 3 & 4 - 1.459 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.541 & 3 \\ 3 & 2.541 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

From the first row: $3.541\,v_{1} + 3\,v_{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{2} = -1.1803\,v_{1}$.

Take $v_{1} = 1$, so $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1.1803 \end{bmatrix}$. The norm is $\sqrt{1 + 1.3931} = \sqrt{2.3931} \approx 1.5470$.

Normalized eigenvector:
$$\mathbf{v}_{2} = \frac{1}{1.5470} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1.1803 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.646 \\ -0.763 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Verify orthogonality.**

$$\mathbf{v}_{1}^{\top}\mathbf{v}_{2} = (0.763)(0.646) + (0.646)(-0.763) = 0.493 - 0.493 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Orthogonality is confirmed, consistent with the spectral theorem for symmetric matrices.

**Step 7 — State the principal components.**

The **first principal component** is along $\mathbf{v}_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.763 \\ 0.646 \end{bmatrix}$, preserving variance $\lambda_{1} \approx 7.541$ (which is $\frac{7.541}{9.000} \approx 83.8\%$ of total variance).

The **second principal component** is along $\mathbf{v}_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.646 \\ -0.763 \end{bmatrix}$, preserving variance $\lambda_{2} \approx 1.459$ (about 16.2% of total variance).

**Conclusion:** A 1-D projection onto $\mathbf{v}_{1}$ alone retains over 83% of the dataset's variance, justifying dimensionality reduction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — SVD-BASED ANALYSIS**

**(a)** *Explain the **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** of a matrix. State and prove any **two important properties** of SVD. Discuss its applications in data analysis.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of SVD.**

For any real matrix $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $\text{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = r$, there exist:
- An orthogonal matrix $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ (left singular vectors)
- An orthogonal matrix $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ (right singular vectors)
- A rectangular diagonal matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with non-negative diagonal entries $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r} > 0$

such that:

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

**Step 2 — Existence of SVD (sketch).**

Consider $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$, which is symmetric positive semi-definite. By the spectral theorem:

$$\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

with eigenvalues $\lambda_{i} \geq 0$. Define singular values $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}}$. Then for each right singular vector $\mathbf{v}_{i}$:

$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i} = \sigma_{i}\mathbf{u}_{i} \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{u}_{i} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{i}}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i}$$

completes the decomposition. ☐

**Step 3 — Property 1: Orthogonality of $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$.**

From $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$, we have $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{\top} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}\mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{\top}\mathbf{U}^{\top} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{\top}\mathbf{U}^{\top}$. Since $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{\top}$ is symmetric, the spectral theorem gives $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{U}^{\top} = \mathbf{I}_{m}$. Similarly, $\mathbf{V}\mathbf{V}^{\top} = \mathbf{I}_{n}$. Hence $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ are orthogonal. ☐

**Step 4 — Property 2: Rank-k Approximation is Optimal (Eckart–Young Theorem).**

The truncated SVD $\mathbf{A}_{k} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_{i}\mathbf{u}_{i}\mathbf{v}_{i}^{\top}$ is the **best rank-$k$ approximation** to $\mathbf{A}$ in both Frobenius and spectral norms:

$$\min_{\text{rank}(\mathbf{B}) = k} \Vert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B} \Vert_{F} = \Vert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \Vert_{F} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_{i}^{2}}$$

*Proof sketch:* Any rank-$k$ matrix $\mathbf{B}$ can be written as $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{U}_{B}\mathbf{\Sigma}_{B}\mathbf{V}_{B}^{\top}$ for some $k$-dimensional orthogonal column spaces. The orthogonal complement of $\mathbf{B}$'s column space in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ is at least $(n-k)$-dimensional. The squared Frobenius error $\Vert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B} \Vert_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_{i}^{2} - \text{terms retained}$ is minimized when $\mathbf{B}$ uses the $k$ largest singular values, yielding the formula above. ☐

**Step 5 — Applications in data analysis.**

| Application | How SVD is used |
|-------------|-----------------|
| **Image compression** | Replace $\mathbf{A}$ with $\mathbf{A}_{k}$; small singular values (high $i$) are often noise. |
| **Recommender systems** | Factorize user-item rating matrix $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$; low-rank $\mathbf{A}_{k}$ predicts missing ratings. |
| **Latent Semantic Analysis (NLP)** | TF-IDF document-term matrix → SVD reveals hidden "topics". |
| **Denoising** | Discarding small $\sigma_{i}$ removes noise; signal concentrates in top singular values. |
| **Pseudoinverse and least squares** | $\mathbf{A}^{+} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{+}\mathbf{U}^{\top}$ solves $\min \Vert \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}$ even when $\mathbf{A}$ is non-square. |

---

**(b)** *For the matrix* $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, *compute the Singular Value Decomposition. State the singular values and explain what the largest singular value represents geometrically.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$.**

$$\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 11 & 7 \\ 7 & 11 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Find eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$.**

$$\det(\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (11 - \lambda)^{2} - 49 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2} - 22\lambda + 72 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{22 \pm \sqrt{484 - 288}}{2} = \frac{22 \pm \sqrt{196}}{2} = \frac{22 \pm 14}{2}$$

$$\lambda_{1} = 18, \quad \lambda_{2} = 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the singular values.**

$$\sigma_{1} = \sqrt{18} = 3\sqrt{2} \approx 4.243, \quad \sigma_{2} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

**Step 4 — Find the right singular vectors (eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$).**

For $\lambda_{1} = 18$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} -7 & 7 \\ 7 & -7 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{1} = v_{2}$$

Normalized: $\mathbf{v}_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$

For $\lambda_{2} = 4$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 7 & 7 \\ 7 & 7 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{1} = -v_{2}$$

Normalized: $\mathbf{v}_{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ -0.707 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 5 — Find the left singular vectors.**

$\mathbf{u}_{i} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{i}} \mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i}$

For $\sigma_{1} = 3\sqrt{2}$:
$$\mathbf{u}_{1} = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 2.828 \\ 2.828 \\ 1.414 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.667 \\ 0.667 \\ 0.333 \end{bmatrix}$$

Verify: $\Vert \mathbf{u}_{1} \Vert = \sqrt{0.444 + 0.444 + 0.111} = \sqrt{0.999} \approx 1$ ✓

For $\sigma_{2} = 2$:
$$\mathbf{u}_{2} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ -0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 1.414 \\ -1.414 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ -0.707 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the SVD.**

$$\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.667 & 0.707 & \ast \\ 0.667 & -0.707 & \ast \\ 0.333 & 0 & \ast \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.243 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{V}^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0.707 \\ 0.707 & -0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$

(The third column of $\mathbf{U}$ completes the orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^{3}$.)

**Step 7 — Geometric interpretation of the largest singular value.**

The largest singular value $\sigma_{1} = 3\sqrt{2} \approx 4.243$ is the **maximum stretching factor** of the linear map $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$. It is the length of the longest semi-axis of the **ellipsoid** obtained by applying $\mathbf{A}$ to the unit circle. The corresponding right singular vector $\mathbf{v}_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1)^{\top}$ is the **direction in the input space** that is mapped to the longest output direction $\mathbf{u}_{1} = \frac{1}{3}(2, 2, 1)^{\top}$. The total "energy" of the matrix, $\sum \sigma_{i}^{2} = 18 + 4 = 22$, equals $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A})$, and $\sigma_{1}^{2}/22 \approx 81.8\%$ of the energy is captured by the first singular value — confirming that $\mathbf{A}$ is effectively low-rank.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist** — Use this the night before your KTU exam.

- [ ] **Vectors** are the atoms of data; every dataset row is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.
- [ ] **Matrices** are linear transformations; a dataset matrix $\mathbf{X}$ maps a basis of samples to features.
- [ ] **Matrix multiplication** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}$ is **not commutative**; always check dimensions: $(m \times n)(n \times p) \to (m \times p)$.
- [ ] The **inner product** $\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ equals $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert \cos\theta$ — memorize this geometric form.
- [ ] **L1 norm** $\to$ sparsity (Lasso); **L2 norm** $\to$ smoothness (Ridge, k-NN).
- [ ] The **eigenvalue equation** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ comes from setting $\det(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$ — derive, don't just quote.
- [ ] **Covariance matrix** $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{m}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ is symmetric, positive semi-definite, hence diagonalizable.
- [ ] **PCA** = eigenvectors of covariance matrix, sorted by eigenvalue magnitude.
- [ ] **SVD** works on **any** matrix (not just square, not just symmetric); always exists.
- [ ] **Eckart–Young theorem**: truncated SVD is the **best** low-rank approximation.
- [ ] **Cosine similarity** $\in [-1, 1]$; insensitive to vector magnitude — perfect for text/NLP.
- [ ] Always **normalize** eigenvectors to unit length before using them as principal components.
- [ ] The **rank** of a matrix equals the number of non-zero singular values.
- [ ] For an $n \times n$ matrix, you get exactly $n$ eigenvalues (counted with multiplicity).
- [ ] **Trace equals sum of eigenvalues**; **determinant equals product of eigenvalues** — both invaluable shortcuts.
- [ ] **Singular values are non-negative**; eigenvalues can be negative, complex.
- [ ] PCA preserves **global structure**; t-SNE preserves **local neighborhoods** (often confused in viva).
- [ ] Linear algebra is **not optional** in AI/DS — every module from here on uses it. Internalize it now.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matrix Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — MATRIX DECOMPOSITION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Matrix Decomposition** (also known as **Matrix Factorization**) is a fundamental technique in numerical linear algebra wherein a given matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is factorized into a product of two or more structured matrices whose forms reveal latent properties of $A$ such as its **rank**, **null space**, **spectral structure**, and **condition number**.

For a rectangular or square matrix $A$, decomposition generally takes the canonical form:

$$A = B \cdot C$$

where $B$ and $C$ belong to specialized matrix classes (e.g., orthogonal $Q$, triangular $L/U$, diagonal $\Lambda$, diagonal $\Sigma$). The choice of factorization class is dictated by the **downstream engineering task** — solving linear systems, computing eigenvalues, dimensionality reduction, or low-rank approximation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (GNEST305 – Module 2):**
> Matrix Decomposition is treated as a **bridging construct** between classical linear algebra and modern AI/ML. Students are expected to understand L U, Q R, Eigenvalue, SVD, and Cholesky decompositions, along with their application in **dimensionality reduction, recommendation engines, and Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cake Factory":**
> Imagine a complicated cake (the matrix $A$) that customers keep asking for. Instead of baking it from scratch every time, the bakery **decomposes** the recipe into three pre-made sub-recipes:
> 1. A **vanilla sponge base** (think of $L$ or $Q$ — a structured, easy-to-apply matrix),
> 2. A **buttercream layer** (think of $U$ or $R$ — another structured matrix),
> 3. A **set of flavorings and toppings** (think of $\Sigma$ or $\Lambda$ — a diagonal "weighting" matrix).
>
> Any cake can be recreated by combining these sub-recipes. The "magic" is that each sub-recipe has a **simpler mathematical structure** than the whole cake, making analysis, storage, and computation vastly cheaper. In data science, $A$ is the data, and decomposition splits data into "importance weights" + "principal directions" + "noise."

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Decompositions Used in AI & Data Science

| Decomposition | Canonical Form | When Used in AI / Data Science |
|---|---|---|
| **LU** | $A = L \cdot U$ | Solving linear regression, inverting matrices |
| **QR** | $A = Q \cdot R$ | Least-squares regression, orthogonalization |
| **Eigendecomposition** | $A = X \Lambda X^{-1}$ | PCA, spectral clustering, PageRank |
| **SVD** | $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ | Image compression, recommender systems, LSA |
| **Cholesky** | $A = L L^{T}$ | Gaussian processes, Kalman filters, covariance matrices |
| **NMF** | $A \approx W \cdot H$ | Topic modeling, feature extraction, audio source separation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constant / Metric:** The **rank** of a matrix, denoted $\text{rank}(A)$, equals the number of **non-zero singular values** in SVD. It is the single most important number in dimensionality reduction — it is the **intrinsic dimensionality** of the data.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Decompose? — The Engineering Rationale

A raw $m \times n$ data matrix is often **dense, ill-conditioned, redundant, and high-dimensional**. Decomposition addresses four core engineering pain points:

- **Computational efficiency:** Solving $A x = b$ costs $O(n^3)$ via Gaussian elimination, but only $O(n^2)$ after LU pre-factorization.
- **Numerical stability:** QR (using Householder reflections) avoids the catastrophic cancellation that plagues $L U$ for ill-conditioned $A$.
- **Interpretability:** SVD separates data into **signal directions** (large $\sigma_i$) and **noise directions** (small $\sigma_i$).
- **Storage:** A rank-$k$ SVD approximation of an $m \times n$ matrix requires only $k(m + n + 1)$ scalars instead of $mn$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 LU Decomposition (Lower–Upper Factorization)
- **Why?** Convert the expensive $A x = b$ solve into two cheap **forward** and **back substitution** steps on triangular systems.
- **How?** Apply Gaussian elimination while recording the multipliers in a lower-triangular matrix $L$. The result of elimination is an upper-triangular $U$.
- **Existence Condition:** $A$ must be a square, non-singular matrix whose leading principal minors are non-zero (i.e., no row pivoting needed). If pivoting is needed, we get $P A = L U$.

### 2.2.2 QR Decomposition
- **Why?** Provides an **orthonormal basis** for the column space of $A$, enabling stable least-squares solves.
- **How?** Three equivalent methods:
  1. **Gram–Schmidt Orthogonalization** (classical, unstable)
  2. **Modified Gram–Schmidt** (numerically improved)
  3. **Householder Reflections** (production-grade, used in NumPy/MATLAB)
  4. **Givens Rotations** (sparse-matrix friendly)

### 2.2.3 Eigenvalue Decomposition
- **Why?** A square matrix $A$ can be **diagonalized** in its eigenbasis, making power iterations, spectral analysis, and PCA trivial.
- **How?** Solve $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ for eigenvalues $\lambda_i$, then solve $(A - \lambda_i I) v_i = 0$ for eigenvectors $v_i$.
- **Restriction:** $A$ must be **diagonalizable**; for symmetric $A$, the Spectral Theorem guarantees $A = Q \Lambda Q^{T}$ with orthogonal $Q$.

### 2.2.4 Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
- **Why?** The most **general and powerful** decomposition — works for *any* $m \times n$ matrix, rectangular or square, singular or non-singular.
- **How?** Compute eigenvectors of $A A^{T}$ and $A^{T} A$, then take square roots to obtain singular values.
- **Key Property:** The **Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem** states that the truncated SVD is the **best low-rank approximation** in both Frobenius and spectral norms.

### 2.2.5 Cholesky Decomposition
- **Why?** Roughly **2× faster** than LU by exploiting symmetry and positive-definiteness — critical in optimization where covariance matrices appear.
- **How?** Recursively compute $L$ such that $A = L L^{T}$.
- **Requirement:** $A$ must be **symmetric positive definite (SPD)**, i.e., $x^{T} A x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Decomposition | Formula | Conditions on $A$ | Complexity | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LU | $A = L U$ | Square, non-singular, leading minors $\neq 0$ | $O(n^3 / 3)$ | Moderate (worsens with pivoting) |
| 2 | Pivoted LU | $P A = L U$ | Any non-singular square | $O(n^3 / 3)$ | High |
| 3 | QR (Householder) | $A = Q R$ | Any $m \ge n$, full column rank | $O(2 m n^2)$ | Very High |
| 4 | Eigenvalue | $A = X \Lambda X^{-1}$ | Diagonalizable, $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors | $O(n^3)$ | Algorithm-dependent |
| 5 | Symmetric Eigendecomp | $A = Q \Lambda Q^{T}$ | $A$ real symmetric | $O(4 n^3 / 3)$ | High |
| 6 | SVD | $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$ | **Any** $m \times n$ matrix | $O(m n^2)$ | Very High |
| 7 | Cholesky | $A = L L^{T}$ | Symmetric, positive-definite | $O(n^3 / 6)$ | Very High |
| 8 | Truncated SVD | $A \approx U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{T}$ | Rank $k \le \min(m,n)$ | Storage: $k(m+n+1)$ | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Symbols:**
> - $L$ — Lower triangular matrix (entries $\ell_{ij} = 0$ for $i < j$)
> - $U$ — Upper triangular matrix (entries $u_{ij} = 0$ for $i > j$)
> - $Q$ — Orthogonal matrix ($Q^{T} Q = I$)
> - $R$ — Upper triangular with $r_{ii} > 0$
> - $\Sigma$ — Diagonal with non-negative entries (singular values) sorted $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \dots \ge 0$
> - $\Lambda$ — Diagonal matrix of eigenvalues
> - $P$ — Permutation matrix (for pivoting)

## 2.4 Real-World AI / Engineering Applications

| Decomposition | Industry Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **LU** | Real-time linear regression in embedded systems, circuit simulation (SPICE) |
| **QR** | Stock-portfolio optimization, adaptive beamforming in 5G antennas |
| **Eigendecomp** | Google's **PageRank** algorithm, **vibrational analysis** in mechanical engineering |
| **SVD** | Netflix / Amazon **recommendation systems**, **facial recognition** (Eigenfaces), **image compression** |
| **Cholesky** | **Gaussian Processes** in Bayesian ML, **Monte Carlo** financial simulations, **Kalman filters** in robotics |
| **NMF** | **Topic modeling** (LDA alternatives), **music transcription**, **hyperspectral unmixing** |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 LU Decomposition — Full Derivation (Doolittle's Form)

**Given:** A square, non-singular matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

**Goal:** Find lower-triangular $L$ (with 1s on the diagonal — Doolittle) and upper-triangular $U$ such that $A = L U$.

Let
$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ \ell_{21} & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \ell_{n1} & \ell_{n2} & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad U = \begin{bmatrix} u_{11} & u_{12} & \cdots & u_{1n} \\ 0 & u_{22} & \cdots & u_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & u_{nn} \end{bmatrix}$$

The product $A = L U$ is computed element-by-element:

For $i \le j$ (upper triangle of $A$):
$$a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik} u_{kj} + u_{ij} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_{ij} = a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik} u_{kj}$$

For $i > j$ (strict lower triangle of $A$):
$$a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik} u_{kj} + \ell_{ij} u_{jj} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ell_{ij} = \frac{a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik} u_{kj}}{u_{jj}}$$

### Worked Example: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 4 & 7 & 7 \\ 6 & 18 & 22 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 1 — Compute first row of $U$:** Use $u_{1j} = a_{1j} - 0 = a_{1j}$ (the sum is empty since upper bound is 0).

$$u_{11} = 2, \quad u_{12} = 3, \quad u_{13} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Compute first column of $L$:** $\ell_{i1} = a_{i1} / u_{11}$.

$$\ell_{21} = \frac{4}{2} = 2, \quad \ell_{31} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute $u_{22}$:**

$$u_{22} = a_{22} - \ell_{21} u_{12} = 7 - (2)(3) = 1$$

**Step 4 — Compute $u_{23}$:**

$$u_{23} = a_{23} - \ell_{21} u_{13} = 7 - (2)(1) = 5$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\ell_{32}$:**

$$\ell_{32} = \frac{a_{32} - \ell_{31} u_{12}}{u_{22}} = \frac{18 - (3)(3)}{1} = \frac{9}{1} = 9$$

**Step 6 — Compute $u_{33}$:**

$$u_{33} = a_{33} - \ell_{31} u_{13} - \ell_{32} u_{23} = 22 - (3)(1) - (9)(5) = 22 - 3 - 45 = -26$$

**Final result:**

$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 3 & 9 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad U = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & -26 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:**

$$L \cdot U = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 2 & 1 \cdot 3 + 0 & 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \\ 2 \cdot 2 & 2 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 1 & 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 5 \\ 3 \cdot 2 & 3 \cdot 3 + 9 \cdot 1 & 3 \cdot 1 + 9 \cdot 5 + 1 \cdot (-26) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 4 & 7 & 7 \\ 6 & 18 & 22 \end{bmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 QR Decomposition — Full Derivation (Gram–Schmidt Method)

**Given:** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $m \ge n$ and full column rank.

**Goal:** $A = Q R$ where $Q$ has orthonormal columns and $R$ is upper-triangular.

Let the columns of $A$ be $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$. Apply Gram–Schmidt:

**Step 1 — First orthogonal vector:** $u_1 = a_1$.

**Step 2 — Normalize:** $q_1 = u_1 / \vert\vert u_1 \vert\vert$, where $\vert\vert u_1 \vert\vert = \sqrt{u_1^{T} u_1}$.

**Step 3 — Project $a_2$ onto $q_1$ and subtract:**

$$u_2 = a_2 - (q_1^{T} a_2) q_1$$

The scalar $r_{12} = q_1^{T} a_2$ is the projection coefficient.

**Step 4 — Normalize:** $q_2 = u_2 / \vert\vert u_2 \vert\vert$.

**Step 5 — Generalize for the $k$-th column:**

$$u_k = a_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} (q_j^{T} a_k) q_j$$

$$q_k = \frac{u_k}{\vert\vert u_k \vert\vert}, \qquad r_{jk} = q_j^{T} a_k \;\; \text{for} \; j < k, \quad r_{kk} = \vert\vert u_k \vert\vert$$

### Worked Example: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 1:** $u_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\vert\vert u_1 \vert\vert = \sqrt{1+0+1} = \sqrt{2}$.

$$q_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** $r_{11} = \sqrt{2}$, $r_{12} = q_1^{T} a_2 = (0.707)(1) + (0)(1) + (0.707)(0) = 0.707$.

$$u_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - 0.707 \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.5 \\ 1 - 0 \\ 0 - 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** $\vert\vert u_2 \vert\vert = \sqrt{0.25 + 1 + 0.25} = \sqrt{1.5} \approx 1.225$.

$$q_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.5}} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.408 \\ 0.816 \\ -0.408 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final result:**

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0.408 \\ 0 & 0.816 \\ 0.707 & -0.408 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = \begin{bmatrix} 1.414 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 1.225 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification of orthogonality:** $Q^{T} Q = I$ (rounded to 4 decimals):

$$Q^{T} Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.500 + 0 + 0.500 & 0.289 + 0 - 0.289 \\ 0.289 + 0 - 0.289 & 0.166 + 0.666 + 0.166 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 0.000 \\ 0.000 & 0.998 \end{bmatrix} \approx I \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Eigendecomposition — Full Derivation

**Given:** A diagonalizable matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:** Solve $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$.

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = (a-\lambda)(d-\lambda) - bc = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + (ad - bc) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve quadratic:**

$$\lambda = \frac{(a+d) \pm \sqrt{(a+d)^2 - 4(ad-bc)}}{2} = \frac{\text{tr}(A) \pm \sqrt{\text{tr}(A)^2 - 4 \det(A)}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — For each eigenvalue, find eigenvector** $v$ from $(A - \lambda_i I) v = 0$.

### Worked Example: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 1 — Characteristic polynomial:**

$$\lambda^2 - (4+3)\lambda + (12 - 2) = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0$$

$$(\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 5, \quad \lambda_2 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 5$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 - 5 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 5 \end{bmatrix} v = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -v_1 + v_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = v_2$$

$$v^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 - 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 2 \end{bmatrix} v = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 v_1 + v_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_2 = -2 v_1$$

$$v^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Form the decomposition:**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad X^{-1} = \frac{1}{-3} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 & 1/3 \\ 1/3 & -1/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** $X \Lambda X^{-1}$:

$$X \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 5 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$X \Lambda X^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 5 & -4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 & 1/3 \\ 1/3 & -1/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/3 + 2/3 & 5/3 - 2/3 \\ 10/3 - 4/3 & 5/3 + 4/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.4 SVD — Complete Construction

**Given:** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $A^{T} A$** (an $n \times n$ symmetric positive semi-definite matrix).

**Step 2 — Eigendecompose:** $A^{T} A = V \Lambda V^{T}$, where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n)$ with $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_n \ge 0$.

**Step 3 — Singular values:** $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$ for $i = 1, \dots, r$ where $r = \text{rank}(A)$.

**Step 4 — Right singular vectors:** $V = [v_1 \; v_2 \; \dots \; v_n]$ from eigenvectors of $A^{T} A$.

**Step 5 — Left singular vectors:** For each $i = 1, \dots, r$:

$$u_i = \frac{A v_i}{\sigma_i}$$

**Step 6 — Complete the basis:** Extend $\{u_1, \dots, u_r\}$ to an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^m$ (e.g., via Gram–Schmidt).

**Step 7 — Assemble:**

$$A = U \Sigma V^{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T}$$

### Worked Example: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ (already diagonal, but instructive)

**Step 1:** $A^{T} A = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2:** Eigenvalues of $A^{T} A$: $\lambda_1 = 9, \lambda_2 = 4$.

**Step 3:** Singular values: $\sigma_1 = 3, \sigma_2 = 2$.

**Step 4:** $V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 5:** $u_1 = A v_1 / 3 = (1/3) \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $u_2 = A v_2 / 2 = (1/2) \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Final result:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrices $U$ and $V$ are **orthogonal** ($U^{T} U = I$, $V^{T} V = I$), and $\Sigma$ is a **diagonal** matrix with non-negative entries sorted in descending order. This is the **canonical** form of SVD that is unique up to sign flips.

## 3.5 Cholesky Decomposition — Algorithmic Derivation

**Given:** $A$ symmetric positive definite (SPD).

**Goal:** Find lower-triangular $L$ with positive diagonal such that $A = L L^{T}$.

**Entry-wise formulation:**
For $j = 1, \dots, n$:

$$\ell_{jj} = \sqrt{a_{jj} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{jk}^2}$$

For $i = j+1, \dots, n$:

$$\ell_{ij} = \frac{a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik} \ell_{jk}}{\ell_{jj}}$$

### Worked Example: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 12 & -16 \\ 12 & 37 & -43 \\ -16 & -43 & 98 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 1 — $j = 1$:** $\ell_{11} = \sqrt{4} = 2$.

$\ell_{21} = 12/2 = 6$, $\ell_{31} = -16/2 = -8$.

**Step 2 — $j = 2$:** $\ell_{22} = \sqrt{37 - 6^2} = \sqrt{37 - 36} = 1$.

$\ell_{32} = (-43 - (-8)(6))/1 = (-43 + 48)/1 = 5$.

**Step 3 — $j = 3$:** $\ell_{33} = \sqrt{98 - (-8)^2 - 5^2} = \sqrt{98 - 64 - 25} = \sqrt{9} = 3$.

**Final result:**

$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 6 & 1 & 0 \\ -8 & 5 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** $L L^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 12 & -16 \\ 12 & 37 & -43 \\ -16 & -43 & 98 \end{bmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Matrix Decompositions (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Matrix A in R power m x n] --> B{Is A Symmetric PD?}
    A --> C{Is A Square?}
    A --> D{Is A Rectangular?}
    A --> E{Is A Diagonalizable?}

    B -- Yes --> F[Cholesky: A = L L transpose]
    B -- No --> G[Use LDL transpose or LU]

    C -- Yes --> H[LU Decomposition: A = L U]
    C -- No --> D

    D -- Yes --> I[QR: A = Q R]
    D -- Yes --> J[SVD: A = U Sigma V transpose]

    E -- Yes --> K[Eigendecomp: A = X Lambda X inverse]
    E -- No --> L[Jordan Form]

    F --> M[Used in: Kalman, GP, Monte Carlo]
    H --> N[Used in: Linear Systems, Regression]
    I --> O[Used in: Least Squares, 5G Beamforming]
    J --> P[Used in: PCA, Recommender, Compression]
    K --> Q[Used in: PageRank, Spectral Clustering]
```

## 4.2 SVD Geometric Interpretation (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Original Data Matrix A m by n] --> R1[Step 1: Rotate via V transpose]
    R1 --> S1[Step 2: Scale by Sigma along axes]
    S1 --> R2[Step 3: Rotate via U]
    R2 --> A1[Reconstructed A hat]

    V0[V columns: Right singular vectors] --> R1
    S0[Sigma: Non-negative scaling factors] --> S1
    U0[U columns: Left singular vectors] --> R2

    A1 --> K[Truncation: Keep top k singular values]
    K --> LR[Low-rank Approximation A k]
    LR --> APP[Applications: Denoising, Compression, Recommendation]
```

## 4.3 SVD Truncation & Dimensionality Reduction

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FULL[Full SVD: rank r]
        D1[Sigma 1] --> D2[Sigma 2] --> D3[Sigma 3] --> D4[Sigma r]
    end

    FULL --> TRUNC{Truncation Threshold k less than r}
    TRUNC -- Keep top k --> KEEP[Sigma 1 to Sigma k retained]
    TRUNC -- Discard --> DROP[Sigma k plus 1 to Sigma r zeroed]

    KEEP --> OUT[Compressed Representation: k left singular vectors, k right singular vectors, k singular values]
    DROP --> OUT

    OUT --> USES[Used in: Latent Semantic Analysis, Image Compression, PCA]
```

## 4.4 Decomposition Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to Decompose Matrix A] --> Q1{Goal?}
    Q1 -- Solve Ax=b --> Q2{A SPD?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CH[Cholesky: 2x faster]
    Q2 -- No --> LU[LU with Pivoting]
    Q1 -- Least Squares --> QR[QR via Householder]
    Q1 -- Find Spectrum --> EIG[Eigendecomposition]
    Q1 -- Reduce Dimensions --> SVD[Truncated SVD]
    Q1 -- Factor Non-negative Data --> NMF[Non-negative Matrix Factorization]

    CH --> APP1[Gaussian Processes, Kalman]
    LU --> APP2[Direct Solvers, SPICE]
    QR --> APP3[Regression, Adaptive Filters]
    EIG --> APP4[PCA, PageRank]
    SVD --> APP5[Recommender, Image Compression]
    NMF --> APP6[Topic Modeling, Source Separation]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). Explain its significance in dimensionality reduction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is a matrix factorization technique that decomposes any $m \times n$ matrix $A$ into the product of three matrices:

$$A = U \Sigma V^{T}$$

where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are orthogonal matrices (containing left and right singular vectors respectively), and $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is a diagonal matrix containing the non-negative singular values $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \dots \ge \sigma_r \ge 0$ where $r = \text{rank}(A)$.

**Significance in Dimensionality Reduction (2 marks):** By the **Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem**, the best rank-$k$ approximation of $A$ in the Frobenius norm is given by the truncated SVD:

$$A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T} = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{T}$$

The singular values $\sigma_i$ measure the **amount of variance** (or "energy") along each direction. Keeping only the top-$k$ singular values yields a compressed representation that retains the most important structure of the data while discarding noise. This is the mathematical foundation of **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**, **image compression**, and **Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)** used in search engines.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between LU decomposition and QR decomposition. State one application of each in data science.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | LU Decomposition | QR Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Form | $A = L U$ (lower & upper triangular) | $A = Q R$ (orthogonal & upper triangular) |
| Existence | Requires non-singular square matrix with non-zero leading minors | Any matrix with full column rank |
| Stability | Moderate (worsens without pivoting) | Very high (orthogonal transformations preserve norms) |
| Computational Cost | $O(n^3 / 3)$ | $O(2 m n^2)$ |

**LU Application (1 mark):** Used in solving linear regression problems efficiently by pre-factorizing the normal equations matrix $X^{T} X$ (or in direct solvers for $A x = b$ in numerical simulations).

**QR Application (1 mark):** Used in numerically stable **least-squares regression** where the system $A x = b$ is overdetermined ($m > n$); the QR factorization provides the solution $x = R^{-1} Q^{T} b$ without forming the ill-conditioned $A^{T} A$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compute the LU decomposition of the matrix
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & -6 & 0 \\ -2 & 7 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
Use it to solve the system $A x = b$ where $b = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ -2 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Find the Singular Value Decomposition of the matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ and verify the result.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Doolittle's LU Decomposition:**

We compute $L$ (with unit diagonal) and $U$ (upper triangular) such that $A = L U$.

**Row 1 of U (no computation needed as i = 1):**
$$u_{11} = 2, \quad u_{12} = 1, \quad u_{13} = 1$$

**Column 1 of L (entries below diagonal):**
$$\ell_{21} = \frac{4}{2} = 2, \quad \ell_{31} = \frac{-2}{2} = -1$$

**Row 2 of U:**
$$u_{22} = a_{22} - \ell_{21} u_{12} = -6 - (2)(1) = -8$$
$$u_{23} = a_{23} - \ell_{21} u_{13} = 0 - (2)(1) = -2$$

**Column 2 of L (entry $\ell_{32}$):**
$$\ell_{32} = \frac{a_{32} - \ell_{31} u_{12}}{u_{22}} = \frac{7 - (-1)(1)}{-8} = \frac{8}{-8} = -1$$

**Row 3 of U:**
$$u_{33} = a_{33} - \ell_{31} u_{13} - \ell_{32} u_{23} = 2 - (-1)(1) - (-1)(-2) = 2 + 1 - 2 = 1$$

**Resulting LU:**
$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad U = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -8 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Forward substitution $L y = b$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ y_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ -2 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

$y_1 = 5$.

$2 y_1 + y_2 = -2 \;\Rightarrow\; 10 + y_2 = -2 \;\Rightarrow\; y_2 = -12$.

$-y_1 - y_2 + y_3 = 9 \;\Rightarrow\; -5 + 12 + y_3 = 9 \;\Rightarrow\; y_3 = 2$.

**Step 3 — Back substitution $U x = y$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -8 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ -12 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$x_3 = 2$.

$-8 x_2 - 2 x_3 = -12 \;\Rightarrow\; -8 x_2 - 4 = -12 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 1$.

$2 x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; 2 x_1 + 1 + 2 = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 1$.

**Final solution:** $x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Verification:** $A x = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & -6 & 0 \\ -2 & 7 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2+1+2 \\ 4-6+0 \\ -2+7+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ -2 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix} = b \quad \checkmark$

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$, a diagonal matrix.

**Step 1 — Compute $A^{T} A$:**
$$A^{T} A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 0 \\ 0 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvalues of $A^{T} A$:** $\lambda_1 = 9, \lambda_2 = 16$.

**Step 3 — Singular values:** $\sigma_1 = \sqrt{9} = 3$, $\sigma_2 = \sqrt{16} = 4$.

**Step 4 — Right singular vectors $V$:** Eigenvectors of $A^{T} A$ for $\lambda_1 = 9$: $v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$. For $\lambda_2 = 16$: $v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

$$V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Left singular vectors $U$:**

$u_1 = \frac{A v_1}{\sigma_1} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$

$u_2 = \frac{A v_2}{\sigma_2} = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$

$$U = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Form $\Sigma$ (sorted in descending order: $\sigma_1 = 4, \sigma_2 = 3$):**

$$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Note:** We must reorder $u_1, u_2$ accordingly so that $\sigma$ values are sorted descending.

**Final SVD:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:**

$$\Sigma V^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$U (\Sigma V^{T}) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is **not** equal to $A$! The issue is the sorting — we must **swap** $u_1 \leftrightarrow u_2$ and $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$ to keep $\sigma$ in descending order:

$$U = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Re-verification:**

$$U \Sigma V^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
Hence write the eigendecomposition $A = X \Lambda X^{-1}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compute the QR decomposition of the matrix
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
using the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization method.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det\begin{bmatrix} 5 - \lambda & 4 \\ 1 & 2 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = (5 - \lambda)(2 - \lambda) - 4 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 - 4 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6 = 0$$

$$(\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 6) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 1, \quad \lambda_2 = 6$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 1$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 5 - 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 - 1 \end{bmatrix} v = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4 v_1 + 4 v_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = -v_2$$

$$v^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 6$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 5 - 6 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 - 6 \end{bmatrix} v = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & -4 \end{bmatrix} v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -v_1 + 4 v_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = 4 v_2$$

$$v^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Form $X$ and $\Lambda$:**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $X^{-1}$:** $\det(X) = (-1)(1) - (4)(1) = -1 - 4 = -5$.

$$X^{-1} = \frac{1}{-5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -4 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/5 & 4/5 \\ 1/5 & 1/5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Eigendecomposition:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1/5 & 4/5 \\ 1/5 & 1/5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** $X \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 24 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$, then $X \Lambda X^{-1}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 24 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1/5 & 4/5 \\ 1/5 & 1/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 + 24/5 & -4/5 + 24/5 \\ -1/5 + 6/5 & 4/5 + 6/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 25/5 & 20/5 \\ 5/5 & 10/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ with columns $a_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and $a_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 1 — Orthogonalize $a_1$:** $u_1 = a_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

$\vert\vert u_1 \vert\vert = \sqrt{1+1+0} = \sqrt{2}$.

$q_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

$r_{11} = \sqrt{2}$.

**Step 2 — Project $a_2$ onto $q_1$ and subtract:**

$r_{12} = q_1^{T} a_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + 2 + 0) = \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$u_2 = a_2 - r_{12} q_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 3/2 \\ 3/2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 \\ 1/2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 3 — Normalize $u_2$:**

$\vert\vert u_2 \vert\vert = \sqrt{1/4 + 1/4 + 1} = \sqrt{3/2} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}$.

$q_2 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 \\ 1/2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/\sqrt{6} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} \\ 2/\sqrt{6} \end{bmatrix}$.

$r_{22} = \vert\vert u_2 \vert\vert = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}$.

**Final Result:**

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{6} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{6} \\ 0 & 2/\sqrt{6} \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{2} & 3/\sqrt{2} \\ 0 & \sqrt{6}/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification ($A = Q R$):**

$$Q R = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{6} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{6} \\ 0 & 2/\sqrt{6} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{2} & 3/\sqrt{2} \\ 0 & \sqrt{6}/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3/2 - 1/2 \\ 1 & 3/2 + 1/2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification ($Q^{T} Q = I$):** Already satisfied since $q_1, q_2$ are orthonormal by Gram–Schmidt construction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (5 marks lost here):**
> 1. **Forgetting the diagonal in Cholesky's square root:** When computing $\ell_{jj}$, students often drop the square root. Half-mark loss each time.
> 2. **SVD sign-flip trap:** Singular vectors are determined only up to a sign. Students must **fix the sign** consistently using $u_i = A v_i / \sigma_i$, not arbitrarily.
> 3. **LU — $u_{jj}$ becomes zero:** If any $u_{jj} = 0$, Doolittle's method fails. Students must either pivot (use $P A = L U$) or switch to QR. The examiner deducts 2 marks if this is ignored.
> 4. **Eigendecomposition requires diagonalizability:** Not every matrix has $n$ independent eigenvectors. Always check $\text{rank}(A - \lambda_i I) = n - 1$ for each eigenvalue.
> 5. **For 14-mark questions, missing the verification step costs 1–2 marks.** Always verify by reconstructing the original matrix.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2: Matrix Decomposition):**

- **LU Decomposition ($A = L U$):** Requires square, non-singular matrix. Solves $A x = b$ in two triangular passes. Doolittle has unit diagonal in $L$; Crout has unit diagonal in $U$. **Use partial pivoting** for stability.
- **QR Decomposition ($A = Q R$):** $Q$ orthogonal, $R$ upper triangular. Best computed via **Householder reflections** (stable) or Modified Gram–Schmidt. **Use for least-squares** in regression.
- **Eigendecomposition ($A = X \Lambda X^{-1}$):** Requires diagonalizability. **Symmetric matrices** always have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. **Use for PCA** and spectral analysis.
- **SVD ($A = U \Sigma V^{T}$):** Most **general** decomposition. Works for any $m \times n$ matrix. **Eckart–Young theorem** guarantees best low-rank approximation. **Use for recommendation systems, image compression, and LSA.**
- **Cholesky ($A = L L^{T}$):** Requires **symmetric positive-definite** $A$. **Twice as fast as LU**. **Use for covariance matrices, Gaussian processes, and Kalman filters.**
- **NMF ($A \approx W H$):** Both factors are non-negative. **Use for topic modeling, source separation, and parts-based representations.**
- **Truncated SVD:** Drop the smallest singular values to obtain a **rank-$k$ approximation** $A_k$ with **minimum reconstruction error** in Frobenius norm.
- **Condition Number:** $\kappa(A) = \sigma_{\max}(A) / \sigma_{\min}(A)$. High $\kappa$ means ill-conditioned — use QR or SVD instead of LU.
- **Storage Trick:** A rank-$k$ SVD of an $m \times n$ matrix needs only $k(m + n + 1)$ scalars, vs. $mn$ for the full matrix — **massive savings** for sparse or low-rank data.
- **Key Constants / Metrics:** Rank $r$, condition number $\kappa$, Frobenius norm $\vert\vert A \vert\vert_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i,j} a_{ij}^2} = \sqrt{\sum_i \sigma_i^2}$, spectral norm $\vert\vert A \vert\vert_2 = \sigma_{\max}$.
- **AI/ML Hot Uses:** PCA (eigendecomp on covariance), Recommender systems (SVD on user-item matrix), Face recognition (eigenfaces = eigenvectors of face-image covariance), NLP (LSA via truncated SVD on term-document matrix).
- **Common Mistake:** Treating **SVD** and **eigendecomposition** as the same. They are equal **only** when $A$ is symmetric positive semi-definite, in which case $U = V$ and $\sigma_i = \lambda_i$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) Theorem:** For *any* real or complex $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ of rank $r$, there exists a factorization of the form
> $$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$$
> where $\mathbf{U}$ is an $m \times m$ orthogonal (unitary) matrix, $\mathbf{V}$ is an $n \times n$ orthogonal matrix, and $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is an $m \times n$ rectangular diagonal matrix with non-negative real numbers $\sigma_{1} \ge \sigma_{2} \ge \dots \ge \sigma_{r} > 0$ on the diagonal (the **singular values**), with the remaining diagonal entries being zero.

### 1.1 The Three Component Matrices

| Matrix | Shape | Called | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{U}$ | $m \times m$ | Left Singular Vectors | Column space basis of $\mathbf{A}$ |
| $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ | $m \times n$ | Singular Value Matrix | Diagonal matrix of "stretching" magnitudes |
| $\mathbf{V}^{T}$ | $n \times n$ | Right Singular Vectors (transposed) | Row space basis of $\mathbf{A}$ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Lens Camera"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are photographing a 3D object with a strange camera that has **three sequential lenses**:
> 1. The **first lens** ($\mathbf{V}^{T}$) **rotates** the object so that its most important direction faces the camera (a *change of basis* / coordinate rotation).
> 2. The **second lens** ($\mathbf{\Sigma}$) **stretches** or **squashes** the image along each axis — it does no rotation, only pure scaling. The amount of stretch on each axis is a *singular value*.
> 3. The **third lens** ($\mathbf{U}$) **rotates** the result back into the standard camera frame.
>
> Net effect: every linear transformation is just **rotate → scale → rotate**. SVD tells you exactly what those rotations and scales are.

### 1.3 Why Every Matrix Has an SVD

Unlike eigendecomposition (which requires square matrices), SVD works for **any** $m \times n$ matrix — tall, wide, square, singular, or non-singular. This universality makes it the **Swiss Army knife of linear algebra** and a cornerstone of Data Science.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, SVD is treated as the engine behind **dimensionality reduction (truncated SVD / PCA)**, **recommender systems**, **image compression**, and **latent semantic analysis (LSA)** in NLP pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Geometry Behind SVD

Consider a unit sphere in $\mathbb{R}^n$ mapped by $\mathbf{A}$ to an ellipsoid in $\mathbb{R}^m$.
- The **semi-axes** of this ellipsoid have lengths equal to the singular values $\sigma_{i}$.
- The **directions** of these axes in the input space are the right singular vectors (columns of $\mathbf{V}$).
- The **directions** in the output space are the left singular vectors (columns of $\mathbf{U}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rank Condition:** If $\text{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = r$, then exactly $r$ singular values are strictly positive. The remaining $m - r$ (or $n - r$) singular values are zero, and the corresponding columns of $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ are arbitrary orthonormal completions.

### 2.2 Existence & Uniqueness

- **Existence:** Always guaranteed for any real or complex matrix (proved via the spectral theorem applied to $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$).
- **Uniqueness:** The singular values $\sigma_{i}$ are unique. The singular vectors are unique **only if all singular values are distinct**. If there are repeated singular values, the corresponding singular vectors form an arbitrary orthonormal basis of that subspace.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your **one-page revision anchor** before the exam. All formulas below are tested directly in KTU End Semester Evaluations (ESE).

| # | Identity / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$ | The SVD factorization itself | $\mathbf{U}, \mathbf{V}$ orthogonal; $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ diagonal |
| 2 | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{2}\mathbf{V}^{T}$ | Right singular vectors are eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric PSD |
| 3 | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{T}\mathbf{U}^{T} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{2}\mathbf{U}^{T}$ | Left singular vectors are eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ is symmetric PSD |
| 4 | $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}\left(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\right)} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}\left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}\right)}$ | Singular values = sqrt of eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ or $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ | Always $\sigma_{i} \ge 0$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{u}_{i} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{i}}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i}$ for $\sigma_{i} \neq 0$ | Recovers left singular vectors once $\mathbf{v}_{i}$ and $\sigma_{i}$ are known | Numerical recipe |
| 6 | $\mathbf{A}_{k} = \mathbf{U}_{k}\mathbf{\Sigma}_{k}\mathbf{V}_{k}^{T}$ | **Truncated SVD** (rank-$k$ approximation) | Eckart–Young theorem: best rank-$k$ approx. |
| 7 | $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{F} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r}\sigma_{i}^{2}}$ | Frobenius-norm error of truncation | $\lVert \cdot \rVert_{F}$ |
| 8 | $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{2} = \sigma_{k+1}$ | Spectral (operator) norm error of truncation | $\lVert \cdot \rVert_{2}$ |
| 9 | $\text{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = $ number of nonzero $\sigma_{i}$ | Rank via singular values | Strict, exact |
| 10 | $\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{U}^{T}$ | Pseudoinverse (Moore–Penrose) for square invertible $\mathbf{A}$ | Generalized for non-square via $\mathbf{A}^{+}$ |
| 11 | $\lVert \mathbf{A} \rVert_{2} = \sigma_{1}$ | Operator norm = largest singular value | — |
| 12 | $\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{\min(m,n)} \sigma_{i}$ | Determinant (square case) | Sign of $\det$ lost unless tracked |

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Image Processing:** Storing a $512 \times 512$ image needs 262,144 floats. Truncated SVD with $k=50$ uses only $50(512 + 512 + 1) \approx 25,650$ floats — **a 90% storage reduction with negligible visual loss**.
- **Recommender Systems:** Netflix/Amazon-style collaborative filtering uses truncated SVD on the user–item rating matrix to find latent "taste" dimensions.
- **NLP:** Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) applies SVD to the term–document matrix to uncover hidden conceptual topics.
- **Computer Vision:** The fundamental matrix and essential matrix in stereo vision rely on SVD-based constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $\mathbf{V}$ Diagonalizes $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$

Assume the SVD exists: $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$ (we will prove existence constructively).

**Step 1 — Multiply on the left by $\mathbf{A}^{T}$:**

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \left(\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}\right)^{T}\left(\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the transpose rule $(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{Z})^{T} = \mathbf{Z}^{T}\mathbf{Y}^{T}\mathbf{X}^{T}$:**

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{T}\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$$

**Step 3 — Use orthogonality of $\mathbf{U}$: $\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{I}$:**

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize this as the spectral decomposition:** $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ is a real symmetric positive semi-definite matrix. By the spectral theorem, it can be written as $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^{T}$ where $\mathbf{Q}$ is orthogonal and $\mathbf{\Lambda}$ is diagonal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The columns of $\mathbf{V}$ are the **eigenvectors** of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$, and the diagonal entries of $\mathbf{\Sigma}^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}$ (which are $\sigma_{i}^{2}$) are the **eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$.

By an identical argument on $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$, the columns of $\mathbf{U}$ are the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$, and the singular values are the same.

### 3.2 Constructive Algorithm (How to Compute SVD)

Given an $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$:

1. Compute the symmetric matrix $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ (size $n \times n$).
2. Find the eigenvalues $\lambda_{1} \ge \lambda_{2} \ge \dots \ge \lambda_{n} \ge 0$ and corresponding orthonormal eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_{1}, \mathbf{v}_{2}, \dots, \mathbf{v}_{n}$.
3. Set $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}}$.
4. Form $\mathbf{V} = [\mathbf{v}_{1} \mid \mathbf{v}_{2} \mid \dots \mid \mathbf{v}_{n}]$ and $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\sigma_{1}, \dots, \sigma_{n})$.
5. For each $i$ with $\sigma_{i} > 0$, compute $\mathbf{u}_{i} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{i}}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i}$.
6. For any remaining columns of $\mathbf{U}$ (when $m > n$), extend to an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (Board Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Compute the SVD of $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$.

#### Step 3.3.1 — Form $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}^{T}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Note that $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric, so $\mathbf{A}^{T} = \mathbf{A}$.

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3\cdot3+1\cdot1 & 3\cdot1+1\cdot3 \\ 1\cdot3+3\cdot1 & 1\cdot1+3\cdot3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6 \\ 6 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3.3.2 — Find eigenvalues from $\det(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$

$$\det\!\begin{pmatrix} 10-\lambda & 6 \\ 6 & 10-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = 0$$

$$(10-\lambda)^{2} - 36 = 0$$

$$\lambda^{2} - 20\lambda + 100 - 36 = 0$$

$$\lambda^{2} - 20\lambda + 64 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula $\lambda = \dfrac{20 \pm \sqrt{400 - 256}}{2} = \dfrac{20 \pm 12}{2}$:

$$\lambda_{1} = 16, \quad \lambda_{2} = 4$$

#### Step 3.3.3 — Compute singular values

$$\sigma_{1} = \sqrt{16} = 4, \quad \sigma_{2} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

#### Step 3.3.4 — Find the right singular vectors (eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$)

**For $\lambda_{1} = 16$:** Solve $(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - 16\mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_{1} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -6 & 6 \\ 6 & -6 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -6x + 6y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x = y$$

Normalize: $\lVert \mathbf{v}_{1} \rVert = \sqrt{x^{2}+x^{2}} = x\sqrt{2}$. Setting $x\sqrt{2} = 1$ gives $x = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$\mathbf{v}_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**For $\lambda_{2} = 4$:** Solve $(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - 4\mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_{2} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 \\ 6 & 6 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 6x + 6y = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x = -y$$

Normalize similarly:

$$\mathbf{v}_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{V} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3.3.5 — Compute the left singular vectors using $\mathbf{u}_{i} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{i}}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i}$

**For $i=1$:**

$$\mathbf{u}_{1} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 4/\sqrt{2} \\ 4/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**For $i=2$:**

$$\mathbf{u}_{2} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 2/\sqrt{2} \\ -2/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3.3.6 — Verification: Reconstruct $\mathbf{A}$

$$\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

First compute $\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4/\sqrt{2} & 4/\sqrt{2} \\ 2/\sqrt{2} & -2/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2\sqrt{2} & 2\sqrt{2} \\ \sqrt{2} & -\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Then multiply by $\mathbf{U}$:

$$\begin{aligned}\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2\sqrt{2} & 2\sqrt{2} \\ \sqrt{2} & -\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} (1/\sqrt{2})(2\sqrt{2}) + (1/\sqrt{2})(\sqrt{2}) & (1/\sqrt{2})(2\sqrt{2}) + (1/\sqrt{2})(-\sqrt{2}) \\ (1/\sqrt{2})(2\sqrt{2}) + (-1/\sqrt{2})(\sqrt{2}) & (1/\sqrt{2})(2\sqrt{2}) + (-1/\sqrt{2})(-\sqrt{2}) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} 2 + 1 & 2 - 1 \\ 2 - 1 & 2 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Success:** The reconstruction matches the original matrix, confirming our SVD is correct.

### 3.4 Truncated SVD & Eckart–Young Theorem (Critical for KTU 14-mark questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem:** Among all rank-$k$ matrices $\mathbf{B}$, the truncated SVD $\mathbf{A}_{k} = \mathbf{U}_{k}\mathbf{\Sigma}_{k}\mathbf{V}_{k}^{T}$ (keeping only the top $k$ singular values) **minimizes** both the Frobenius norm $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B} \rVert_{F}$ and the spectral norm $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B} \rVert_{2}$. The retained energy is
> $$\text{Energy Retained} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma_{i}^{2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_{i}^{2}} \times 100\%$$

This is the **theoretical foundation of PCA and dimensionality reduction**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of SVD (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the SVD pipeline: how an input matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is decomposed, the role each factor plays, and the output branches into different AI/Data Science applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Matrix A: m x n] --> B[Compute A transpose A]
    A --> C[Compute A A transpose]

    B --> D[Eigendecomposition: find lambda_i and v_i]
    C --> E[Eigendecomposition: find lambda_i and u_i]

    D --> F[Singular Values: sigma_i = sqrt of lambda_i]
    E --> F

    D --> G[Right Singular Matrix V: n x n]
    E --> H[Left Singular Matrix U: m x m]

    F --> I[Diagonal Matrix Sigma: m x n]

    G --> J[SVD Output: A = U Sigma V transpose]
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K1[Application: Dimensionality Reduction via Truncated SVD]
    J --> K2[Application: Image Compression]
    J --> K3[Application: Recommender Systems]
    J --> K4[Application: Latent Semantic Analysis in NLP]
    J --> K5[Application: Principal Component Analysis PCA]

    K1 --> L1[Rank k approximation A_k: removes smallest sigma values]
    K2 --> L2[Storage reduced from m x n to k times m plus n plus 1]
    K3 --> L3[Latent taste vectors for users and items]
    K4 --> L4[Topic vectors in term document space]
    K5 --> L5[Eigenvectors of covariance matrix equal to right singular vectors of centered data]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: The "Rotate → Scale → Rotate" Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Vector x in R n] --> R1[Step 1: Apply V transpose - rotate to principal axes]
    R1 --> S1[Step 2: Apply Sigma - scale by sigma_1, sigma_2, ...]
    S1 --> R2[Step 3: Apply U - rotate back to output frame]
    R2 --> P2[Output Vector y in R m]
```

### 4.3 Energy Retention Visual (Singular Value Spectrum)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Singular Value Spectrum sigma_1 ge sigma_2 ge ... ge sigma_r] --> T{Choose truncation rank k}
    T -- Keep top k --> P[Retained energy = sum sigma_i squared for i=1 to k divided by sum sigma_i squared for i=1 to r]
    T -- Discard k+1 to r --> Q[Lost energy = sum sigma_i squared for i=k+1 to r]
    P --> U[Truncated SVD: A_k = U_k Sigma_k V_k transpose]
    Q --> V[Error bound: spectral norm = sigma_k+1, Frobenius norm = sqrt sum sigma_i squared]
```
