## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. State the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) theorem. What are the dimensions of the three matrices involved when decomposing an $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$?** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
> The Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) theorem states that any real $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ of rank $r$ can be factorized as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$, where $\mathbf{U}$ is an $m \times m$ orthogonal matrix whose columns are the left singular vectors (eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$), $\mathbf{V}$ is an $n \times n$ orthogonal matrix whose columns are the right singular vectors (eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$), and $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is an $m \times n$ rectangular diagonal matrix whose diagonal entries are the singular values $\sigma_{1} \ge \sigma_{2} \ge \dots \ge \sigma_{r} > 0$ arranged in descending order, with the remaining entries being zero. The SVD exists for every real (or complex) matrix, irrespective of whether it is square, full-rank, or non-singular.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q2. How are the singular values of a matrix $\mathbf{A}$ related to the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$? Why are singular values always non-negative?** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
> The singular values of $\mathbf{A}$ are the non-negative square roots of the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$. That is, if $\lambda_{i}$ are the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$, then the singular values are $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}}$. This relationship arises because $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ is a real symmetric positive semi-definite matrix, and therefore all its eigenvalues $\lambda_{i} \ge 0$. Taking the square root of a non-negative number yields a real, non-negative result, which is why the singular values $\sigma_{i}$ are always guaranteed to be real and $\sigma_{i} \ge 0$. Furthermore, by convention they are arranged in non-increasing order: $\sigma_{1} \ge \sigma_{2} \ge \dots \ge \sigma_{r} > 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark SVD problems, students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting to **normalize** the eigenvectors (singular vectors must be unit-length — KTU strictly checks this).
> 2. Computing the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ instead of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ for $\mathbf{V}$ (and vice-versa for $\mathbf{U}$) — dimension mismatch is an instant deduction.
> 3. Skipping the **verification step** by reconstructing $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$ to confirm $\mathbf{A}$. Examiners reward explicit reconstruction.
> 4. Confusing **singular values** with **eigenvalues** in the final answer.
> 5. Not stating the orthogonality property $\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{I}$ when claiming the matrix is orthogonal.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 14 marks]**

**Q. (a)** Compute the Singular Value Decomposition of the matrix
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
Clearly show the steps to find $\mathbf{U}$, $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, and $\mathbf{V}^{T}$. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$:**

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+0+1 & 1+0+0 \\ 1+0+0 & 1+1+0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Find eigenvalues:** $\det(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (2-\lambda)^{2} - 1 = \lambda^{2} - 4\lambda + 3 = (\lambda - 3)(\lambda - 1) = 0$.

So $\lambda_{1} = 3, \lambda_{2} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Singular values:** $\sigma_{1} = \sqrt{3}, \sigma_{2} = 1$.

**Step 4 — Right singular vectors:**
- For $\lambda_{1}=3$: $(2-3)x + y = 0 \Rightarrow -x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = x$. Normalized: $\mathbf{v}_{1} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- For $\lambda_{2}=1$: $(2-1)x + y = 0 \Rightarrow x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = -x$. Normalized: $\mathbf{v}_{2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$.

$$\mathbf{V} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Left singular vectors:**

$$\mathbf{u}_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{u}_{2} = \frac{1}{1}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Third column of $\mathbf{U}$ (orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}_{1}$ and $\mathbf{u}_{2}$):** Use cross product. $\mathbf{u}_{3} = \mathbf{u}_{1} \times \mathbf{u}_{2}$:

$$\mathbf{u}_{3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{2}}\begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{12}}\begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 1 - 1\cdot(-1) \\ 1\cdot 0 - 2\cdot 1 \\ 2\cdot(-1) - 1\cdot 0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -2 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{U} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/\sqrt{6} & 0 & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & -1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final SVD:** $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$.  ✓ (Full mark awarded upon verification.)

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Eckart–Young theorem** and show how it is used in **truncated SVD for dimensionality reduction**. Why is the truncated SVD considered the "best" low-rank approximation of a matrix? Mention its role in **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

> **Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem (Statement):** Given any $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ of rank $r$ with singular values $\sigma_{1} \ge \sigma_{2} \ge \dots \ge \sigma_{r}$, the **truncated SVD** $\mathbf{A}_{k} = \mathbf{U}_{k}\mathbf{\Sigma}_{k}\mathbf{V}_{k}^{T}$ (formed by retaining only the first $k < r$ singular values and corresponding singular vectors) is the **best rank-$k$ approximation** of $\mathbf{A}$ in both the Frobenius norm and the spectral (operator) norm sense. That is, for any rank-$k$ matrix $\mathbf{B}$:
> $$\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{F} \le \lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B} \rVert_{F}, \quad \lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{2} \le \lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B} \rVert_{2}$$
> The corresponding error bounds are $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{F} = \sqrt{\sigma_{k+1}^{2} + \sigma_{k+2}^{2} + \dots + \sigma_{r}^{2}}$ and $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{2} = \sigma_{k+1}$.

> **Dimensionality Reduction via Truncated SVD:** In a high-dimensional dataset (say, an $m \times n$ data matrix with $n \gg 1$ features), we form $\mathbf{A}_{k}$ which uses only $k(m + n + 1)$ parameters instead of the original $mn$ entries. The retained variance/energy ratio is
> $$\eta = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma_{i}^{2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_{i}^{2}} \times 100\%$$
> By choosing $k$ such that $\eta \ge 95\%$ (a common engineering threshold), we achieve massive compression with negligible information loss.

> **Why "Best":** No other rank-$k$ matrix — whether obtained via random projection, feature selection, or any other heuristic — can come closer to $\mathbf{A}$ in either norm. The proof follows from the unitary invariance of the Frobenius and spectral norms and the fact that the orthogonal complement of $\text{span}(\mathbf{v}_{1}, \dots, \mathbf{v}_{k})$ is the unique subspace that maximally reduces the error.

> **Connection to PCA:** When $\mathbf{A}$ is the **mean-centered** data matrix (rows = samples, columns = features), the right singular vectors $\mathbf{v}_{i}$ coincide with the **principal components** (eigenvectors of the covariance matrix $\frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$), and the squared singular values $\sigma_{i}^{2}$ are proportional to the variance explained by each principal component. Thus, **truncated SVD on mean-centered data IS PCA**. This duality is exploited in scikit-learn's `TruncatedSVD` and `PCA` classes.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 14 marks]**

**Q. (a)** Find the Singular Value Decomposition of
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 3 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$$
Verify your result by reconstructing $\mathbf{A}$. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$:**

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 0 & -5 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 3 & -5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 16+9 & 0-15 \\ 0-15 & 0+25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & -15 \\ -15 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvalues:** $\det(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (25 - \lambda)^{2} - 225 = \lambda^{2} - 50\lambda + 625 - 225 = \lambda^{2} - 50\lambda + 400 = 0$.

$$\lambda = \dfrac{50 \pm \sqrt{2500 - 1600}}{2} = \dfrac{50 \pm 30}{2} \Rightarrow \lambda_{1} = 40, \lambda_{2} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Singular values:** $\sigma_{1} = \sqrt{40} = 2\sqrt{10}, \sigma_{2} = \sqrt{10}$.

**Step 4 — Right singular vectors:**
- For $\lambda_{1}=40$: $(25-40)x - 15y = 0 \Rightarrow -15x - 15y = 0 \Rightarrow y = -x$. Normalized: $\mathbf{v}_{1} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- For $\lambda_{2}=10$: $(25-10)x - 15y = 0 \Rightarrow 15x - 15y = 0 \Rightarrow y = x$. Normalized: $\mathbf{v}_{2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

$$\mathbf{V} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Left singular vectors:**

$$\mathbf{u}_{1} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{10}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 3 & -5 \end{bmatrix}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{20}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\cdot 2\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{u}_{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 3 & -5 \end{bmatrix}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{20}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{U} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 2\sqrt{10} & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{10} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$\mathbf{V}^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 2\sqrt{10} & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{10} \end{bmatrix}\cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 2\sqrt{10} & -2\sqrt{10} \\ \sqrt{10} & \sqrt{10} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 2\sqrt{10} & -2\sqrt{10} \\ \sqrt{10} & \sqrt{10} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2\sqrt{10} & -2\sqrt{10} \\ \sqrt{10} & \sqrt{10} \end{bmatrix}$$

Wait — simplify step-by-step. $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$. So the leading scalar is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$, and the matrix product is:

$$\begin{aligned}\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}\begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 2\sqrt{10} + 2\cdot \sqrt{10} & 1\cdot(-2\sqrt{10}) + 2\cdot \sqrt{10} \\ 2\cdot 2\sqrt{10} + (-1)\cdot \sqrt{10} & 2\cdot(-2\sqrt{10}) + (-1)\cdot \sqrt{10} \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}\begin{bmatrix} 4\sqrt{10} & 0 \\ 3\sqrt{10} & -5\sqrt{10} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 3 & -5 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss in detail the **applications of SVD** in (i) **image compression**, (ii) **recommender systems**, and (iii) **latent semantic analysis (LSA)**. How does the truncation parameter $k$ control the trade-off between information loss and storage? `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

> **(i) Image Compression:** A grayscale image is stored as an $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ (pixel intensities). Computing its SVD gives $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$. The full SVD requires $m^{2} + n^{2} + r$ floats, but the truncated SVD $\mathbf{A}_{k}$ requires only $k(m + n + 1)$ floats. The compression ratio is
> $$\text{CR} = \dfrac{mn}{k(m+n+1)}$$
> For a $512 \times 512$ image with $k=50$, this is approximately $10\times$ compression. The retained image quality is governed by the energy ratio $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma_{i}^{2} / \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_{i}^{2}$.

> **(ii) Recommender Systems:** In collaborative filtering, the user–item rating matrix $\mathbf{R}$ is typically sparse (most users have rated only a few items). Applying truncated SVD to $\mathbf{R}$ produces a low-rank approximation $\mathbf{R}_{k}$. Each user $\mathbf{u}$ is then represented by a $k$-dimensional "taste vector" $\mathbf{u}^{T}\mathbf{V}_{k}\mathbf{\Sigma}_{k}^{-1}$, and each item by a $k$-dimensional "feature vector" $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{k}^{-1}\mathbf{U}_{k}^{T}\mathbf{i}$. The predicted rating of user $u$ for item $i$ is the dot product of these latent vectors. This is the engine behind the famous **Netflix Prize** winning algorithms.

> **(iii) Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) in NLP:** Given a term–document matrix $\mathbf{M}$ (rows = unique words, columns = documents, entries = TF-IDF weights), SVD reveals the latent "topic" structure. The columns of $\mathbf{U}_{k}$ are **topic vectors for terms**, the columns of $\mathbf{V}_{k}$ are **topic vectors for documents**, and $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{k}$ scales them. Two documents are deemed semantically similar if their $\mathbf{V}_{k}$ representations are close in cosine similarity — even if they share no literal words (handling **synonymy** and **polysemy**).

> **Role of truncation parameter $k$:**
> - **Small $k$** → aggressive compression, fast computation, but high information loss (large error $\sigma_{k+1}$).
> - **Large $k$** → faithful representation, high fidelity, but more storage and slower downstream operations.
> - **Standard engineering practice:** Choose $k$ such that the energy ratio exceeds **90–95%**, or use the **scree plot** (log-log plot of $\sigma_{i}$ vs. $i$) to locate the "elbow" where singular values drop sharply.
> - **Mathematically:** The error committed by truncating after the $k$-th singular value is bounded by the spectral norm $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{2} = \sigma_{k+1}$, and by the Frobenius norm $\lVert \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_{k} \rVert_{F} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r}\sigma_{i}^{2}}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.)**

- ✅ **Theorem:** Every $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ can be written as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$ — *no exceptions* (square, rectangular, singular, or non-singular).
- ✅ **Sizing rule:** $\mathbf{U}$ is $m \times m$, $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is $m \times n$, $\mathbf{V}^{T}$ is $n \times n$. (For the *thin/economy* SVD, $\mathbf{U}$ is $m \times r$, $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is $r \times r$, $\mathbf{V}^{T}$ is $r \times n$, where $r = \text{rank}(\mathbf{A})$.)
- ✅ **Computation recipe (in order):** (1) Form $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$. (2) Find its eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ and normalized eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_i$. (3) Set $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$. (4) Form $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. (5) Compute $\mathbf{u}_i = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_i / \sigma_i$.
- ✅ **Always normalize** singular vectors to unit length — the exam almost always checks this.
- ✅ **Singular values are non-negative** and arranged in **descending order**.
- ✅ **Rank** of $\mathbf{A}$ = number of strictly positive singular values.
- ✅ **Eckart–Young theorem:** Truncated SVD is the *best* rank-$k$ approximation in both Frobenius and spectral norms.
- ✅ **PCA Connection:** Right singular vectors of the *mean-centered* data matrix = principal components; squared singular values = variance explained.
- ✅ **Operator norm:** $\lVert \mathbf{A} \rVert_{2} = \sigma_{1}$ (largest singular value).
- ✅ **Pseudoinverse:** $\mathbf{A}^{+} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{+}\mathbf{U}^{T}$ where $\mathbf{\Sigma}^{+}$ replaces nonzero $\sigma_i$ by $1/\sigma_i$.
- ✅ **Orthogonality checks:** $\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{I}_m$ and $\mathbf{V}^{T}\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I}_n$ — verify by inspection.
- ✅ **Determinant (square case):** $\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i$.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Students compute $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ when asked for $\mathbf{V}$ (which requires $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$). Memorize: **$\mathbf{V}$ comes from $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ (size $n \times n$); $\mathbf{U}$ comes from $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ (size $m \times m$).**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectral Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spectral Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectral Decomposition (Spectral Theorem for Symmetric Matrices):** Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix (i.e., $A = A^T$). Then there exists an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^n$ consisting entirely of eigenvectors of $A$. Equivalently, $A$ can be factored as:
> $$A = Q \Lambda Q^T$$
> where $Q$ is an orthogonal matrix whose columns are the normalized eigenvectors of $A$, and $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix containing the corresponding eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$ on its main diagonal. This representation is called the **Spectral Decomposition** of $A$.

The term "spectrum" refers to the set of all eigenvalues $\{\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n\}$ of the matrix $A$, and "decomposition" refers to the factorization of $A$ into simpler, structured components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rubber sheet stretched along a wooden frame**. The rubber sheet deforms in different directions with different intensities depending on the direction of pull. Now imagine the sheet is square and made of a special material whose stiffness differs in three perpendicular directions.

- The **eigenvectors** are the *natural directions* along which the material simply stretches or compresses (no twisting or shearing).
- The **eigenvalues** are the *stretching factors* (how much the material expands or contracts in each of those natural directions).
- The **spectral decomposition** says: *any complicated stretching of the material can be understood as a combination of simple stretches along these natural directions, plus a rotation that aligns everything*.

So instead of looking at one big, scary transformation matrix, we can understand it as: **rotate → stretch in three perpendicular directions → rotate back**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Spectral decomposition is essentially the matrix analog of breaking a complex motion into simple components along perpendicular axes. It transforms a complicated coupled system into independent one-dimensional problems.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Orthogonal Matrix:** A square matrix $Q$ such that $Q^T Q = Q Q^T = I$ (identity matrix). The columns of $Q$ form an orthonormal set.
- **Diagonal Matrix:** A matrix $\Lambda$ where all off-diagonal entries are zero.
- **Symmetric Matrix:** A matrix $A$ such that $A = A^T$ (equals its own transpose). The **only requirement** for real spectral decomposition to exist.

## 1.4 Why This Matters in AI and Data Science

Spectral decomposition is the **mathematical engine** behind:
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA):** Reduces high-dimensional data to its most informative directions.
- **Spectral Clustering:** Clusters data using eigenvectors of similarity matrices.
- **Recommender Systems:** Low-rank approximations via largest eigenvalues.
- **Graph Neural Networks:** Spectral graph theory uses Laplacian eigendecomposition.
- **Google's PageRank Algorithm:** Uses the dominant eigenvector of a stochastic matrix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Concepts (Prerequisites)

### 2.1.1 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

For a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, a scalar $\lambda$ is an **eigenvalue** and a non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ is the corresponding **eigenvector** if:

$$A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

The eigenvalues are the roots of the **characteristic polynomial**:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

### 2.1.2 Orthogonality of Eigenvectors of Symmetric Matrices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** For a real symmetric matrix $A$, eigenvectors corresponding to **distinct eigenvalues** are mutually **orthogonal**. For repeated eigenvalues, an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors can always be constructed (via Gram-Schmidt process).

If $\mathbf{v}_i$ and $\mathbf{v}_j$ are eigenvectors with $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$, then:

$$\mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j = 0$$

## 2.2 The Spectral Decomposition Theorem

### 2.2.1 Statement

If $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is symmetric ($A = A^T$), then there exists an orthogonal matrix $Q$ and a diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ such that:

$$A = Q \Lambda Q^T$$

### 2.2.2 Expanded Form (Outer Product Representation)

Let $Q = [\mathbf{q}_1 \; \mathbf{q}_2 \; \cdots \; \mathbf{q}_n]$ where $\mathbf{q}_i$ are the normalized eigenvectors. Then:

$$\boxed{A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T}$$

This is the **spectral expansion** or **outer product form**. Each term $\lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$ is a rank-1 matrix called a **spectral projector** onto the direction $\mathbf{q}_i$.

## 2.3 Properties of Spectral Decomposition

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Meaning |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Orthogonality of $Q$** | $Q^T Q = I$ | Columns of $Q$ are orthonormal basis vectors |
| **Eigenvector equation** | $A \mathbf{q}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i$ | Each column of $Q$ is a fixed direction of $A$ |
| **Rank-1 decomposition** | $A = \sum_{i} \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$ | $A$ is a weighted sum of projectors |
| **Inverse** | $A^{-1} = Q \Lambda^{-1} Q^T$ | Inversion becomes trivial if all $\lambda_i \neq 0$ |
| **Power** | $A^k = Q \Lambda^k Q^T = \sum_i \lambda_i^k \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$ | Matrix powers are easy to compute |
| **Trace** | $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Trace equals sum of eigenvalues |
| **Determinant** | $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Determinant equals product of eigenvalues |
| **Symmetry preservation** | $A = A^T \Rightarrow A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ | Required condition for real decomposition |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Engineering:** Principal stress analysis decomposes stress tensors into principal directions and magnitudes.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Natural frequencies of structures are eigenvalues of mass-stiffness matrices.
- **Quantum Mechanics:** Observables are Hermitian operators; their eigenvalues are measurable quantities.
- **Computer Vision:** Image compression via truncated spectral decomposition (eigenfaces).
- **Signal Processing:** Karhunen-Loève Transform uses spectral decomposition for optimal decorrelation.

## 2.5 Conditions for Existence

| Condition | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Necessary** | $A$ must be square |
| **Sufficient (Real)** | $A$ is real and symmetric ($A = A^T$) |
| **Sufficient (Complex)** | $A$ is Hermitian ($A = A^*$) |
| **General case** | Non-symmetric matrices have *Schur decomposition* $A = Q T Q^*$ with $T$ upper triangular |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Eigenvalue Equation to Spectral Form

**Step 1 — Start with the eigenvalue equation for each eigenvector:**

For each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, we have:

$$A \mathbf{q}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i$$

**Step 2 — Stack all eigenvector equations into a single matrix equation:**

Place all eigenvectors as columns of matrix $Q$ and all eigenvalues on the diagonal of $\Lambda$:

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} \vert & \vert & & \vert \\ \mathbf{q}_1 & \mathbf{q}_2 & \cdots & \mathbf{q}_n \\ \vert & \vert & & \vert \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:

$$A Q = Q \Lambda$$

**Step 3 — Use orthogonality of $Q$:**

Since $A$ is symmetric, its eigenvectors are mutually orthogonal. After normalizing, $Q^T Q = I$, hence $Q^{-1} = Q^T$.

**Step 4 — Solve for $A$:**

Right-multiplying both sides of $A Q = Q \Lambda$ by $Q^T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A Q &= Q \Lambda \\
A Q Q^T &= Q \Lambda Q^T \\
A I &= Q \Lambda Q^T \\
\therefore A &= Q \Lambda Q^T
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Outer Product Form

Starting from $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ and writing out the matrix products explicitly:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \begin{bmatrix} \vert & \vert & & \vert \\ \mathbf{q}_1 & \mathbf{q}_2 & \cdots & \mathbf{q}_n \\ \vert & \vert & & \vert \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} - & \mathbf{q}_1^T & - \\ - & \mathbf{q}_2^T & - \\ & \vdots & \\ - & \mathbf{q}_n^T & - \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying $\Lambda Q^T$ first gives scaled rows:

$$\Lambda Q^T = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 \mathbf{q}_1^T \\ \lambda_2 \mathbf{q}_2^T \\ \vdots \\ \lambda_n \mathbf{q}_n^T \end{bmatrix}$$

Now multiplying $Q$ by this:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{q}_i (\lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i^T) \\
A &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **outer product form**, showing that $A$ is a weighted sum of rank-1 projectors $\mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$ with weights $\lambda_i$.

## 3.3 Worked Example: 2×2 Symmetric Matrix

**Problem:** Find the spectral decomposition of:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 1 — Compute Eigenvalues

$$\begin{aligned}
\det(A - \lambda I) &= 0 \\
\det \begin{bmatrix} 5 - \lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 5 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} &= 0 \\
(5 - \lambda)^2 - 4 &= 0 \\
(5 - \lambda)^2 &= 4 \\
5 - \lambda &= \pm 2 \\
\lambda_1 = 7, \quad \lambda_2 &= 3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Eigenvectors

**For $\lambda_1 = 7$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(A - 7I)\mathbf{v}_1 &= 0 \\
\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This gives $-2x + 2y = 0 \Rightarrow x = y$. So $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, normalized:

$$\mathbf{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**For $\lambda_2 = 3$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(A - 3I)\mathbf{v}_2 &= 0 \\
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This gives $x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = -x$. So $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$, normalized:

$$\mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Verify Orthogonality

$$\mathbf{q}_1^T \mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{2}(1)(1) + \frac{1}{2}(1)(-1) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Form the Spectral Decomposition

$$Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= Q \Lambda Q^T \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 7 \\ 3 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4 \\ 4 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Outer Product Form

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 7 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{7}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{3}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Inverse via Spectral Decomposition

**Step 1 — Start with $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ and invert both sides:**

$$A^{-1} = (Q \Lambda Q^T)^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the property $(XYZ)^{-1} = Z^{-1} Y^{-1} X^{-1}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A^{-1} &= (Q^T)^{-1} \Lambda^{-1} Q^{-1} \\
&= (Q^T)^{-1} \Lambda^{-1} Q^T
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Use $Q^{-1} = Q^T$ (orthogonality), so $(Q^T)^{-1} = Q$:**

$$A^{-1} = Q \Lambda^{-1} Q^T$$

**Step 4 — Write out the inverse diagonal matrix explicitly:**

$$\Lambda^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 1/\lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1/\lambda_n \end{bmatrix}$$

In outer product form:

$$A^{-1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\lambda_i} \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$$

This shows that **inverting a matrix** through spectral decomposition is equivalent to inverting each scalar eigenvalue — an enormous computational simplification, provided no $\lambda_i = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence of Inverse:** A symmetric matrix $A$ is invertible if and only if all its eigenvalues are non-zero ($\lambda_i \neq 0$ for all $i$).

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Operation | Direct Form | Spectral Form |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| $A^k$ | Hard for large $k$ | $Q \Lambda^k Q^T$ |
| $A^{-1}$ | $O(n^3)$ via Gaussian elimination | $Q \Lambda^{-1} Q^T$ in $O(n^2)$ |
| $\text{tr}(A)$ | Sum of diagonal entries | $\sum \lambda_i$ |
| $\det(A)$ | Recursive cofactor expansion | $\prod \lambda_i$ |
| Solve $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ | Gaussian elimination | $\mathbf{x} = Q \Lambda^{-1} Q^T \mathbf{b}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Spectral Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Symmetric Matrix A"]:::input --> B["Compute Characteristic Polynomial det A minus lambda I"]
    B --> C["Find Eigenvalues lambda 1 lambda 2 ... lambda n"]
    C --> D["Solve A q_i equals lambda_i q_i for each i"]
    D --> E["Apply Gram Schmidt to get Orthonormal Set"]
    E --> F["Form Orthogonal Matrix Q from columns q_i"]
    F --> G["Form Diagonal Matrix Lambda from eigenvalues"]
    G --> H["Spectral Form A equals Q Lambda Q transpose"]
    H --> I["Equivalent Outer Product Sum lambda_i q_i q_i transpose"]
    I --> J["Applications PCA Clustering Recommenders"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Space R^n with basis e1 e2 ... en"]:::input --> B["Rotation via Q transpose"]
    B --> C["Principal Axis Space R^n with basis q1 q2 ... qn"]
    C --> D["Independent Scaling by lambda 1 ... lambda n"]
    D --> E["Diagonal Action via Lambda"]
    E --> F["Rotation Back via Q"]
    F --> G["Result Transformed Vector"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Relationship Between Concepts

```mermaid
graph TD
    Matrix["Square Matrix A"]:::core --> SymCheck{"Is A equals A transpose"}:::decision
    SymCheck -->|Yes| Sym["Symmetric Matrix A"]:::special
    SymCheck -->|No| NonSym["Schur Decomposition Only"]:::alt

    Sym --> SpecDecomp["Spectral Decomposition A equals Q Lambda Q transpose"]:::method
    SpecDecomp --> Q["Orthogonal Q columns are eigenvectors"]:::comp
    SpecDecomp --> Lambda["Diagonal Lambda eigenvalues on diagonal"]:::comp
    SpecDecomp --> Outer["Outer Product Sum lambda_i q_i q_i transpose"]:::comp

    Q --> Apps["PCA Recommender Systems Clustering"]:::app
    Lambda --> Apps
    Outer --> Apps

    classDef core fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef special fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef alt fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Algorithmic Decision Map (For Computation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need to decompose A"]:::start --> Step1["Step 1 Verify A equals A transpose"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2 Form characteristic polynomial"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3 Solve det A minus lambda I equals 0 for eigenvalues"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Step 4 For each eigenvalue solve null space of A minus lambda I"]
    Step4 --> Step5["Step 5 Normalize each eigenvector"]
    Step5 --> Step6["Step 6 Verify q_i transpose q_j equals 0 for i not equal j"]
    Step6 --> Step7["Step 7 Assemble Q and Lambda"]
    Step7 --> Done["Output A equals Q Lambda Q transpose"]:::done

    classDef start fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the Spectral Decomposition Theorem for a real symmetric matrix.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> If $A$ is a real $n \times n$ symmetric matrix (i.e., $A = A^T$), then there exists a real orthogonal matrix $Q$ and a real diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ such that $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$, where the diagonal entries of $\Lambda$ are the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$ of $A$ and the columns of $Q$ are the corresponding orthonormal eigenvectors. Equivalently, $A$ can be expressed as $A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$, where $\mathbf{q}_i$ are the orthonormal eigenvectors. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What are the necessary and sufficient conditions for a real matrix to have a spectral decomposition?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The necessary condition is that the matrix must be **square**. The sufficient condition is that the matrix must be **real and symmetric** ($A = A^T$). For such matrices, all eigenvalues are real, eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal, and an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors can always be constructed, ensuring $Q$ is orthogonal. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Choice Pattern:** In the KTU End Semester Examination, students answer **one full question (14 marks) per module**, with internal choice between Question A and Question B. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Find the spectral decomposition of the matrix:**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the eigenvalues:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\det(A - \lambda I) &= 0 \\
\det \begin{bmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 4 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} &= 0 \\
(4 - \lambda)^2 - 4 &= 0 \\
16 - 8\lambda + \lambda^2 - 4 &= 0 \\
\lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 12 &= 0 \\
(\lambda - 6)(\lambda - 2) &= 0 \\
\therefore \lambda_1 = 6, \quad \lambda_2 &= 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Find the eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 6$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(A - 6I)\mathbf{v}_1 &= \mathbf{0} \\
\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This gives $-2x + 2y = 0 \Rightarrow x = y$. Choose $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, normalized:

$$\mathbf{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Find the eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(A - 2I)\mathbf{v}_2 &= \mathbf{0} \\
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This gives $x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = -x$. Choose $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$, normalized:

$$\mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Form $Q$ and $\Lambda$:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Write the spectral decomposition:**

$$\boxed{A = Q \Lambda Q^T = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \Lambda &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 6 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \\
A = Q \Lambda Q^T &= \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 6 & -2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 4 \\ 4 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Given the spectral decomposition $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ of a symmetric matrix, show that $A^k = Q \Lambda^k Q^T$ and compute $A^3$ for the matrix in part (a).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Prove $A^k = Q \Lambda^k Q^T$ by induction:**

For $k = 1$, trivially $A^1 = Q \Lambda^1 Q^T$. Assume true for $k$, i.e., $A^k = Q \Lambda^k Q^T$.

$$\begin{aligned}
A^{k+1} &= A^k \cdot A \\
&= (Q \Lambda^k Q^T)(Q \Lambda Q^T) \\
&= Q \Lambda^k (Q^T Q) \Lambda Q^T \\
&= Q \Lambda^k I \Lambda Q^T \quad \text{(since } Q^T Q = I \text{)} \\
&= Q (\Lambda^k \Lambda) Q^T \\
&= Q \Lambda^{k+1} Q^T
\end{aligned}$$

By induction, $A^k = Q \Lambda^k Q^T$ for all positive integers $k$. $\blacksquare$

**Step 2 — Compute $A^3$ for the given matrix:**

From part (a): $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

$$\Lambda^3 = \begin{bmatrix} 6^3 & 0 \\ 0 & 2^3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 216 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A^3 &= Q \Lambda^3 Q^T \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 216 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 216 & 8 \\ 216 & -8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 224 & 208 \\ 208 & 224 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 112 & 104 \\ 104 & 112 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification (by direct multiplication):**

$$\begin{aligned}
A^2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 16 \\ 16 & 20 \end{bmatrix} \\
A^3 &= A^2 \cdot A = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 16 \\ 16 & 20 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 112 & 104 \\ 104 & 112 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**State and prove the spectral decomposition theorem for a real symmetric matrix.**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** If $A$ is an $n \times n$ real symmetric matrix, then $A$ can be expressed as $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ where $Q$ is orthogonal and $\Lambda$ is diagonal.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Existence of real eigenvalues:**

Since $A$ is real and symmetric, the characteristic polynomial $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$ has real coefficients. By the fundamental theorem of algebra, $p(\lambda)$ has at least one complex root $\lambda_1$. Let $\mathbf{v}_1 \in \mathbb{C}^n$ be a corresponding complex eigenvector: $A \mathbf{v}_1 = \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1$.

Taking the conjugate transpose:
$$\begin{aligned}
A^* \mathbf{v}_1^* &= \lambda_1^* \mathbf{v}_1^* \\
\text{Since } A \text{ is real, } A^* = A^T &= A, \text{ so } A \mathbf{v}_1^* = \lambda_1^* \mathbf{v}_1^*
\end{aligned}$$

Also from $A \mathbf{v}_1 = \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_1^* A \mathbf{v}_1 &= \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1^* \mathbf{v}_1 \\
\mathbf{v}_1^* A^T \mathbf{v}_1 &= \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1^* \mathbf{v}_1 \\
(A \mathbf{v}_1)^* \mathbf{v}_1 &= \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1^* \mathbf{v}_1 \\
(\lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1)^* \mathbf{v}_1 &= \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1^* \mathbf{v}_1 \\
\lambda_1^* \mathbf{v}_1^* \mathbf{v}_1 &= \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1^* \mathbf{v}_1
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\mathbf{v}_1^* \mathbf{v}_1 \neq 0$, we get $\lambda_1^* = \lambda_1$, so $\lambda_1$ is real. All $n$ eigenvalues are real.

**Step 2 — Orthogonality of eigenvectors (distinct eigenvalues):**

Let $A \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$ and $A \mathbf{v}_j = \lambda_j \mathbf{v}_j$ with $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_i^T A \mathbf{v}_j &= \lambda_j \mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j \\
(A \mathbf{v}_i)^T \mathbf{v}_j &= \lambda_j \mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j \\
\lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j &= \lambda_j \mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j \\
(\lambda_i - \lambda_j) \mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$, we conclude $\mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j = 0$. $\blacksquare$

**Step 3 — Construction of orthonormal basis:**

Normalize each eigenvector to obtain $\mathbf{q}_i = \mathbf{v}_i / \Vert \mathbf{v}_i \Vert$. For repeated eigenvalues, apply Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization to obtain an orthonormal set. Since $\mathbb{R}^n$ has dimension $n$, we obtain $n$ orthonormal eigenvectors $\mathbf{q}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{q}_n$.

**Step 4 — Form the decomposition:**

$$Q = [\mathbf{q}_1 \; \mathbf{q}_2 \; \cdots \; \mathbf{q}_n], \quad \Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$$

Then $A Q = Q \Lambda$. Since $Q^T Q = I$, $Q^{-1} = Q^T$, and $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Using spectral decomposition, compute the inverse of:**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the eigenvalues computed earlier:** From the worked example, $\lambda_1 = 7$, $\lambda_2 = 3$ (both non-zero, so $A^{-1}$ exists).

**Step 2 — Form $\Lambda^{-1}$:**

$$\Lambda^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/7 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Use $A^{-1} = Q \Lambda^{-1} Q^T$:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q^T = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Note: $Q$ is symmetric in this case, so $Q^T = Q$.)

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \Lambda^{-1} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1/7 & 1/3 \\ 1/7 & -1/3 \end{bmatrix} \\
A^{-1} &= Q \Lambda^{-1} Q^T \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1/7 & 1/3 \\ 1/7 & -1/3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1/7 + 1/3 & 1/7 - 1/3 \\ 1/7 - 1/3 & 1/7 + 1/3 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 10/21 & -4/21 \\ -4/21 & 10/21 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 5/21 & -2/21 \\ -2/21 & 5/21 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify:**

$$A \cdot A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 5/21 & -2/21 \\ -2/21 & 5/21 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 21/21 & 0 \\ 0 & 21/21 \end{bmatrix} = I \quad \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify symmetry:** Students often attempt spectral decomposition on non-symmetric matrices. Always check $A = A^T$ first. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Unnormalized eigenvectors:** Forgetting to normalize eigenvectors to obtain $Q^T Q = I$. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Not checking orthogonality:** For matrices with repeated eigenvalues, must apply Gram-Schmidt and verify $\mathbf{q}_i^T \mathbf{q}_j = 0$. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Wrong transpose position:** Writing $A = Q^T \Lambda Q$ instead of $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$. **(−3 marks — full mark loss on the answer)**
> 5. **Computational arithmetic errors:** Sign mistakes in eigenvectors (e.g., for $\lambda_2 = 3$ taking $\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ instead of $\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$). **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 6. **Not computing the characteristic polynomial correctly:** Especially for $3 \times 3$ matrices, expansion errors are common. **(−1 to −2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spectral Decomposition**

- **Definition:** $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ for real symmetric $A$, where $Q$ is orthogonal and $\Lambda$ is diagonal.
- **Prerequisite:** Matrix $A$ **must be symmetric** ($A = A^T$). Non-symmetric matrices do not have real spectral decomposition.
- **$Q$ is orthogonal:** $Q^T Q = Q Q^T = I$, so $Q^{-1} = Q^T$.
- **Columns of $Q$:** Normalized eigenvectors of $A$.
- **Diagonal of $\Lambda$:** Eigenvalues of $A$, in the order matching the eigenvectors.
- **Outer product form:** $A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$ — useful for understanding structure.
- **Matrix power shortcut:** $A^k = Q \Lambda^k Q^T = \sum_i \lambda_i^k \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^T$.
- **Inverse shortcut:** $A^{-1} = Q \Lambda^{-1} Q^T$ exists iff all $\lambda_i \neq 0$.
- **Trace shortcut:** $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_i \lambda_i$.
- **Determinant shortcut:** $\det(A) = \prod_i \lambda_i$.
- **Eigenvector orthogonality:** For symmetric $A$, eigenvectors of distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal; for repeated eigenvalues, use Gram-Schmidt.
- **AI/DS Applications:** PCA, spectral clustering, PageRank, recommender systems, eigenfaces, graph Laplacian.
- **Common 2×2 test case:** $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & a \end{bmatrix}$ has eigenvalues $a+b$ and $a-b$ with eigenvectors along $(1,1)$ and $(1,-1)$.
- **Existence of inverse:** A symmetric matrix is invertible iff $\det(A) = \prod \lambda_i \neq 0$ iff all $\lambda_i \neq 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of transforming high-dimensional data (data with a large number of features/variables) into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving as much of the *meaningful structural information* (variance, class separability, neighborhood relationships) as possible. Formally, given a dataset $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ with $n$ samples and $p$ features, dimensionality reduction seeks a mapping $f : \mathbb{R}^{p} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{k}$ where $k \ll p$, such that the intrinsic properties of $\mathbf{X}$ are retained in the reduced space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for GNEST305, this concept is treated as a foundational preprocessing pillar of the AI/ML pipeline, sitting logically between *Data Preprocessing* and *Model Building*.

### Sub-Categorization (KTU Syllabus Alignment)

| # | Category | Description | KTU Example |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Feature Selection** | Picks a *subset* of original features | Filter, Wrapper, Embedded methods |
| 2 | **Feature Extraction** | Creates *new* features from combinations of originals | PCA, LDA, t-SNE |
| 3 | **Projection Methods** | Linear/non-linear mapping onto lower space | PCA, SVD, Autoencoders |
| 4 | **Manifold Learning** | Preserves non-linear low-dim structure | t-SNE, Isomap, LLE |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Photograph Shrink" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a photographer who has shot a portrait in **12 megapixels** ($p=12$). For your Instagram post, you only need a **1 megapixel** ($k=1$) image. You cannot simply delete pixels at random — the face might become unrecognizable. Instead, you *intelligently compress* the image by combining neighbouring pixel information into averaged blocks. Dimensionality reduction does exactly this with data columns (features) — instead of deleting them blindly, it **merges correlated features** into compact representative ones.

Another intuition: Think of a 3D object (a cube) being viewed from one corner. The 3D information is already *projected* onto a 2D screen. PCA works in reverse — it finds the *best viewing angle* such that the 2D shadow preserves maximum information about the 3D structure.

---

## 1.3 The Curse of Dimensionality — Why Reduction is Needed

> [!WARNING]
> As the number of features $p$ grows, the volume of the feature space grows **exponentially**, causing data to become sparse (the **Hughes Phenomenon**). Distance metrics lose meaning, models overfit, and computational cost explodes. The *Bellman curse* states that the amount of data needed to maintain the same statistical significance grows exponentially with $p$.

### Practical Engineering Effects

- **Overfitting risk:** $\uparrow$ dramatically when $p \approx n$.
- **Storage cost:** $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot p)$ — becomes infeasible for genomics, image, IoT.
- **Distance concentration:** Euclidean distances between random points converge to a single value as $p \to \infty$.
- **Visualization:** Humans can only comprehend up to **3 dimensions** directly.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

Key metrics a KTU student must commit to memory:

- **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** = $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j}$, dimensionless.
- **Cumulative Explained Variance (CEV)** = $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{EVR}_i$, must be $\geq 0.95$ for engineering acceptance.
- **Reconstruction Error** = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \vert^{2}$, measured in squared feature units.
- **Kaiser Criterion:** Retain components with eigenvalue $\lambda_i > 1$ (only valid for *standardized* data).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — The Workhorse

PCA is the **most heavily tested** dimensionality reduction technique in KTU papers. It is an *unsupervised, linear, orthogonal projection* method that maximizes variance retention.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Standardize the data matrix** $\mathbf{X}$ to zero mean and unit variance (mandatory when features are on different scales).
2. **Compute the covariance matrix** $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ for centered $\mathbf{X}$.
3. **Perform eigendecomposition** of $\mathbf{C}$: $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$.
4. **Sort eigenvectors** by descending eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_p$.
5. **Select top-$k$ eigenvectors** to form projection matrix $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k]$.
6. **Transform data:** $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}_k$ where $\mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind each step:**
> - *Why standardize?* Covariance is scale-dependent. Without scaling, a feature in km would dominate over one in mm.
> - *Why eigendecomposition?* Eigenvectors of $\mathbf{C}$ point along the *directions of maximum variance* — proven via the Rayleigh Quotient.
> - *Why sort?* Principal components must be ordered by information content.
> - *Why top-$k$?* Lower-variance components often encode noise.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Domain | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Mean vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}_i$ | $\mathbb{R}^{p}$ | Column vector |
| 2 | Covariance matrix | $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_{c}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{c}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ | Symmetric, positive semi-definite |
| 3 | Eigendecomposition | $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$ | — | $\mathbf{V}$ orthogonal, $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ diagonal |
| 4 | Explained Variance Ratio | $\text{EVR}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}$ | dimensionless | $\sum \text{EVR}_i = 1$ |
| 5 | Cumulative EVR | $\text{CEV}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{EVR}_i$ | dimensionless | KTU target: $\geq 0.95$ |
| 6 | Reconstruction Error | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{p-k} \lambda_i$ | squared units | Sum of discarded eigenvalues |
| 7 | Kaiser Criterion | Keep if $\lambda_i > 1$ | — | Only for standardized data |
| 8 | Scree elbow | Plot $\lambda_i$ vs $i$ | — | Visual $k$ selection |
| 9 | SVD relation | $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$ | — | $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^{2}/(n-1)$ |
| 10 | LDA scatter ratio | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$ | dimensionless | Generalized eigenvalue problem |
| 11 | t-SNE perplexity | $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$ | entropy-based | KTU default: 30 |
| 12 | KL divergence | $\text{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij}\log\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$ | nats or bits | Objective t-SNE minimizes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical syntax note for KTU exams:** When writing absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or norm $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert$ inside a markdown table, **never use a bare pipe symbol** — use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage. The same rule applies to set notation: $\{ x \mid x > 0 \}$.

---

## 2.3 Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) — Supervised Cousin

LDA is a **supervised** dimensionality reduction technique. Unlike PCA which maximizes *total variance*, LDA maximizes **between-class separability** while minimizing within-class spread.

### Two Scatter Matrices (KTU High-Yield)

- **Within-class scatter:** $\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{c=1}^{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in c} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^{\top}$
- **Between-class scatter:** $\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{c=1}^{C} n_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Fisher's Criterion** is maximized: $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$
> The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ solves the generalized eigenvalue problem $\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$.
> **Maximum reduced dimensions** for LDA = $C-1$ where $C$ is the number of classes (a frequent KTU trick question).

### PCA vs LDA Comparison

| Property | PCA | LDA |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Supervision | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| Objective | Max variance | Max class separability |
| Max $k$ | $p$ | $C-1$ |
| Assumption | Linear, Gaussian not required | Multivariate Gaussian, equal covariance |
| Use case | Compression, denoising | Classification preprocessing |

---

## 2.4 t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) — Non-Linear

> [!NOTE]
> t-SNE is a **non-linear**, stochastic technique designed primarily for **2D/3D visualization** of high-dimensional data. It models pairwise similarities as probability distributions (Gaussian in high-D, Student-t in low-D) and minimizes the Kullback–Leibler divergence between them.

### Key Equations

- **High-dimensional affinity:** $p_{ij} = \frac{\exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2} / 2\sigma_i^{2})}{\sum_{k \neq i}\exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^{2} / 2\sigma_i^{2})}$
- **Symmetrized:** $p_{ij} = \frac{p_{i \vert j} + p_{j \vert i}}{2n}$
- **Low-dimensional affinity (Student-t, 1 d.o.f.):** $q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \Vert^{2})^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \Vert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \Vert^{2})^{-1}}$
- **Loss:** $\text{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij}\log\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$

The **heavy tails** of the Student-t distribution solve the *crowding problem* that afflicts SNE.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision:** Eigenfaces for face recognition (PCA-based).
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression dimensionality reduction (thousands of genes → tens of PCs).
- **NLP:** Latent Semantic Analysis (SVD on term-document matrices).
- **Anomaly Detection:** Reconstruction error in PCA flags outliers.
- **IoT / Edge AI:** Reduces sensor dimensionality for low-power inference.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Truncated SVD underlies matrix factorization in collaborative filtering.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Why Eigenvectors Maximize Variance

We need to prove that PCA's principal components are eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

### Setup

Let $\mathbf{X}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ be the *centered* data matrix (each column has zero mean). The covariance matrix is:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$$

Let $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ be a unit projection direction ($\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = 1$). The projected data is $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

### Step 1 — Variance of the Projection

The variance of the projected scalar values is:

$$\sigma_z^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{z}^{\top}\mathbf{z} = \frac{1}{n-1}(\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v})^{\top}(\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v}) = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v}$$

Substituting $\mathbf{C}$:

$$\sigma_z^{2} = \mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}$$

### Step 2 — Constrained Optimization (Lagrange Multiplier)

We wish to maximize $\mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}$ subject to $\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{v} = 1$.

The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{v}, \lambda) = \mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{v} - \lambda(\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - 1)$$

### Step 3 — Take the Derivative and Set to Zero

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}} = 2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} - 2\lambda \mathbf{v} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **defining equation of an eigenproblem**! The directions that maximize variance are exactly the **eigenvectors of $\mathbf{C}$**, and the maximized variance equals the corresponding **eigenvalue $\lambda$**. QED.

### Step 4 — Multi-Dimensional Generalization

For the top-$k$ principal components, we maximize total variance:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i \quad \text{subject to } \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_j = \delta_{ij}$$

The top-$k$ eigenvectors of $\mathbf{C}$ are the unique solution. This is the Courant–Fischer min-max theorem in action.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full PCA on a Toy Dataset

> [!NOTE]
> This is the type of **14-mark computation** that frequently appears in KTU ESE Module 2. Walk through every line.

**Given:** Centered dataset $\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ (already mean-zero).

**Step 1: Compute Covariance Matrix**

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} (4+0+1+1) & (0+0-1+2) \\ (0+0-1+2) & (0+1+1+4) \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 1 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1/3 \\ 1/3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Solve Characteristic Equation**

$$\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0 \implies (2-\lambda)^{2} - \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = 0$$

$$(2-\lambda)^{2} = \frac{1}{9} \implies 2-\lambda = \pm\frac{1}{3}$$

$$\lambda_1 = 2 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667, \quad \lambda_2 = 2 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.333$$

Wait — let me re-evaluate since the largest eigenvalue must come from the *plus* branch if the off-diagonal is positive. Re-solving: $\lambda = 2 \pm \frac{1}{3}$ gives $\lambda_1 = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.333$ and $\lambda_2 = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667$.

**Step 3: Find First Eigenvector (for $\lambda_1 = 7/3$)**

$$(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = 0 \implies \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 7/3 & 1/3 \\ 1/3 & 2 - 7/3 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} -1/3 & 1/3 \\ 1/3 & -1/3 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This yields $-v_{11} + v_{12} = 0 \implies v_{12} = v_{11}$. With unit normalization:

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Find Second Eigenvector (orthogonal to $\mathbf{v}_1$)**

$$\mathbf{v}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Choose $k$ via Cumulative Variance**

$$\text{Total variance} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = \frac{7}{3} + \frac{5}{3} = 4$$

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{7/3}{4} = \frac{7}{12} \approx 0.5833$$

$$\text{EVR}_2 = \frac{5/3}{4} = \frac{5}{12} \approx 0.4167$$

$$\text{CEV}_1 = 0.5833 < 0.95 \implies k = 2 \text{ (need both components)}$$

**Step 6: Project Data to 1D (if $k=1$ was acceptable)**

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 0 \\ -3/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.3 Reconstruction Error Derivation

If $\mathbf{X}_c$ is projected to $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}_k$ and reconstructed as $\hat{\mathbf{X}}_c = \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{W}_k^{\top}$, the reconstruction error is:

$$E = \Vert \mathbf{X}_c - \hat{\mathbf{X}}_c \Vert_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \Vert^{2}$$

Using the orthogonality of $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{W}_k^{\top}\mathbf{W}_k = \mathbf{I}_k$:

$$E = \sum_{j=k+1}^{p} \lambda_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Discarding the *bottom* $(p-k)$ principal components (lowest variance) minimizes the squared reconstruction error. This is why PCA with the Kaiser criterion is *optimal in the L2 sense*.

---

## 3.4 LDA Optimization Derivation (Generalized Eigenvalue)

Maximizing $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$. Set $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$:

$$2\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}) - 2\mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}) = 0$$

Dividing and letting $\lambda = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$:

$$\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} \implies \mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The optimal discriminant directions are the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B$. At most $C-1$ non-zero eigenvalues exist, capping LDA's reduced dimension.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw High Dimensional Data X] --> B[Step 1 Mean Centering]
    B --> C[Step 2 Feature Standardization]
    C --> D[Step 3 Covariance Matrix C Computation]
    D --> E[Step 4 Eigendecomposition C times v equals lambda v]
    E --> F[Step 5 Sort Eigenvalues in Descending Order]
    F --> G[Step 6 Select Top k Eigenvectors]
    G --> H[Step 7 Form Projection Matrix W k]
    H --> I[Step 8 Compute Z equals X times W k]
    I --> J[Step 9 Verify CEV greater than 0.95]
    J --> K{Acceptable?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Reduced Representation Z in R to the k]
    K -- No --> M[Increase k and Repeat]
    M --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each block represents a transformation stage. The feedback loop from `Increase k` back to the projection matrix stage implements the *iterative* nature of choosing $k$. The threshold $0.95$ for cumulative explained variance is a **common KTU board exam marking point** — students who forget to verify CEV lose 2 marks.

---

## 4.2 PCA vs LDA vs t-SNE Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Dimensionality Reduction Task] --> Q1{Labels Available?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Linear Assumption OK?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> LDA[LDA Path]
    LDA --> LDA1[Compute S W and S B]
    LDA1 --> LDA2[Solve Generalized Eigenvalue Problem]
    LDA2 --> LDA3[Pick Top C minus 1 Components]
    LDA3 --> LDAOUT[Reduced Data for Classification]
    Q2 -- Yes --> PCA1[Standardize Data]
    PCA1 --> PCA2[Compute Covariance Matrix]
    PCA2 --> PCA3[Eigendecomposition]
    PCA3 --> PCA4[Project to Top k Components]
    PCA4 --> PCAOUT[Reduced Data for Visualization or Compression]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need 2D or 3D Visualization?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> TSNE[t-SNE Path]
    Q3 -- No --> UMAP[UMAP or Autoencoder]
    TSNE --> TSNE1[Compute Pairwise Affinities P]
    TSNE1 --> TSNE2[Initialize Low D Embedding Y]
    TSNE2 --> TSNE3[Minimize KL Divergence via Gradient Descent]
    TSNE3 --> TSNEOUT[2D or 3D Cluster Map]
```

---

## 4.3 Variance Retention Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORI[Original Space R to the p]
        O1[Feature 1]
        O2[Feature 2]
        O3[Feature 3]
        O4[Feature p]
    end
    subgraph PC1[Principal Component 1]
        PC1A[Max Variance Direction]
    end
    subgraph PC2[Principal Component 2]
        PC2A[Second Max Variance Orthogonal to PC1]
    end
    subgraph KPC[Reduced Space R to the k]
        K1[Component 1]
        K2[Component 2]
        KK[Component k]
    end
    ORI --> PC1
    ORI --> PC2
    ORI --> KPC
    PC1 --> K1
    PC2 --> K2
```

---

## 4.4 Eigendecomposition Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    COV[Covariance Matrix C in R p by p] --> EIG[Eigendecomposition]
    EIG --> V[Eigenvector Matrix V Orthogonal]
    EIG --> LAMBDA[Eigenvalue Diagonal Lambda]
    V --> PC1A[PC 1 Direction of Max Variance]
    V --> PC2A[PC 2 Direction of Max Remaining Variance]
    V --> PCpA[PC p Direction of Min Variance]
    LAMBDA --> SORT[Sort Descending]
    SORT --> SEL[Select Top k Eigenvectors]
    SEL --> PROJ[Projection Matrix W k]
    PROJ --> OUT[Transformed Data Z]
```

---

## 4.5 Curse of Dimensionality — Intuitive Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[D equals 1] --> D1A[Data fills space densely]
    D2[D equals 2] --> D2A[Data on plane manageable]
    D3[D equals 3] --> D3A[Data in volume sparse already]
    D100[D equals 100] --> D100A[Data points are almost equidistant]
    D1000[D equals 1000] --> D1000A[All distances converge to same value]
    D1A --> OVER[Overfitting Risk LOW]
    D2A --> OVER
    D3A --> OVER2[Overfitting Risk RISING]
    D100A --> OVER2
    D1000A --> OVER3[Overfitting Risk EXTREME]
    OVER2 --> SOL[Dimensionality Reduction Required]
    OVER3 --> SOL
    SOL --> SOL1[PCA]
    SOL --> SOL2[LDA]
    SOL --> SOL3[t-SNE]
    SOL --> SOL4[Feature Selection]
```
