<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define the *Curse of Dimensionality*. List any four challenges it poses in machine learning.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Curse of Dimensionality** refers to the various phenomena that arise when analyzing data in high-dimensional spaces (typically $p \gg 3$) which do not occur in low-dimensional settings. It was formally introduced by Richard Bellman in the context of dynamic programming.

**Four challenges:**
1. **Data sparsity:** As $p$ grows, the volume of the feature space grows exponentially, so the available data becomes sparse.
2. **Distance concentration:** Pairwise Euclidean distances between random points converge to a single value, making nearest-neighbor methods unreliable.
3. **Overfitting:** With $p \approx n$, models memorize noise; the Hughes phenomenon shows accuracy degrades beyond an optimal $p$.
4. **Computational cost:** Storage and time complexity scale as $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot p)$ or worse, making algorithms intractable.

---

### Question A2

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between *Feature Selection* and *Feature Extraction* with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Feature Selection | Feature Extraction |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Picks a *subset* of original features | Creates *new* features as combinations of originals |
| Original features retained? | Yes | No (transformed) |
| Interpretability | High | Low (in transformed space) |
| Example 1 | Chi-squared test picks top 10 words from 1000 | PCA creates 10 principal components from 1000 features |
| Example 2 | Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) | LDA projection onto $C-1$ discriminant axes |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Choose this OR Question B]

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step procedure of Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the following centered 2D dataset, perform PCA and find the first principal component. State the percentage of variance retained. (7 marks)

$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ -2 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Solution to (a) — PCA Procedure (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution hint:** Diagram = 2 marks, Step listing = 3 marks, Justification of each step = 2 marks.

**Block Diagram** (refer to SECTION 4.1).

**Steps:**

1. **Mean centering:** Compute $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_i$ and subtract from each row to obtain $\mathbf{X}_c$.

2. **Standardization (optional but recommended for mixed-scale features):** Divide each column by its standard deviation $\sigma_j$ so that every feature has unit variance.

3. **Covariance matrix computation:** 
$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$$

4. **Eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{C}$:** Solve $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ to obtain $p$ eigenpairs $(\lambda_i, \mathbf{v}_i)$.

5. **Sort eigenpairs** in descending order of eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_p$.

6. **Choose $k$** using one of: (i) Cumulative explained variance $\geq 0.95$, (ii) Kaiser criterion $\lambda_i > 1$, (iii) Scree plot elbow.

7. **Form projection matrix** $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2 \mid \dots \mid \mathbf{v}_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}$.

8. **Transform the data:** $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$.

**Justification:** PCA works because eigenvectors of the covariance matrix point along the directions of maximum variance (proved via the Rayleigh quotient), and the variance along each eigenvector equals its corresponding eigenvalue. Discarding low-variance components minimizes the L2 reconstruction error.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical PCA (7 marks)

**Step 1: Verify centering.** Check: $\sum \mathbf{x} = (0, 0)^{\top}$, so data is already mean-zero. ✓

**Step 2: Compute Covariance Matrix.**

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{3}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 & -1 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & -1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ -2 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Element $(1,1)$: $\frac{1}{3}(4+4+1+1) = \frac{10}{3}$

Element $(1,2)$: $\frac{1}{3}(6-2+1-3) = \frac{2}{3}$

Element $(2,2)$: $\frac{1}{3}(9+1+1+9) = \frac{20}{3}$

$$\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/3 & 2/3 \\ 2/3 & 20/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Characteristic equation.**

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} 10/3 - \lambda & 2/3 \\ 2/3 & 20/3 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$(10/3 - \lambda)(20/3 - \lambda) - (2/3)^{2} = 0$$

$$\lambda^{2} - 10\lambda + \frac{200 - 4}{9} = 0 \implies \lambda^{2} - 10\lambda + \frac{196}{9} = 0$$

Multiplying by 9: $9\lambda^{2} - 90\lambda + 196 = 0$.

Using the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{90 \pm \sqrt{8100 - 7056}}{18} = \frac{90 \pm \sqrt{1044}}{18} = \frac{90 \pm 2\sqrt{261}}{18}$$

$\sqrt{261} \approx 16.155$, so:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{90 + 32.31}{18} \approx 6.795, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{90 - 32.31}{18} \approx 3.205$$

**Step 4: First Eigenvector (for $\lambda_1$).**

$$(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = 0 \implies \begin{bmatrix} 10/3 - 6.795 & 2/3 \\ 2/3 & 20/3 - 6.795 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} -3.462 & 0.667 \\ 0.667 & -0.129 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$$

From row 2: $0.667 v_{11} - 0.129 v_{12} = 0 \implies v_{12} = 5.17 v_{11}$.

Normalizing: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 26.74}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 5.17 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.190 \\ 0.982 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 5: Variance Retention.**

$$\text{Total variance} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 \approx 6.795 + 3.205 = 10$$

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{6.795}{10} = 0.6795 = 67.95\%$$

**Conclusion:** The first principal component is $\mathbf{v}_1 \approx (0.19, 0.98)^{\top}$ and it retains **~67.95%** of the total variance. Since this is below the typical 95% threshold, we would retain both components in practice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** algorithm. Define the within-class and between-class scatter matrices. Why is the maximum number of linear discriminants equal to $C-1$ where $C$ is the number of classes? (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **PCA, LDA, and t-SNE** along the following dimensions: (i) Supervision, (ii) Linearity, (iii) Primary objective, (iv) Maximum reduced dimensions, (v) Computational complexity, (vi) Suitable use case, (vii) Scalability to large datasets. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to (a) — LDA Theory (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** LDA steps = 3 marks, Scatter matrix definitions = 2 marks, $C-1$ proof = 2 marks.

**LDA Algorithm Steps:**

1. Compute the **mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ of the entire dataset and the **class mean** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ for each class $c \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\}$.

2. Compute the **within-class scatter matrix** $\mathbf{S}_W$ and **between-class scatter matrix** $\mathbf{S}_B$ (see definitions below).

3. Compute the matrix $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B$ (assuming $\mathbf{S}_W$ is non-singular, else use pseudo-inverse or regularization).

4. Find the **eigenvectors** and **eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B$.

5. Sort the eigenpairs in descending order; pick the top $k$ eigenvectors (where $k \leq C-1$) to form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$.

6. Transform the data: $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}$.

**Scatter Matrix Definitions:**

- **Within-class scatter matrix** measures the spread of data *within* each class:
$$\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{c=1}^{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in c} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^{\top}$$

- **Between-class scatter matrix** measures the spread of class *means* around the global mean:
$$\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{c=1}^{C} n_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top}$$

**Why $C-1$ is the maximum?**

The rank of $\mathbf{S}_B$ is at most $C-1$ because:
- Each $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})$ lies in the affine subspace spanned by $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_C\}$.
- The vectors $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})$ satisfy the linear dependency: $\sum_{c=1}^{C} n_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = n\boldsymbol{\mu} - n\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{0}$.
- Thus, only $C-1$ of these vectors are linearly independent.
- Consequently, $\text{rank}(\mathbf{S}_B) \leq C-1$, and only $C-1$ non-zero eigenvalues exist for $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}\mathbf{S}_B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LDA singularity check:** When $p > n$ (e.g., in face recognition with 10000 pixels but 100 images), $\mathbf{S}_W$ becomes singular. Solutions: (i) PCA-LDA pipeline (regularize via PCA first), (ii) Use $\mathbf{S}_W + \epsilon \mathbf{I}$ (regularized LDA).

---

#### Solution to (b) — Comparative Table (7 marks)

**Mark distribution:** Each row worth ~1 mark. Students must write 2–3 lines of *justification* per cell, not just a one-word answer.

| Dimension | PCA | LDA | t-SNE |
|-----------|-----|-----|-------|
| **Supervision** | Unsupervised — uses only $\mathbf{X}$ | Supervised — requires class labels $y$ | Unsupervised — uses only $\mathbf{X}$ |
| **Linearity** | Linear projection | Linear projection | Non-linear (uses Student-t kernel) |
| **Primary Objective** | Maximize total variance $\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v}$ | Maximize Fisher ratio $\frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_B\mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_W\mathbf{w}}$ | Minimize KL divergence between $P$ and $Q$ |
| **Max Reduced Dim** | $p$ (number of features) | $C-1$ (number of classes minus one) | Typically 2 or 3 (visualization) |
| **Computational Cost** | $\mathcal{O}(p^{3})$ for eigendecomp | $\mathcal{O}(p^{3})$ for matrix inversion + eigendecomp | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ — *not scalable to $n>10^{4}$* |
| **Suitable Use Case** | Compression, denoising, feature engineering | Preprocessing for classifiers (SVM, k-NN) | 2D/3D visualization of clusters |
| **Scalability to Large Data** | Good (use randomized SVD for $n \gg p$) | Good for moderate $C$ | Poor — use Barnes-Hut t-SNE or opt for UMAP |
| **Output Determinism** | Deterministic (closed-form) | Deterministic (closed-form) | Stochastic (random init + gradient descent) |
| **Preserves** | Global variance structure | Class separability | Local neighborhood structure |
| **Distance Preserved** | Euclidean (linear) | Mahalanobis (linear) | Conditional probability (non-linear) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfalls costing 2–3 marks:**
> 1. Writing only a one-word answer (e.g., "Supervised") without justification. Examiners expect at least a clause.
> 2. Confusing *LDA* (Linear Discriminant Analysis, a dimensionality reduction technique) with *Latent Dirichlet Allocation* (an NLP topic model). These are **completely different**.
> 3. Claiming t-SNE is deterministic — it is not. Running t-SNE twice yields different embeddings.
> 4. Forgetting that t-SNE cannot be used for new (test) data projection without retraining — there is no closed-form transform.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Dimensionality Reduction**

### Core Concepts
- Dimensionality reduction maps $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ with $k \ll p$.
- Two paradigms: **Feature Selection** (subset) vs **Feature Extraction** (transform).
- The **Curse of Dimensionality** motivates the need; data sparsity and distance concentration are key symptoms.

### PCA — Must-Know
- $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$ — symmetric positive semi-definite.
- Eigendecomposition: $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$; eigenvalues are variances along eigenvectors.
- Always **center** the data; **standardize** when features have different units.
- $k$ selection: $\text{CEV}_k \geq 0.95$, Kaiser rule ($\lambda_i > 1$), or scree elbow.
- Reconstruction error equals sum of discarded eigenvalues.
- PCA is **sensitive to outliers** and assumes **linearity**.

### LDA — Must-Know
- Supervised; uses class labels.
- Within-class $\mathbf{S}_W$ and between-class $\mathbf{S}_B$ scatter matrices.
- Fisher criterion: maximize $\frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_B\mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{S}_W\mathbf{w}}$.
- Maximum reduced dimension = $C-1$.
- Assumes multivariate Gaussian and equal class covariances.
- Cannot handle $p > n$ directly — use PCA + LDA or regularization.

### t-SNE — Must-Know
- Non-linear, stochastic, primarily for **visualization**.
- High-D: Gaussian kernel; Low-D: Student-t (1 d.o.f.) kernel — heavy tails solve crowding.
- Loss: KL divergence between joint probability distributions.
- Hyperparameter: **perplexity** (typical 5–50, default 30).
- **Disadvantages:** $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ complexity; no closed-form projection for new data; non-deterministic; global structure not preserved.

### KTU Examiner's Quick-Fire Facts
- SVD relation: $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ — singular values $\sigma_i = \sqrt{(n-1)\lambda_i}$.
- Autoencoders are the **neural network generalization** of PCA (linear activation = PCA).
- UMAP is the modern alternative to t-SNE — faster, preserves more global structure.
- Feature selection methods: **Filter** (Chi-square, ANOVA), **Wrapper** (RFE, forward/backward), **Embedded** (Lasso L1, tree importance).

### Common Numerical Pitfalls
- Forgetting to divide by $(n-1)$ in the covariance — use $n$ if defined that way in your textbook, but KTU prefers $(n-1)$.
- Eigenvectors not normalized to unit length before projection.
- Mixing up the number of principal components $k$ with the original dimension $p$.
- Not verifying $\sum \text{EVR}_i = 1$ as a sanity check.

### Mark-Winning Tip
> [!NOTE]
> In 14-mark PCA questions, KTU examiners allocate approximately: **2 marks for the diagram, 3 marks for step listing, 7 marks for the numerical computation, 2 marks for conclusion/variance retention**. Missing the variance retention percentage alone costs 1–2 marks. Always end with a **conclusion sentence** stating the chosen $k$ and its CEV.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GNEST305 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a linear, unsupervised **dimensionality reduction** technique that transforms a set of correlated high-dimensional variables into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called **Principal Components (PCs)**, while preserving as much of the original **variance** (information content) as possible.

The transformation is defined by an orthogonal linear projection of the data onto a new coordinate system, where the greatest variance lies along the first axis (first principal component), the second greatest variance along the second axis, and so on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *PCA is a statistical procedure that uses an orthogonal transformation to convert a set of observations of possibly correlated variables into a set of values of linearly uncorrelated variables called principal components.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Reducing the number of input features/variables while retaining essential information. |
| **Variance** | Measure of spread of data points around the mean. PCA preserves maximum variance. |
| **Covariance** | Measure of how two variables change together (joint variability). |
| **Eigenvector** | A non-zero vector whose direction remains unchanged when a linear transformation is applied. |
| **Eigenvalue** | The scalar factor by which an eigenvector is stretched during the transformation. |
| **Principal Component (PC)** | A new axis (linear combination of original features) capturing maximum variance. |
| **Mean Centering** | Subtracting the mean from each feature so that data is centered at the origin. |
| **Loading Vector** | The unit eigenvector (weights) that defines the direction of a principal component. |
| **Score Vector** | The projection of original data onto the principal component axis. |
| **Scree Plot** | A line plot of eigenvalues ordered from largest to smallest, used to pick the number of PCs. |

### 1.3 The "Shadow on the Wall" Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a room full of people (your data points), all jostling in 3D space. Now imagine shining a bright flashlight onto a flat wall opposite the room. The shadows cast on the wall are 2D representations of the 3D people.

> **Analogy:** PCA is exactly like projecting 3D data onto a 2D wall. The trick is to **tilt the wall** so that the shadows capture the *most spread* (variance) of the people. The direction in which the people are most spread out becomes the **first principal component**. The second most spread-out perpendicular direction becomes the **second principal component**. The wall itself (2D) is your new, compressed view of a complex 3D world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is PCA useful in AI and Data Science?**
> 1. **Curse of Dimensionality** mitigation: High-dimensional data is sparse and computationally expensive. PCA reduces features, speeding up ML models.
> 2. **Visualization**: Humans can only visualize up to 3D. PCA allows 2D/3D plots of high-dimensional data.
> 3. **Noise Reduction**: Discarding low-variance components often removes noise.
> 4. **Multicollinearity Removal**: PCs are orthogonal (uncorrelated), solving correlated feature problems.
> 5. **Data Compression**: Storage and transmission become efficient.

### 1.4 Core Assumptions of PCA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board frequently tests these assumptions:**
> 1. **Linearity:** PCA assumes relationships between variables are linear.
> 2. **Mean and Variance are Sufficient Statistics:** PCA uses only first and second-order statistics.
> 3. **High Variance = High Information:** Variables with larger variance carry more information.
> 4. **Orthogonality of Components:** Principal components are perpendicular to each other.
> 5. **Large Sample Size:** PCA is sensitive to outliers and small sample noise.
> 6. **Mean Centering Required:** Data should be centered (mean = 0) before applying PCA; standardizing (scaling) is recommended when features have different units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Geometry of PCA — Step-by-Step Logic

The mathematical machinery of PCA rests on **eigen-decomposition of the covariance matrix**. To truly understand *why* this works, follow this logic chain:

1. **Goal:** Find a new axis (direction) such that when we project the data onto it, the **projected variance is maximized**.
2. **For a given unit vector $\mathbf{w}$**, the variance of the projected data $\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}$ is given by $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$, where $\mathbf{C}$ is the covariance matrix of the centered data $\mathbf{X}$.
3. **Optimization Problem:** Maximize $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$ subject to the constraint $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$ (unit vector).
4. **Use Lagrange Multipliers:** Form the Lagrangian $L(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1)$.
5. **Take derivative w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero:** $2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.
6. **Recognition:** This is the **eigenvalue equation**! The directions of maximum variance are precisely the **eigenvectors** of the covariance matrix, and the magnitude of variance in each direction is the corresponding **eigenvalue**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Intuition of the Eigendecomposition:**
> The covariance matrix $\mathbf{C}$ is a symmetric positive semi-definite matrix. Symmetry guarantees real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. Positive semi-definiteness guarantees non-negative eigenvalues. The largest eigenvalue points in the direction of maximum spread.

### 2.2 The PCA Algorithm (Step-by-Step Pipeline)

| Step | Operation | Formula/Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Step 1** | **Input Data** | Acquire the dataset $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with $n$ samples and $d$ features. |
| **Step 2** | **Mean Centering** | Subtract column means: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \bar{\mathbf{X}}$ (each feature now has mean 0). |
| **Step 3** | **Standardization (Optional)** | Divide by standard deviation: $\mathbf{X}_s = \mathbf{X}_c / \sigma$ (recommended when features have different units/scales). |
| **Step 4** | **Compute Covariance Matrix** | $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$ (a $d \times d$ symmetric matrix). |
| **Step 5** | **Eigendecomposition** | Solve $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$ to get $d$ eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_d$ and eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_d$. |
| **Step 6** | **Sort and Select** | Sort eigenpairs by descending eigenvalue. Pick top $k$ components (where $k < d$) using scree plot or cumulative variance threshold. |
| **Step 7** | **Form Projection Matrix** | $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$. |
| **Step 8** | **Project to Lower Dimension** | $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ (the new reduced representation). |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every formula below is examinable. Memorize the meaning, units, and the use of each.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mean Centering | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Shifts data origin to the centroid. |
| 2 | Standardization (Z-score) | $z_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$ | Places each feature on the same scale. |
| 3 | Covariance Matrix | $\mathbf{C} = \dfrac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$ | $d \times d$ symmetric matrix of feature covariances. |
| 4 | Covariance of Two Features | $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \dfrac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | Joint variability; $>0$ = positively correlated. |
| 5 | Eigenvalue Equation | $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Defines each principal component direction. |
| 6 | Projected Variance | $\text{Var}(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} = \lambda$ | Variance along direction $\mathbf{w}$ equals its eigenvalue. |
| 7 | Loading Vector (PC direction) | $\mathbf{v}_i = [v_{i1}, v_{i2}, \ldots, v_{id}]^{\top}$ | Unit eigenvector; weights of original features. |
| 8 | Score (Projection of a sample) | $z_i = \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_i$ | New coordinate of sample along $i$-th PC. |
| 9 | Reduced Data Matrix | $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$ | $n \times k$ transformed dataset. |
| 10 | Variance Explained Ratio | $\eta_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Fraction of total variance carried by $i$-th PC. |
| 11 | Cumulative Variance Explained | $\eta_{\text{cum}}(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Information retained by keeping first $k$ components. |
| 12 | Total Variance Identity | $\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(\mathbf{C}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \sigma_j^2$ | Total variance is invariant under rotation. |
| 13 | Reconstruction (Approximate) | $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{W}^{\top} + \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Maps reduced data back to original $d$ dimensions. |
| 14 | Reconstruction Error (SSE) | $E_k = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i$ | Sum of discarded eigenvalues = information lost. |
| 15 | Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) link | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$ | PCs are columns of $\mathbf{V}$; eigenvalues $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^2 / (n-1)$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of PCA:**
> * **Image Processing:** Face recognition (Eigenfaces), image compression.
> * **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression analysis (thousands of genes per sample).
> * **Finance:** Portfolio risk reduction, feature extraction from price data.
> * **Signal Processing:** Separating signal from noise.
> * **NLP & Recommender Systems:** Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA).

### 2.4 How to Choose $k$ — The Three KTU-Standard Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a 7-mark KTU-favorite question!**

**Method 1: Cumulative Variance Threshold (Elbow / 95% Rule)**
Choose the smallest $k$ such that $\eta_{\text{cum}}(k) \geq 0.95$ (or 0.90 in stricter engineering applications).

**Method 2: Scree Plot (Cattell's Method)**
Plot $\lambda_i$ vs. $i$. Find the **"elbow"** — the point where the curve flattens. Pick $k$ at the elbow; components beyond the elbow are usually noise.

**Method 3: Kaiser Criterion (for correlation matrix)**
Keep all components with $\lambda_i \geq 1$ (only meaningful when PCA is run on the standardized correlation matrix).

**Method 4: Proportion of Total Variance**
Choose $k$ such that $\eta_{\text{cum}}(k) \geq 0.80$ (for visualization) or $0.99$ (for near-perfect reconstruction).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the *exact* style of KTU 14-mark question. Master this template.**

**Problem Statement:**
Given the following 2D dataset (5 samples, 2 features), perform Principal Component Analysis (PCA) from scratch. Compute the principal components, the explained variance ratio, and project the data onto the first principal component.

| Sample ($i$) | $X_1$ | $X_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2.5 | 2.4 |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0.7 |
| 3 | 2.2 | 2.9 |
| 4 | 1.9 | 2.2 |
| 5 | 3.1 | 3.0 |

*(This is the classic Fisher Iris-derived toy dataset, modified to be 2-D for KTU 2024 paper style.)*

---

#### Step 1 — Compute the Mean Vector

The mean of each feature is:

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{2.5 + 0.5 + 2.2 + 1.9 + 3.1}{5} = \frac{10.2}{5} = 2.04
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = \frac{2.4 + 0.7 + 2.9 + 2.2 + 3.0}{5} = \frac{11.2}{5} = 2.24
$$

So, the mean vector is $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = [2.04, \; 2.24]^{\top}$.

#### Step 2 — Mean-Center the Data

Subtract the mean from each row:

| Sample | $X_1 - \bar{x}_1$ | $X_2 - \bar{x}_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.46 | 0.16 |
| 2 | -1.54 | -1.54 |
| 3 | 0.16 | 0.66 |
| 4 | -0.14 | -0.04 |
| 5 | 1.06 | 0.76 |

The centered data matrix $\mathbf{X}_c$ is:

$$
\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0.46 & 0.16 \\ -1.54 & -1.54 \\ 0.16 & 0.66 \\ -0.14 & -0.04 \\ 1.06 & 0.76 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Covariance Matrix

Using the unbiased estimator $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$, where $n = 5$:

First, compute $\mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$:

$$
\mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0.46^2 + 1.54^2 + 0.16^2 + 0.14^2 + 1.06^2 & (0.46)(-1.54) + \cdots \\ \cdots & 0.16^2 + 1.54^2 + 0.66^2 + 0.04^2 + 0.76^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each entry numerically:

$$
(0.46)^2 + (1.54)^2 + (0.16)^2 + (0.14)^2 + (1.06)^2 = 0.2116 + 2.3716 + 0.0256 + 0.0196 + 1.1236 = 3.7520
$$

$$
(0.16)^2 + (1.54)^2 + (0.66)^2 + (0.04)^2 + (0.76)^2 = 0.0256 + 2.3716 + 0.4356 + 0.0016 + 0.5776 = 3.4120
$$

$$
(0.46)(-1.54) + (-1.54)(-1.54) + (0.16)(0.66) + (-0.14)(-0.04) + (1.06)(0.76)
$$

$$
= -0.7084 + 2.3716 + 0.1056 + 0.0056 + 0.8056 = 2.5800
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 3.7520 & 2.5800 \\ 2.5800 & 3.4120 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Dividing by $n - 1 = 4$:

$$
\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 3.7520 & 2.5800 \\ 2.5800 & 3.4120 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9380 & 0.6450 \\ 0.6450 & 0.8530 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 4 — Solve the Eigenvalue Equation

Solve $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 0.9380 - \lambda & 0.6450 \\ 0.6450 & 0.8530 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

$$
(0.9380 - \lambda)(0.8530 - \lambda) - (0.6450)^2 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - (0.9380 + 0.8530)\lambda + (0.9380 \times 0.8530 - 0.4160) = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 1.7910\,\lambda + (0.8001 - 0.4160) = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 1.7910\,\lambda + 0.3841 = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.7910 \pm \sqrt{1.7910^2 - 4 \times 0.3841}}{2} = \frac{1.7910 \pm \sqrt{3.2077 - 1.5364}}{2} = \frac{1.7910 \pm \sqrt{1.6713}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.7910 \pm 1.2928}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{1.7910 + 1.2928}{2} = \frac{3.0838}{2} = 1.5419, \qquad \lambda_2 = \frac{1.7910 - 1.2928}{2} = \frac{0.4982}{2} = 0.2491
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute the Eigenvectors

**Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 1.5419$:**

Solve $(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I}) \mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0.9380 - 1.5419 & 0.6450 \\ 0.6450 & 0.8530 - 1.5419 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
-0.6039 \, v_{11} + 0.6450 \, v_{12} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{12} = 0.9362 \, v_{11}
$$

Normalize: $v_{11}^2 + v_{12}^2 = 1 \Rightarrow v_{11}^2 (1 + 0.8765) = 1 \Rightarrow v_{11} = 0.7300$.

So $\mathbf{v}_1 = [0.7300, \; 0.6835]^{\top}$.

**Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 0.2491$:**

$$
0.9380 - 0.2491 = 0.6889 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.6889 \, v_{21} + 0.6450 \, v_{22} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{22} = -1.0681 \, v_{21}
$$

Normalize: $v_{21}^2 (1 + 1.1408) = 1 \Rightarrow v_{21} = 0.6834$.

So $\mathbf{v}_2 = [0.6834, \; -0.7300]^{\top}$.

> **Verification of orthogonality:** $\mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2 = (0.7300)(0.6834) + (0.6835)(-0.7300) = 0.4989 - 0.4990 \approx 0$. ✓

#### Step 6 — Explained Variance Ratio

Total variance: $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 1.5419 + 0.2491 = 1.7910$.

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{1.5419}{1.7910} \approx 0.8609 \quad (86.09\%)
$$

$$
\eta_2 = \frac{0.2491}{1.7910} \approx 0.1391 \quad (13.91\%)
$$

#### Step 7 — Project Data onto First Principal Component

The projection of each centered sample onto $\mathbf{v}_1$ is $z_i = \mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_1$:

$$
z_1 = (0.46)(0.7300) + (0.16)(0.6835) = 0.3358 + 0.1094 = 0.4452
$$

$$
z_2 = (-1.54)(0.7300) + (-1.54)(0.6835) = -1.1242 - 1.0526 = -2.1768
$$

$$
z_3 = (0.16)(0.7300) + (0.66)(0.6835) = 0.1168 + 0.4511 = 0.5679
$$

$$
z_4 = (-0.14)(0.7300) + (-0.04)(0.6835) = -0.1022 - 0.0273 = -0.1295
$$

$$
z_5 = (1.06)(0.7300) + (0.76)(0.6835) = 0.7738 + 0.5195 = 1.2933
$$

**Final reduced dataset (1-D, 5 samples):**

$$
\mathbf{Z} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4452 \\ -2.1768 \\ 0.5679 \\ -0.1295 \\ 1.2933 \end{bmatrix}
$$

We have reduced 2D data to 1D, retaining **86.09%** of the variance.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mean vector $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ | $[2.04, \; 2.24]^{\top}$ | Centroid of original data. |
| Covariance matrix $\mathbf{C}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0.9380 & 0.6450 \\ 0.6450 & 0.8530 \end{bmatrix}$ | Symmetric, positive-definite. |
| Eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ | 1.5419 | Variance along PC1. |
| Eigenvalue $\lambda_2$ | 0.2491 | Variance along PC2. |
| Eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_1$ (PC1) | $[0.7300, \; 0.6835]^{\top}$ | Direction of maximum variance. |
| Eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_2$ (PC2) | $[0.6834, \; -0.7300]^{\top}$ | Orthogonal to PC1, captures residual. |
| Explained variance ratio (PC1) | **86.09%** | Most information retained. |
| Explained variance ratio (PC2) | **13.91%** | Drop this to compress. |
| Projection vector $\mathbf{Z}$ | $[0.4452, \; -2.1768, \; 0.5679, \; -0.1295, \; 1.2933]^{\top}$ | New 1-D representation. |
| Reconstruction error | $\lambda_2 = 0.2491$ | Information lost by dropping PC2. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconstruction Check (KTU tip):**
> $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + z_i \mathbf{v}_1$ should approximate $\mathbf{x}_i$. For sample 1: $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_1 = [2.04 + 0.4452 \times 0.7300, \; 2.24 + 0.4452 \times 0.6835]^{\top} = [2.365, \; 2.544]^{\top}$ — close to the original $[2.5, 2.4]^{\top}$. ✓

```mermaid

    H --> I[Step 8: Project Data Z = Xc times W]

    J --> K[Step 9: Reconstruction if needed]
    K --> L[Approximate Original X hat]

    subgraph CHOOSING_K [Choosing k methods]

    end
```

### 4.3 Scree Plot Decision Logic (Choosing $k$)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute All Eigenvalues] --> B[Sort Descending Lambda1 to Lambdad]
    B --> C[Plot Scree Curve]
    C --> D{Elbow Point Detected?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Choose k at the elbow]
    D -- No --> F[Check Cumulative Variance]
    F --> G{Cum Variance greater than 95 percent?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Choose smallest such k]
    G -- No --> I[Increase k by 1 and recheck]
    I --> F
    E --> J[Final k selected]
    H --> J
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    M6 --> M7[Variance Retained equals sum lambdai over sum lambdaj]
```
